Sean Gallagher: Make sure “class” label is strictly {short,long} (and maybe “unknown”) and not a freeform tier.

Hiro Tanaka: Also please gate “prefill/decode separation hooks” behind the same flag. We don’t want the refactor to change behavior when policy is off.

Sofia Ivanova: Yes. Plan is: with flag off, code path should behave exactly like today’s queue selection. We’ll add regression tests around batch composition and ordering.

Noah Patel: Another concern: this looks like SRPT-lite. In multi-tenant systems SRPT can be unfair under certain distributions. ADR should include explicit statement: “We’re not implementing SRPT; we’re implementing two-class scheduling with bounded starvation.” And list failure modes.

Lena Fischer: Will update ADR with a section:
- Not SRPT (no continuous reordering by remaining tokens)
- Risks: long degradation, kernel thrash, cache eviction, oscillation
- Mitigations: token budgets, min-run, aging, caps

Connor O'Brien: Release perspective: if behavior changes even with flag off, we can’t land it right before runtime cut. Please keep “no-op when disabled” super tight.

Allison Grant: Re dashboards: we should require a pre-defined dashboard before canary. Otherwise SRE is blind. Can you add “dashboard exists” as a rollout prerequisite in ADR?

Sean Gallagher: Yes please. Also a rollback trigger: e.g. if overall p95 or long-class p95 increases > N% for > M minutes, auto page + flip flag.

Mateo Alvarez: Exec sponsor view: I’m okay taking some risk in canary as long as the blast radius is controlled. But I want the ADR to be crisp about what’s gated and what the “kill switch” is. And we should call out that “multi-tenant QoS is out of scope” explicitly so we don’t accidentally promise it.

Noah Patel: Summarizing required ADR edits from this thread:
- Define fairness objective + explicit invariants (starvation bound, dispatch floor/caps)
- Clarify aging scope (per-request, global queue)
- Explicit gating: flag default OFF, allowlist pools, per-model/pool params, bounded config
- “No-op when disabled” requirement (prefill/decode refactor included)
- Observability requirements: per-class metrics with bounded cardinality + dashboard prerequisite
- Rollback triggers + kill switch
- Explicitly not SRPT + enumerate failure modes

Lena Fischer: Ack. I’ll push an ADR revision today and tag reviewers. :thumbsup:

Sean Gallagher: Add me on the SRE sign-off for canary gating. Once metrics + dashboard spec are in, we can pre-approve the canary plan.

Allison Grant: Please loop me on metric names before you merge. I’ll sanity check against our conventions.

Hiro Tanaka: And loop kernels once you decide min-run/token budget defaults; we need to validate no kernel oscillation on A100/H100.

Noah Patel: Cool. Marking “changes requested” on ADR until the above is addressed.

Architecture Bot: Review status updated: changes requested by Noah Patel.

source.thread:
Jada Williams: Need quick architecture review input. For auto-rollback on SLO breach: should SLO breach evaluation run *inside* rollout-controller (as part of the rollout state machine) or as a separate “SLO evaluator” service the controller calls? Looking for opinions on failure modes + consistency guarantees.

Ethan Park: My bias: keep the decision logic close to where the action happens. If evaluator is remote, you now have controller <-> evaluator RPC failure modes + retries. Also need to define who owns “truth” if they disagree.

Jordan Lee: +1 that we should be explicit about consistency. If evaluation is external and async, you can get races like: evaluator says breach, controller already advanced step and shifted traffic. Then rollback executor fights current desired state.

Chloe Martin: From Console/backend POV, a separate service can give us cleaner audit trails + a single place to query “why did we decide X”. But we can still get that if controller emits structured audit events w/ snapshots.

Benji Okafor: Policy engine angle: if we embed evaluation in controller, we need to be careful about config consistency. Controller should evaluate using a pinned policy version (or at least include policy hash/version in the decision) so the decision is reproducible.

Allison Grant: Observability/cardinality: whichever side runs evaluation must emit metrics w/ stable labels. External service makes it easier to centralize instrumentation, but also creates another service w/ its own SLO + alerts.

Sean Gallagher: SRE failure modes list:
- monitor provider partial outage / query timeouts
- stale data (burn-rate endpoint returning old timestamps)
- low volume rollouts (canary step too small -> noisy)
- telemetry lag (p95 looks fine until buffers flush)
- controller restart mid-step

If evaluation is inside controller, we need “fail-open vs fail-closed” semantics. For MVP I’d rather fail-open (pause only if confident) and page/notify on missing/stale monitors.

Priya Natarajan: What do we need for first release? If rollback is allowed to happen automatically, I’m uncomfortable with an extra network hop being required to *avoid* paging. Simpler path seems safer: evaluation in controller process, using a library client to slo-toolkit.

Jada Williams: The concern I had w/ controller-local evaluation is tying up the state machine loop on slow monitor queries. We can do async though.

Ethan Park: Yeah, don’t block the main transition. Pattern:
1) Step enters “observing”
2) controller schedules periodic eval (e.g., every 30s)
3) eval result written to rollout status / audit event stream
4) only state transitions happen under lock

Jordan Lee: Also avoids split-brain. The controller owns the desired state; evaluation just produces a recommendation. Decision boundary still in controller.

Benji Okafor: +1 on “recommendation” object. Something like:
- scope (route/model/region/tier)
- windows evaluated (fast/slow)
- sample sizes
- status (OK/BREACH/INSUFFICIENT_DATA/STALE/MONITOR_MISSING)
- policy_version
- monitor_ids

Then controller applies policy: pause vs rollback vs suppress.

Allison Grant: If we do that, make sure we emit:
- `rollout_slo_eval_result{result=..., reason=...}`
- `rollout_auto_action_total{action=rollback|pause|suppress, reason=...}`
And please cap free-form “reason” values (enum only) so we don’t explode dashboards.

Sean Gallagher: On fail-open/fail-closed: for rollback specifically I want:
- Never rollback on a single window
- require dual-window confirmation (fast burn + slow burn) OR a stronger fast burn threshold
- require min req count AND min error count for error-rate SLO
- staleness => do NOT rollback, but emit “monitor_stale” + notify.

Chloe Martin: Dedicated evaluator service could implement the above centrally, but agree it’s heavy for MVP. We can still model it as a component w/ clear interface, so later we can move it out.

Jada Williams: What about “consistency guarantees” write-up? I want something we can put in the design doc.

Jordan Lee: Draft bullets:
- Controller is single writer for rollout state.
- Evaluation runs as sidecar task but decisions are applied via the same state transition mechanism (CAS/lock).
- Decisions are idempotent: same input snapshot => same decision; controller stores `decision_id` + `policy_version`.
- Rollback executor is idempotent and rate-limited (so repeated triggers don’t thrash traffic split).
- If monitor data missing/stale: emit audit event + metrics; default action = no rollback (optionally pause if configured).

Ethan Park: Also “time” consistency: use the monitor provider’s reported `data_end_ts` and refuse to make rollback decisions if data is older than X (say 2m) to avoid acting on delayed metrics.

Benji Okafor: Config consistency: controller fetches policy at step start and pins it for that step (or entire rollout). If policy changes mid-step, you don’t get non-deterministic behavior.

Allison Grant: Please include in audit event a pointer to the exact monitor query params (scope labels) and the window values. Doesn’t need raw time series, just the evaluated burn-rate and sample size numbers.

Sean Gallagher: One more failure mode: “monitor says breach but it’s global incident.” Need suppression hook (“do not rollback during known incident”). If that’s in policy engine or incident tooling, controller needs a simple boolean “suppressed_by_incident=true” signal.

Priya Natarajan: Decision: MVP = evaluation inside rollout-controller, implemented as an SLO evaluator module calling slo-toolkit. Keep interface such that we can later split into a dedicated service if needed. Required in design doc:
- async eval loop
- pinned policy version
- staleness + min-sample gating
- fail-open default for missing monitors (no rollback)

Jada Williams: Works for me. I’ll update the design spec w/ this choice + list tradeoffs. Action items:
- @Jordan Lee: write the “consistency guarantees” section
- @Sean Gallagher: confirm fail-open semantics + recommended staleness threshold
- @Allison Grant: send metric label enums we should use

Sean Gallagher: Ack. For staleness, I’d start with `>120s` stale = don’t rollback; `>300s` stale = alert/notify harder. We can tune after staging.

Allison Grant: I’ll drop a short list of enums + cardinality guidance in thread later today.

Jordan Lee: Will do. I’ll also add note about avoiding re-eval storms (debounce + holdoff between actions).

Ethan Park: :thumbsup: Keep it boring and deterministic.

source.content:
Noah Patel: Kicking off ADR-017 discussion here. We need to decide: selector v2 is (A) rule-based decision tree keyed on (model family, seq bucket, GPU SKU) or (B) learned scoring model w/ features -> kernel choice. Main concerns I’m hearing: explainability + rollback safety + determinism. Let’s debate and land it.

ConfluenceBot: ADR draft: adr-017-kernel-auto-selection-v2-features-and-fallbacks (link)

Talia Benson: +1 please keep it crisp. Mateo wants a decision today so release train isn’t blocked.

Omar El-Sayed: From tail-latency/operability POV: learned scoring makes me nervous unless we can *fully* explain why a request got kernel X and reproduce it. If we can’t quickly answer “why did p99 spike in us-east on L40S for long-context?”, oncall is toast.

Hiro Tanaka: Learned scoring is tempting for perf (more nuance than buckets), but we’ll pay for it in debugging. Also, our failure modes are usually discrete (kernel unsupported/unstable for SKU+driver, or misbucket long-context prefill). Rules let us force the safe fallback hierarchy.

Yuki Sato: If we do learned scoring, we still need hard constraints: allowlist/denylist per SKU, min driver/CUDA, and a deterministic tie-break. Otherwise we’ll get nondeterministic picks when scores are close.

Grace Kim: Also: what’s the "model" for learned scoring? If it’s trained on benchmarks, it’ll overfit to our lab distributions. Real traffic has mixed workloads. We'd still end up adding hand-tuned guardrails => complexity.

Jordan Lee: Control plane / policy engine wants deterministic, auditable decisions. If selector output depends on a learned model artifact, we need versioning, rollout, and the ability to replay decisions from logs. That’s doable, but it becomes a product in itself.

Allison Grant: Observability angle: whatever we do, we need stable telemetry fields. Learned scoring usually means feature explosion + cardinality risk (everyone wants to log “top 10 candidates with scores”). That’ll blow up metrics unless we’re careful.

Noah Patel: Re determinism: requirement is “same inputs => same output” across processes/hosts. A learned model *can* be deterministic if we fix weights/version and avoid floating nondeterminism, but it’s more surface area. Rule-based is simpler to reason about + can be unit tested with golden cases.

Omar El-Sayed: And rollback semantics: rule-based => flip gate off (v2->v1) or pin kernel in config, done. Learned scoring => also need to ensure the scoring model isn't partially rolled out. I can already see us in a state where fleet has mixed model versions.

Connor O'Brien: From releases: we can manage artifacts, but runtime 1.21 timeline is tight. If learned scoring adds another deployable/versioned thing, it will slip.

Hiro Tanaka: A practical point: kernel availability is discrete by GPU SKU. We already maintain a registry of “supported kernels per SKU + known-bad combos”. That registry itself is essentially a rule table. Learned scoring can only choose among candidates, so we still do rule gating first.

Yuki Sato: So maybe the compromise is: rule-based candidate generation + fallback precedence, with an optional scoring function later.

Noah Patel: That’s basically what ADR draft proposes: (1) hard filters (denylist/compat), (2) map (model family, seq bucket, sku) -> preferred kernel, (3) fallback chain.

Jordan Lee: If we want “scoring” later, make it an implementation detail behind a stable interface: `Selector::rank(candidates, features)` but default is simple deterministic ranking. Keep ADR scoped to rule-based for v2 GA.

Grace Kim: I’d also like to not ship any probabilistic behavior. If scores are close, you’ll get flapping when minor feature values change. Rules avoid flapping.

Allison Grant: +1 on flapping. Also please add an explicit field to telemetry like `selection_reason` with enumerated values (e.g., `PREFERRED_MATCH`, `FALLBACK_UNAVAILABLE`, `FALLBACK_DENYLISTED`, `FORCED_PIN`). That helps dashboards.

Omar El-Sayed: Can we commit that selection traces include: model family tag, seq bucket, gpu sku normalized, chosen kernel id, and fallback depth? (depth=0 means preferred). That’s how we’ll debug incidents.

Noah Patel: Yes. Also replay tests: feed same request metadata twice and assert same pick. If rule-based, easy.

Talia Benson: What’s the actual decision? I’ll write it into ADR + epic update.

Noah Patel: Proposal: choose rule-based decision logic for v2 GA. Explicitly *not* doing learned scoring in v2. We’ll leave an extension point for future learned ranker, but behind the same determinism + audit requirements.

Hiro Tanaka: +1.

Omar El-Sayed: +1. With condition: hard rollback path documented + hot disable by (model, sku, region).

Ethan Park: Jumping in (control plane). Rule-based is much easier for feature gating + allow/deny lists. Learned scoring would require us to gate by “model artifact version” too. I strongly prefer rules for v2.

Jordan Lee: +1. I’ll add language about “policy must be explainable and replayable” to align w/ deterministic policy engine requirements.

Allison Grant: +1. Please keep telemetry bounded. Suggest sampling for candidate set; always-on for chosen kernel + reason.

Grace Kim: +1.

Yuki Sato: +1. I’ll make sure selector returns a structured explanation that we can serialize.

Noah Patel: Great. Action items:
- Noah: update ADR-017 with decision + rationale + explicit non-goals (no learned scoring in v2).
- Yuki: implement `selection_reason` enums + fallback depth in selector output.
- Ethan/Jordan: confirm gating semantics (env/region/model + denylist overrides) and hot reload expectations.
- Omar/Allison: define minimal required telemetry + sampling guidelines.

Connor O'Brien: Works for release. Please ensure ADR includes rollback safety section (what oncall does in 5 mins) and references the runbook.

Talia Benson: Noted. I’ll post summary in ENG-4821 after ADR update.

Noah Patel: (small note) ADR should call out why learned scoring is deferred: explainability, deterministic replay, operational rollback, artifact/version management. Not “never”, just “not for v2 timeline/risk budget”.

Omar El-Sayed: :thumbsup: Also add “no per-request dynamic adaptation” in v2 (don’t want selector to change decisions based on local rolling stats until we’ve proven it won’t create oscillations).

Yuki Sato: good call. Scheduler hints can be an input later but still deterministic per snapshot.

Noah Patel: Cool. I’ll push ADR edits shortly and tag everyone for final review.

Allison Grant: ADR review time 🙏 — baselines/thresholds + rollout gates for perf-canary. Please leave comments async here or inline on the doc by tomorrow.
Link: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ARCH/ADR-0142-Perf-canary-baselines-thresholds-rollout-gates

Allison Grant: Key questions I want eyes on:
1) fixed vs rolling baselines (and drift handling)
2) region/SKU dimension in baseline key
3) min sample size + dampening (anti-flap)
4) what exactly gates a rollout (warn vs page, per-model vs aggregate)

Logan Wright: Skimmed. +1 on being strict about baseline keys. If we go (model, runtime_version, hardware_sku, region) we need to be very deliberate on what’s a label vs what lives in baseline store.

Logan Wright: From telemetry perspective: please do *not* add `gpu_uuid` / node / pool_id to metrics labels. If the ADR implies we “slice by fleet”, we should do that via logs/traces or a separate low-cardinality signal (e.g. `fleet_generation=v2`).

Nadia Rahman: On rolling baselines: I’m nervous about pure rolling window because it will “learn” regressions during slow rollouts (esp if the canary is running continuously). ADR mentions “rolling with change-point detection” but that’s a lot for MVP.

Nadia Rahman: Suggestion: baseline is fixed per (model_id, model_version, runtime_build_id, kernel_set_id?, sku, region) and updated only by an explicit baseline recompute job after a known-good bake. Drift handled by (a) separate “recalibration” workflow and (b) broader thresholds for noisy models.

Noah Patel: Agree w/ Nadia. The baseline store will already have versioning; making it rolling introduces a second “moving target” and will blow up debugging.

Noah Patel: One nuance: we can’t key on *every* runtime_build_id if we ship nightly. That’s baseline explosion. ADR currently says baseline pinned to “runtime minor” + kernel tag. That seems OK if kernel changes are what cause perf swings.

Hiro Tanaka: +1 baseline explosion risk. Kernel set id is the one that matters for perf; runtime build id helps attribution but not baseline key IMO.

Hiro Tanaka: If kernel auto-selection changes, we need that to show up as a tag in the canary metrics + alert payload (“selected_kernel=flash_v3”), but baseline should be for “expected kernel set for this model on this sku”. Otherwise you’ll never compare apples-to-apples.

Sofia Ivanova: On the scheduler side: I strongly prefer baselines to include `scheduler_config_hash` (or at least a “scheduler profile” name). Batch heuristics tweaks can shift p95 without kernel changes.

Sofia Ivanova: But again, key explosion. Alternative: baseline keyed to (model, sku, region) and we emit tags for (scheduler_profile, runtime_build, kernel_id) and the alert payload prints those so we can correlate.

Allison Grant: Good callouts. Baseline key explosion is my #1 worry. What’s the minimal key we can commit to without making every alert “maybe it’s just eu-west”?

Aisha Bello: For paging hygiene: region needs to be in the key (or at least in the baseline lookup) if we plan to page on top-model regressions. We’ve seen eu-west variance vs us-east from fleet + networking. If we baseline on global and eu-west is systematically +8% slower, we’ll page constantly.

Aisha Bello: For warning-only, I’m ok with global baseline and region as a dimension in the dashboard. But the ADR is proposing gating releases based on these signals, so it needs to be stable.

Sean Gallagher: +1 to region baselines for paging/gates.

Sean Gallagher: I’d also like clarity in ADR: what does “gate” mean operationally? Is it “auto-pause rollout” or “block promote unless someone acknowledges”? SRE can support either but auto-rollback is a higher bar.

Connor O'Brien: From release eng: please don’t make gating depend on p99 right now. We’ll freeze deployments forever. p95 + tokens/sec is enough.

Connor O'Brien: Also: gating needs a very explicit time window and pass/fail contract. Something like: “for models in top-5 list, no sustained p95 regression > X% for 30 minutes in the rollout region”. Otherwise it’s not implementable.

Marissa Cole: Product perspective: for phase 1 we want “auto-pause” (hold next stage) not auto-rollback. Humans decide rollback with context.

Marissa Cole: But we do want a clear red status in Console + a Slack notification to #eng-releases so we don’t accidentally keep promoting.

Rafael Mendes: +1. Also please specify who is the approver to override a gate. In the incident flow, it should be runtime oncall + release DRI.

Logan Wright: Re thresholds: ADR says “10% p95 regression OR 10% tps drop triggers warn, 15% triggers page” (paraphrasing). That seems aggressive for some models given normal noise. Do we have per-model noise estimates?

Nadia Rahman: We do (from week-1 baseline results). Some models have ~3–4% p95 variance even under controlled load, others are stable ~1%. ADR should allow per-model thresholds (defaults + overrides), otherwise we’ll tune in code.

Noah Patel: Yep. Plan is: defaults in ADR (warn=+10% p95, page=+15% p95; warn=-8% tps, page=-12% tps) but config supports per-model override.

Noah Patel: Also I’d like to add “min samples”: p95 computed only after N=200 requests in window (or equivalent tokens). The ADR says 50; I think that’s too low for tail.

Sofia Ivanova: 200 is better but we need to check overhead budget. If we’re only allowed <0.5% GPU per region, we can’t hammer huge QPS.

Noah Patel: We can do longer windows to accumulate samples. The evaluator already supports 15m rolling eval; we can move to 30m for gating.

Allison Grant: Proposal based on thread:
- Baseline key: (model_id + model_version, hardware_sku, region). NOT runtime_build_id.
- Store baseline version metadata includes: runtime_minor, kernel_set_id, scheduler_profile.
- On alert: payload includes observed tags (runtime_build_id, selected_kernel_id, scheduler_profile, fleet_generation) for attribution.
- Baseline updates only via explicit “baseline recompute” job + approval; no continuous rolling.
Thoughts?

Hiro Tanaka: Works for me if selected_kernel_id is always present and consistent. Otherwise we’ll be blind when auto-selection changes.

Logan Wright: Works. From telemetry POV: baseline store metadata can be in object storage/config service; don’t emit it as labels. Alert payload can fetch baseline metadata and include it.

Aisha Bello: On anti-flap: please add “two-of-three windows” or “sustained for 2 eval intervals” before paging/gate. One bad 15m slice shouldn’t page.

Sean Gallagher: +1. Also add a “cooldown” after a page (e.g. 60m) to prevent spam while we mitigate.

Connor O'Brien: Gating: please codify as:
- Gate applies only to rollout region
- Applies only to top models list (config)
- Condition: sustained page-level regression for >= 30m AND min samples met
- Action: auto-pause next stage + notify #eng-releases + create incident thread (Sev2)
If we do warn-level gating, everyone will hate us.

Marissa Cole: Agree. Warn-level should be “yellow” and show in Console but not block.

Nadia Rahman: One more: drift. If baseline is fixed, what happens when fleet gets 5% slower over 2 months (different GPUs, driver)? We’ll get chronic warns.

Nadia Rahman: Maybe add an explicit quarterly baseline refresh, plus a “baseline stale” indicator after 30 days so we remember to recalibrate.

Allison Grant: 👍 will add “baseline staleness” section: baseline artifacts have `created_at`; dashboards flag >30d; monthly refresh for top models, quarterly for others.

Rafael Mendes: Please add in ADR: if baseline stale and alert fires, oncall should treat as warn unless corroborated (customer metrics, multiple regions). Otherwise we page on expected drift.

Allison Grant: Makes sense. Might be runbook-level guidance but can add note.

Mateo Alvarez: Read through. This is the right direction (fixed baselines + explicit refresh). Strongly prefer we bias towards fewer pages early.

Mateo Alvarez: For rollout gates: pause-only in phase 1. Auto-rollback only after we have 4+ weeks of low false positives.

Allison Grant: Thanks all. I’ll incorporate these changes and mark ADR “Accepted w/ amendments” once updated.

Confluence Bot: Allison Grant updated https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ARCH/ADR-0142-Perf-canary-baselines-thresholds-rollout-gates (version 7). Summary: baseline key simplified; gating contract clarified; anti-flap + staleness added.

Allison Grant: @channel last call: any objections to the updated ADR language? (esp baseline key + gating scope)

Sean Gallagher: Looks good from SRE.

Connor O'Brien: +1, implementable.

Logan Wright: 👍

Allison Grant: Marking accepted. Next step: link this ADR from the rollout checklist + alert policy doc.

source.thread:
Priya Natarajan: Dropping ADR-023 for arch review: “Workload-aware batching defaults (fast/standard/cost-efficient)”. Link: confluence:///architecture-and-standards/decision-records/adr-023-workload-aware-batching-defaults

Key call: 3 SLO-tier defaults + workload-aware heuristics, not per-tenant real-time tuning (for v1). Please review for: safety under overload, override precedence, and observability/metrics risks. Would love sign-off today so runtime can proceed w/ 1.22.

Architecture Review Bot: :spiral_note_pad: ADR review started. Please add approvals with :white_check_mark: and any required follow-ups.

Noah Patel: Read through. Decision seems right (per-tenant tuning is a whole product + control-plane dependency we don’t want in v1).

Main concern: tail-latency for fast tier when mixed prompt lengths are in same queue. The ADR says “tight wait budget + admission control”, but please be explicit:
- Is admission control token-based (prefill tokens) or request-based?
- What’s the starvation prevention mechanism when fast tier requests come in behind large prefill jobs?

Also overload mode: ADR says “prefer backpressure for fast tier vs increasing batching”. +1, but we should state what happens when we hit sustained overload (queue depth cap? drop policy? 429 vs 503?).

Sofia Ivanova: +1 to Noah. Current plan (per scheduler doc) is admission control on estimated prefill compute (prompt_tokens bucket) + concurrency. We also cap max_wait_ms per tier and have an anti-starvation rule: fast-tier small prompts can preempt new prefill admission when queue older than X ms.

We can add to ADR:
- Admission is token-estimate based (prefill tokens bucket) + inflight decode slots.
- Starvation prevention = “age-based bypass” + separate wait budget for prefill vs decode.

Jordan Lee: On overload behavior: proposal is tier-specific queue admission:
- fast: hard cap on queue time + reject new requests once queue_depth > threshold (return 429 + Retry-After)
- standard: allow deeper queue, but enforce max queue time (fail open to standard tier? TBD)
- cost-efficient: allow deepest queue, but switch to protective mode if queue growth slope exceeds guardrail (reduce batch size + start rejecting oldest)

I’ll paste the exact config/logic once we settle constants.

Allison Grant: Obs review: the ADR’s “tier labels on metrics” is the biggest risk.

If we slap `tier={fast|standard|cost_efficient}` on EVERYTHING + reason codes + model + route + region, we’ll blow up cardinality. Seen this movie.

Request for ADR:
- Explicitly list which metrics get tier dimension (latency histograms, queue depth, batch size distribution) vs which do NOT (request logs, tracing spans maybe sampled).
- Reason codes should be enumerated small set; do not emit raw “policy string” / “route id” as a label.
- Decide whether tier is a tag on traces (ok) vs metrics (careful). If both, sampling + drop rules needed.

Logan Wright: +1 Allison. Please put in ADR as a hard constraint: “no high-cardinality dimension in timeseries backend”.

Suggested mitigations we’ve used:
- Keep tier label on a curated subset of RED (rate/errors/duration) metrics only.
- For “why this profile” debug, use structured event logs (OTel logs) with sampling, not metrics.
- If we need per-route views, compute in analytics pipeline (rollups) vs raw metrics labels.

Also note Dedicated: tenants can be high-cardinality. Don’t label tenant_id.

Rafael Mendes: Rollout/rollback section looks good but I want two concrete gates called out in ADR:
1) Perf canary gate (p99 for fast tier, p95 for standard) before expanding cohort.
2) Telemetry budget gate (metrics series count / ingestion rate) before leaving canary.

Also who owns kill switch? Needs to be explicit: runtime flag + SRE has perms.

Marissa Cole: +1 to Rafael. Also please clarify “override hierarchy” because this is where we get customer escalations.

In words, I want in ADR:
customer override > dedicated tenant policy > route policy > tier default > runtime fallback

And: if an override is invalid (model constraint), we fail closed or degrade? My vote: fail closed to standard tier + emit reason code.

Aditya Rao: Product angle: ADR text on “why 3 tiers” is good, but make sure we don’t overpromise “cost-efficient always cheaper”. Under some workloads (short prompts + streaming), it can be worse due to queueing.

Can we add a line: “cost-efficient optimizes throughput/$ per token on non-streaming + moderate/long prompts; not recommended for strict inter-token latency”?

Mateo Alvarez: Read comments. Agree with direction.

Requirements for sign-off:
- Explicit overload behavior per tier (reject/backpressure) + anti-starvation callout (Noah/Sofia)
- Explicit telemetry/cardinality constraints + mitigations (Allison/Logan)
- Rollout gates include perf + telemetry budget + who can flip kill switch (Rafael/Marissa)

Once that’s in ADR, I’m :white_check_mark:.

Sofia Ivanova: Taking edits.

Proposed updates to ADR (will push in next hour):
- Add “Risk register” section with: tail latency regression (fast tier), starvation, queue blowups (cost-efficient), metric cardinality, and override precedence confusion.
- Add overload mode behavior bullets (429 + Retry-After for fast; queue cap + max queue time for standard; protective mode + gradual shed for cost-efficient).
- Add observability guardrails (tier label only on curated metrics; reason codes enumerated; no tenant/route ids as metric labels; trace sampling).

Noah Patel: On starvation: please avoid “preempt” language unless we actually preempt running kernels (we don’t). Call it “bypass admission / separate lane for small prefill” or similar.

Also: add note that fast tier may run smaller batches even under load; we prioritize tail latency over throughput.

Allison Grant: If you add “reason codes as metrics label” please cap it to like <= 12 values and keep it stable. Also add that we’ll have a circuit breaker if series count spikes.

Logan Wright: +1. Please state “series budget” owner + alerting. I can help with the concrete number, but put placeholder: “must not increase total series by >X% during canary”.

Rafael Mendes: Kill switch: needs to live in config service with “break glass” perms for SRE on-call. We’ve had runtime-only toggles get stuck behind deploys.

Jordan Lee: Override invalid handling: we currently plan “degrade to closest compatible profile” (usually standard) and emit reason_code=MODEL_CONSTRAINT_FALLBACK. If customer explicitly pins fast and it’s incompatible, we should return 400 rather than silently switching. For route-level defaults, fallback is fine.

Marissa Cole: +1 Jordan. “Explicit customer pin” should fail fast w/ clear error; “default selection” can fallback.

Priya Natarajan: This is great. Sofia, can you tag me on the ADR edit so I can re-run arch checklist?

Sofia Ivanova: Pushed edits to ADR-023. Diffs include:
- Risk register + mitigations
- Telemetry constraints + series budget gate
- Overload behavior + error codes
- Override precedence + invalid override handling

Mateo Alvarez: Looked at updated ADR. :white_check_mark: Approved (conditional items addressed).

Allison Grant: :white_check_mark: Approved. Follow-up action item: I’ll open a ticket to define series budget threshold + add “cardinality spike” alert routing to #incidents.

Logan Wright: :white_check_mark: Approved. Please loop me in on metrics taxonomy doc before implementation merges.

Noah Patel: :white_check_mark: Approved. Please ensure scheduler doc uses “admission bypass / separate queue” language and not “preempt”.

Rafael Mendes: :white_check_mark: Approved. I’ll add rollout checklist items for telemetry budget gate and break-glass toggle verification.

Architecture Review Bot: ADR review complete. Approvals recorded: Mateo Alvarez, Allison Grant, Logan Wright, Noah Patel, Rafael Mendes. Required follow-ups noted in thread.

Priya Natarajan: Thanks all. I’ll mark ADR-023 as Accepted and link this thread + follow-ups (series budget alert, wording update, rollout checklist).

Miles Thornton: Need eyes on ADR-043 rollback guardrails before I mark it “ready for decision”. Open question: do we default to *rollback* once primary region looks healthy again, or do we use a hold-down timer (stay failed over until explicit criteria + minimum dwell)? Also: auditability requirements for automated flips (what must be recorded + retention). Link to draft: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ARCH/ADR-043

Ravi Menon: +1 on dwell/hold-down as default. Automatic rollback as soon as health recovers is where we’ll oscillate. The signal pipeline is still noisy (esp tail latency). We can do “revert only if primary is healthy for N minutes AND secondary is not overloaded” but that’s basically a hold-down + exit criteria.

Jordan Lee: From policy engine POV: easiest to implement deterministically if we model it as a state machine.
- state: NORMAL(primary)
- state: FAILED_OVER(to secondary)
Transitions require evidence.
Rollback is a transition, not “absence of failover signal”.
If ADR says “auto rollback immediately when failover condition clears”, we’ll keep fighting edge cases.

Benji Okafor: guardrail angle: we should cap transitions per tenant per hour regardless (anti-flap). I’d like ADR to explicitly say: “no more than X flips in Y minutes per {tenant,route} unless manual override.” Otherwise the controller can thrash a single high-QPS tenant.

Allison Grant: auditability: please be specific about event schema. For every recommendation + execution + rollback we need:
- decision_id / correlation_id
- evaluated policy version/hash
- triggering signals + thresholds met (ideally as a compact evidence list)
- actor (automation vs human), oncall ack if present
- impacted scope (route, model, tier, region pair)
- timestamps for “signal first seen”, “decision made”, “change applied”
And we need the dashboards to ingest those as annotations (INT-1149 was painful).

Sean Gallagher: SRE perspective: hold-down timer by default. Rollback should be conservative: we don’t want to bounce customers back mid-incident because one probe went green.
Also please call out who has authority to override hold-down in prod (oncall primary + incident commander).

Tomáš Novák: +1. Also operationally, “auto rollback” is surprising to oncall. They’ll be in the middle of mitigating capacity in the secondary, then traffic shifts again.
If we do auto rollback, it must page/notify and put a note in #incidents thread.

Ethan Park: control plane note: we can record the routing policy diff applied (before/after) but avoid dumping full configs into logs if they’re huge. Suggest storing pointers:
- policy_id old/new
- config_ref (S3/object store) with retention
Plus we need to ensure audit events are emitted even if the controller crashes mid-apply.

Devon Price: incident-bot can post a summary if we standardize event payload. Something like:
“Failover executed us-east -> eu-west (reason=reachability, corroboration=5xx_burn) scope=hosted/chat tier=standard decision_id=…”
Also for rollback events.

Victor Alvarez: network reachability is the most binary signal we have. If we’re failed over due to reachability, rollback should require *stable reachability* from multiple vantage points + minimum dwell. We’ve seen intermittent cross-region packet loss that looks green for 30s and then red again.

Ravi Menon: can we phrase ADR as:
1) Enter failover: fast path (reachability) with corroboration OR error burn w/ corroboration, etc.
2) Exit failover (rollback): slow path w/ stricter conditions + mandatory hold-down.
That’s consistent with “prefer safety over optimal latency once you’ve moved.”

Jordan Lee: yes. Implementation detail: keep `failed_over_since` and `last_transition_at` in controller state. Hold-down uses `now - last_transition_at`.
Also: rollback should be blocked if secondary is above load-shed threshold (so we don’t cause cascading overload by shifting again).

Benji Okafor: + please specify “rollback is rate-limited the same as failover” in ADR.
We’ve had “failover was rate-limited but rollback wasn’t” in other systems and it’s exactly where the flapping comes from.

Allison Grant: on evidence list: don’t put raw timeseries in the event (cardinality/size). Just include the evaluated condition names + window + outcome.
Example:
- reachability_probe: p95>timeout over 2m => true
- http_5xx_burn: 14x over 5m/1h => true
- capacity_headroom: < 10% for 3m => false
That’s enough for an auditor + oncall.

Tomáš Novák: and record “dry-run would have recommended X” vs “executed X”. If approvals are required, we need the pending state visible (otherwise oncall asks “why didn’t it fail over?”).

Sean Gallagher: can we add explicit language: “automation decisions MUST be replayable”? Meaning given the same signal snapshots + config version, simulation endpoint should output same recommended action. Determinism is the whole point.

Jordan Lee: good call. That means we should store signal snapshot IDs (or last_ingested_ts per signal) in the audit event. Then the simulation endpoint can pull the recorded snapshot.

Ethan Park: +1 but careful: storage/retention. Maybe store snapshots for 14d by default, longer for enterprise/compliance tier.

Confluence Bot: Miles Thornton updated ADR-043 Automated Routing Policy Rollback (section: Exit criteria + audit events) https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ARCH/ADR-043

Miles Thornton: Proposed decision text (tell me if this is acceptable):
- Default behavior after failover is HOLD-DOWN (minimum dwell) + conservative exit criteria.
- Rollback is a first-class automated action with the *same* safety gates as failover (rate limits, per-tenant caps, approvals if enabled).
- Exit criteria: primary healthy for T_stable (multi-signal), no active reachability failures, and secondary below overload threshold; otherwise remain failed over.
- Audit events required for recommend/execute/rollback with decision_id, policy hash, evidence list, scope, actor, and pointers to config + signal snapshot.
If no objections I’ll mark ready for final review.

Ravi Menon: works for me. Make sure “T_stable” isn’t tiny. I’d suggest 10–15m for prod, configurable per route/tier.

Benji Okafor: +1. Please include explicit “max flips per hour” default values (even if configurable) so teams don’t ship without guardrails.

Allison Grant: 👍 from obs. Add “emit audit event even when action is suppressed by guardrail” (super important for debugging why it didn’t move).

Sean Gallagher: 👍. And add note: automation should post to incident-bot when it *changes* state, and when it *refuses* to change due to guardrails during an active incident.

Miles Thornton: Got it. I’ll incorporate those last tweaks + push for signoff tomorrow.

source.messages:
Confluence Bot: ADR updated: ADR-042 Failover Trigger Signal Hierarchy https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ARCH/ADR-042

Miles Thornton: dropping this here for architecture review. main questions: what signals are authoritative enough to flip regions, and what guardrails we need to avoid oscillation. would love comments today/tomorrow so we can unblock ENG-2412.

Ravi Menon: skimmed. overall +1 on “reachability > 5xx burn > tail latency burn > capacity”. one nit: reachability needs to be *from where*? if it’s only from our control plane vantage points, we risk failing over for a bad ISP / partial internet issue.

Victor Alvarez: yeah, strongly agree. reachability is tricky. i’d recommend defining “authoritative reachability” as: (a) cloud internal (region-to-region) + (b) external synthetic probes from at least 2 providers/PoPs. if only one fails, it’s not authoritative.

Benji Okafor: +1. also, reachability should have a hold-down like “must be failing for N consecutive windows” AND ideally corroborate with data-plane symptoms (e.g., gateway 5xx burn or TLS handshake failures). otherwise we’ll get flip-flop when probes are flaky.

Allison Grant: on the measurement side: please be explicit which 5xx signal we treat as authoritative. app 5xx vs gateway 5xx vs upstream (model backend) errors. i’ve seen incidents where backend error spikes but gateway was fine due to retries masking it.

Jordan Lee: deterministic evaluation question: ADR says “take highest-priority signal that exceeds threshold.” what happens when reachability is “unknown” because telemetry stream is missing? defaulting unknown => unhealthy would be a disaster.

Miles Thornton: good call. my intent is unknown => “insufficient evidence”, not unhealthy. in that case the policy should fall back to next signal *only if* it’s corroborated by at least one independent signal.

Tomáš Novák: +1 on the “unknown handling” being spelled out. we’ve been paged by missing metrics before. would prefer “unknown never triggers failover” unless operator forces.

Sean Gallagher: i want to push on one thing: do we really want reachability to be highest? historically our biggest customer impact is sustained elevated 5xx + timeouts, not just probe failures. reachability probes can be noisy.

Victor Alvarez: if we don’t treat reachability as top-tier, we’ll fail over late in cases of real network partition where app metrics lag (because traffic can’t reach the region to generate 5xx). but we can tighten the definition so it’s less noisy.

Ravi Menon: maybe we separate “hard down” vs “degraded” modes? like: reachability-hard-fail => immediate flip (fast path), but reachability-soft / partial => needs corroboration with 5xx burn.

Mina Haddad: +1, also helps with partial outages. we’ve had cases where only one AZ had issues. if reachability is “region down” but it’s actually one LB path, we might move too much traffic and cause overload elsewhere.

Allison Grant: could encode this as levels: HEALTHY / DEGRADED / UNHEALTHY. reachability can drive UNHEALTHY only when (1) probe failure across >=2 PoPs AND (2) internal east-west also failing OR error burn is already elevated.

Jordan Lee: from policy-engine POV, would love it if ADR defines:
- signal “authoritativeness” (hard vs soft)
- required corroboration count
- minimum dwell time before a flip
- cooldown after flip
So we can implement deterministically and unit test.

Benji Okafor: on oscillation: the ADR currently has “dampening timers” but doesn’t say if they’re per-signal or per-action. i’d recommend:
1) per-signal debounce (e.g., 3/5 windows)
2) action hold-down (don’t flip back for X minutes unless catastrophic)
3) rate limit flips per tenant/tier (max 1 per 30m?)

Sergio Costa: +1. and log a reason code that’s stable (small enum) for dashboards. if we include raw threshold values in reason, cardinality will explode.

Miles Thornton: noted. reason codes will be like REACHABILITY_HARD_FAIL, ERROR_BURN_FAST, LATENCY_BURN_FAST, CAPACITY_EXHAUSTION, OPERATOR_OVERRIDE.

Sean Gallagher: can we make “capacity exhaustion” not an automatic failover trigger by default? we’ve seen headroom metrics lie during scale events. if we fail over on capacity signal alone, we could cascade.

Mina Haddad: agree. capacity should probably gate *how much* traffic we shift, not *whether* we shift. e.g. “if target region headroom < X, then partial failover + shed low-priority tiers.”

Benji Okafor: yes. i want explicit mention: “capacity is a safety gate and shaping input, not a primary trigger.” failover triggered by reachability/errors/latency; capacity decides blast radius.

Victor Alvarez: also: reachability failures can happen due to DNS / TTL / resolver cache weirdness. do we have a plan to distinguish “DNS wrong” vs “region down”? maybe include separate DNS health (authoritative resolvers + anycast).

Allison Grant: we can add a “network/DNS reachability” panel, but i’m wary of adding another top-tier signal without clarity. maybe fold into reachability bucket but require internal + external agreement.

Jordan Lee: for “prevent oscillation”: is rollback handled in ADR-043? if so, ADR-042 should at least reference the interface: i.e., this ADR decides *when to flip*, ADR-043 decides *when to revert*.

Miles Thornton: yep, ADR-043 is coming next. i’ll add a section in ADR-042: “this ADR does not define rollback; see ADR-043.” and will clarify unknown-handling + hard/soft reachability.

Sean Gallagher: request: include explicit operator mode guidance. e.g. “automation in prod requires corroboration >=2 signals” vs “operator override can flip with 1 signal.” we don’t want oncall to assume the system will do something it won’t.

Tomáš Novák: +1 and please document “do not fail over” scenarios. ex: single model backend regression in one region shouldn’t flip all Hosted API routes.

Ravi Menon: last point: tail latency burn can be a side effect of retry storms. if we trigger failover on latency burn, we might amplify. can we require latency burn + elevated 5xx OR latency burn + reachability degraded?

Miles Thornton: this is super helpful. action items i’m taking for ADR text:
- define reachability hard vs soft and required corroboration
- unknown signal semantics (unknown != unhealthy)
- capacity as safety gate/shaping, not standalone trigger
- oscillation: per-signal debounce + action hold-down + rate limits (with refs to ADR-043)
- add explicit operator vs automation corroboration rules
will push an updated draft EOD and re-tag.

Confluence Bot: Miles Thornton edited ADR-042 Failover Trigger Signal Hierarchy https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ARCH/ADR-042

Allison Grant: once it’s updated, ping me and i’ll sanity check the metrics definitions so we don’t bake in ambiguous “5xx” language.

Victor Alvarez: i’ll add a comment in-page with a concrete reachability criteria proposal (PoPs/providers/internal).

source.content:
Talia Benson: trying to close out ADR-022 wording today. can we align on taxonomy for "overhead" vs "request-attributable" cost? main confusion: Console wants numbers that match invoices, Optimize wants marginal cost for what-if. what’s in/out?

Ethan Park: +1. proposing we standardize: (A) request_attributable = things that would go to ~0 if request volume goes to 0, holding deployment fixed; (B) allocated_overhead = shared pool (idle, warm capacity, control plane, etc.) allocated by a policy.

Laura Bennett: careful on that definition. for Dedicated, if volume goes to 0 we *still* pay for reserved GPUs. so request_attributable can’t be “goes to zero” universally.

Rishi Malhotra: +1. FP&A needs two lenses: (1) incremental usage (variable) and (2) fully-loaded (incl. reserved capacity / idle / shared). we need the API to support both without hand-waving.

Chloe Martin: Console requirement: when a customer clicks a single request we need a breakdown that’s not misleading. if we show fully-loaded on a single request it’ll look random/noisy, esp at low traffic.

Logan Wright: telemetry side: we can reliably attribute per-request: tokens_prefill, tokens_decode, kv_cache_hit_tokens, kv_cache_miss_tokens, gpu_ms_prefill, gpu_ms_decode, batch_size_effective, queue_ms. we *cannot* reliably attribute “GPU idle” to a specific request without a policy.

Nadia Rahman: + also retries. do we treat retries as overhead or request-attributable? telemetry can attach retry_count and “attempt cost” if it’s within our control plane.

Allison Grant: observability principle: separate measurement from allocation. request-attributable should be tied to measured per-request counters; overhead is a separate measured pool + allocation rule. if we mix those we’ll never explain drift.

Sofia Ivanova: runtime POV: batch inefficiency is tricky. if you run batch size 1, you’re “wasting” potential throughput, but the GPU kernels still ran for that request. I’d include that as request-attributable compute time (gpu_ms) and then optionally show “efficiency delta” relative to an ideal batch.

Omar El-Sayed: +1. also KV cache: prefill avoided due to cache hit is *negative* delta vs baseline, but we still might have cache memory overhead. recommend: attribute cache benefit to request_attributable (fewer gpu_ms) and treat cache infra/memory as overhead pool.

Cole Summers: for billing hooks: invoices today are mixed. hosted API is usage-based (variable). Dedicated is commit-based + overage. Private depends. if Console wants reconciliation we need fields that can map to “billable components” but cost-model v1 should not become billing system.

Talia Benson: ok so proposal: cost-model output includes both “measured direct” and “allocated” components, and UI can choose. but taxonomy needs crisp categories. what exactly counts as overhead pool?

Laura Bennett: overhead pool includes:
- GPU idle time within a billing window (hosted + dedicated pools)
- warm standby / autoscaler buffer
- cluster fragmentation (unusable slices)
- control plane + schedulers (compute)
- networking egress *if* we treat it as shared, otherwise direct per-request bytes
- storage for model weights + cache store
- observability pipeline (metrics/logs) and oncall is not in product COGS at request level (but for unit economics it’s overhead)

Ethan Park: we should be careful: control plane CPU can be “per request” (auth, routing) and measurable. do we want that request-attributable? the API can include cpu_ms_control_plane per request.

Allison Grant: I’d still keep it under request_attributable if it’s measured per request. overhead should mean “not directly measured per request” OR “intentionally allocated at window-level”.

Rishi Malhotra: +1. finance doesn’t care whether it’s CPU or GPU; we care about consistent classification and reconciliation. If we can measure per-request control plane compute, great—put it in request-attributable.

Chloe Martin: for Console, a sane default breakdown for a request:
- compute_prefill
- compute_decode
- cache_savings (or cache_effect)
- control_plane
- network
and then optional “allocated overhead” at daily/monthly window.

Logan Wright: for network we can do bytes_in/bytes_out per request and multiply by region price table. but we don’t have perfect accounting for internal service-to-service hops.

Nadia Rahman: in v1 can we just scope request-attributable network to external egress (response payload) and keep internal traffic overhead? :shrug:

Ethan Park: that matches our ability to bound complexity. add explicit fields:
- request_attributable.network_egress_usd
- allocated_overhead.network_shared_usd (optional)
with policy_version in output.

Sofia Ivanova: question: is queue time cost? i.e., request sits queued while GPU is idle vs busy. queue time itself isn’t cost, but it indicates saturation. i’d avoid turning queue_ms into cost.

Omar El-Sayed: +1. queue_ms is SLO, not COGS. cost is captured in gpu_ms. but in dedicated, queue can happen while reserved GPUs are maxed; still not an extra cost line.

Laura Bennett: agreed. don’t invent cost from latency.

Cole Summers: retries: if we retry due to transient infra error, customers may not be billed for that attempt (depending on policy). but for cost, we did pay for it. i'd treat retry attempt compute as request-attributable *to the original request* with a subcategory “waste/retry”.

Allison Grant: +1, and a flag for “billable=false attempt” vs “billable=true”. keeps cost model distinct from invoicing but supports reconciliation.

Talia Benson: ok, but I still need the doc-level definition of overhead so people stop arguing in review. can we lock language like:
"Overhead: costs that are not deterministically attributable to an individual request via runtime/telemetry counters, and are allocated to requests/tenants via a documented policy and reporting window." ?

Rishi Malhotra: yes, and add that allocation policy must be versioned + windowed (daily/monthly) to reconcile with FP&A reporting windows.

Laura Bennett: +1. include examples: idle GPU minutes, reserved capacity not utilized, warm pool, shared storage, baseline platform spend.

Ethan Park: and explicitly exclude “batch inefficiency” from overhead. it’s a property of how the request was served and captured in gpu_ms + observed throughput. we can add an *informational* metric “efficiency_ratio” but cost remains direct.

Chloe Martin: :thumbsup: also ask: should Console show overhead by default? i vote no; provide toggle “Fully loaded cost (includes allocated overhead)” with tooltip pointing to window + policy.

Allison Grant: that’s consistent with observability best practice. default view should be deterministic per-request.

Logan Wright: telemetry implication: need to ensure per-request gpu_ms split prefill/decode is stable (esp with speculative decoding etc.). we’ll document “measured stages” and allow unknown stage bucket.

Omar El-Sayed: + add clarification: kv cache hit avoids prefill compute; but cache memory pressure can increase eviction and impact others. that externality lives in overhead, not per-request.

Talia Benson: sounds like we have convergence. action items:
1) Ethan + Allison: update ADR-022 with overhead definition + explicit non-examples (batch inefficiency, queue time)
2) Laura + Rishi: provide the overhead pool examples + allocation policy requirements (version, window)
3) Logan + Nadia: confirm which counters are “deterministic per-request” for v1 so taxonomy matches reality

Cole Summers: and pls add a sentence: cost-model outputs are not the billing source of truth; invoices may differ due to pricing rules/credits/minimums.

Talia Benson: noted. I’ll add that to the ADR preamble.

Chloe Martin: thanks all. once ADR text lands, Console can align copy and stop the "why doesn’t this equal invoice" ping-pong.

Laura Bennett: appreciate it. consistency > perfection here. as long as we can reconcile at the window level with policy_version, we’re good.

Ethan Park: will push draft text in ~1h and tag for review.

Allison Grant: I’ll sanity check naming ("request_attributable" vs "direct") to match API guidelines.

Logan Wright: looping in Thomas separately on cardinality for overhead allocation dimensions (tenant x model x region) so we don’t blow up tables.

Talia Benson: closing thread once draft is out. thanks.

source.text:
Talia Benson: Hi folks — need architecture eyes on the Shadow Traffic routing ADR before we mark it “accepted”. Can you review and leave comments inline by EOD tomorrow? Main things we need clarity on: *where* the mirroring happens in the request path + expected perf impact.

Talia Benson: Confluence ADR draft: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ARCH/ADR-Shadow-Traffic-Routing-Architecture (WIP)

Ravi Menon: Read through. Overall direction makes sense (inline mirroring, fail-open). I think the ADR needs to be explicit about placement:
1) request enters router
2) auth/tenant resolution
3) rollout variant assignment (control)
4) return control response to user
5) mirror *in parallel* to shadow target(s)

Ravi Menon: Right now section 2.1 kind of implies we might mirror before variant assignment; that would make comparing control vs shadow messy.

Mina Haddad: +1 to Ravi. Also: mirroring should happen after request normalization (canonicalized request payload) so fingerprinting is stable + we don’t end up hashing raw headers w/ PII.

Priya Natarajan: Skimmed. I’m aligned on inline mirroring as default vs async replay, but the ADR needs stronger justification on *performance risk*. Specifically:
- additional CPU in the hot path (hashing + serialization)
- additional network fanout and connection pool pressure
- any extra allocations around streaming

Priya Natarajan: Can we quantify overhead targets in the ADR (even rough): e.g., p99 added router CPU < X%, added latency on user path ~0ms (since fire-and-forget), and hard drop conditions.

Ethan Park: We can add a perf budget section. Implementation plan we’re using is: sampling decision happens early, and if not sampled we do basically nothing besides compute fingerprint (which we already do for rollout bucketing).

Ethan Park: For sampled requests, we spawn an async task with strict limits (max inflight per route + global circuit breaker). User path never awaits the shadow call.

Sean Gallagher: SRE view: the ADR should explicitly state what “fail-open” means at runtime. e.g.
- shadow target call timeout: 1.5s (or lower)
- if shadow queue depth > N OR event ingestion lag > M -> disable shadowing automatically (circuit breaker)
- if router is under load -> shadowing is first thing to shed

Sean Gallagher: Also I’d like it to call out that shadow errors are “acceptable” up to some threshold without paging (ticket vs page), so folks don’t assume shadow 99.9% success is required.

Allison Grant: +1. Also add a note about metrics cardinality. If router emits per-request tags, we’ll blow up Prometheus/OTel. The ADR should reference the companion data model ADR and explicitly say we only emit low-card dimensions.

Caleb Johnson: On perf: if we do inline mirroring, do we plan to emit an event on the control path (request start/end) AND a separate shadow pair event later? Need clarity on how we join control+shadow without storing huge payloads in the router.

Jordan Lee: Re: join key, in the schema thread we agreed on a deterministic `request_fingerprint_v1` + a per-request `shadow_pair_id` (or request_id) that’s safe to emit. Suggest ADR says: join happens in telemetry pipeline, not in router memory.

Jordan Lee: Also the ADR should explicitly specify the hashing input. I don’t want “implementation-defined” here. Proposed:
- canonicalized request body (excluding headers)
- route_id + tenant_id
- HMAC w/ server secret
Then truncate to 128 bits.

Mina Haddad: +1 but be careful: request body can be huge (multi-message chat). We should specify we hash a bounded representation: e.g., stable hash over first N bytes + lengths + model params. Otherwise we create CPU spikes.

Ravi Menon: Agree. For bucketing we don’t need cryptographic perfection, just stable-enough and privacy safe. HMAC is good. Also call out collision expectations and that collisions just add noise, not correctness bugs.

Benji Okafor: FYI we already made some policy decisions in eng-platform (schema review). Shadow policy defaults to consistent hashing using `request_fingerprint_v1`; no custom hash keys. Fail-open only (v1). Default `max_inflight_per_route=100` server-side. Might be useful to incorporate those into the ADR so it doesn’t read like “anything goes”.

Priya Natarajan: Great. Another question: where exactly is the mirror executed? In the same router process? separate threadpool? sidecar? The ADR says “routing layer” but that’s ambiguous.

Ethan Park: Current plan is router process (same binary), but separate “shadow executor” threadpool + bounded queue. When queue is full we drop. No backpressure to user path.

Sean Gallagher: Please spell out: bounded queue, bounded concurrency, and “drop” semantics (including counters). Without that, folks will keep adding retries and we’ll self-DDOS.

Allison Grant: Also: we should state whether the shadow call uses the same egress path and connection pools as the user call. If shared, we need a per-pool cap so shadowing doesn’t starve the control traffic under ephemeral port pressure.

Ravi Menon: Good call. ADR can say: shadow egress uses a dedicated client with lower priority + smaller pool + separate timeouts.

Caleb Johnson: Streaming: do we mirror streaming requests at all? If we do “mirror full prompt at start” that’s fine, but mirroring the stream tokens might double traffic and create weird partial captures.

Mina Haddad: For v1, I think the ADR should say: we mirror the *input* (request body) and capture the *shadow output* as non-streaming in the background (i.e., worker consumes full response). For user path, we keep streaming unaffected.

Jordan Lee: There’s still the issue that some request types depend on streaming partial tool calls, etc. But for shadow eval we mostly need final output. We can be explicit that shadow capture may be truncated/partial under timeouts and scoring must handle “incomplete”.

Priya Natarajan: +1: make degraded behavior explicit. The ADR should say what happens when:
- shadow target returns 429/5xx
- shadow target exceeds timeout
- scoring pipeline is down
- telemetry ingestion is lagging

Sean Gallagher: And importantly: none of those should page oncall unless control path is impacted. Shadow path pages should be separate and higher threshold.

Ethan Park: Action items I’m hearing for ADR:
- add explicit placement diagram (after control variant selection)
- define perf budget + overhead expectations
- define executor model (bounded queue, bounded concurrency, drop policy)
- define deterministic fingerprint input + bounded hashing
- define streaming semantics (v1: input mirrored, shadow output captured best-effort)
- clarify egress isolation (separate pool)
- add failure mode table w/ behavior and metrics

Ravi Menon: Yep. Also suggest we add a short section “Why not async replay from logs?” with the key point: replay requires retaining prompts longer + creates selection bias + not real-time for canary decisions.

Allison Grant: Please reference the data-model/cardinality ADR in the routing ADR (even just a link + one paragraph). Otherwise folks will invent dimensions and we’ll pay later.

Benji Okafor: I’ll paste the schema constraints into the ADR comments (max targets, fail-open only, header allowlist/denylist) so it stays consistent.

Priya Natarajan: Sounds good. Once those are incorporated I’m comfortable approving. I’ll re-review tomorrow.

Talia Benson: Thanks all. Ethan/Ravi/Mina can you take the ADR edits? Please tag @Priya Natarajan and me when the revised draft is ready, and we’ll move it to “Accepted” if there are no remaining blockers. :thumbsup:

Ethan Park: Acknowledged. Will push updates and ping.

source.content:
Ibrahim Khan: Dropping draft for SDK retry/timeout standard v0.9. Goal: publish as API standard + reference ADRs for SDK parity work. Looking for SRE + platform feedback before we stamp this.

Ibrahim Khan: Doc link: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ARCH/sdk-retry-timeout-standard (permissions should be open)

Ibrahim Khan: Key proposals:
- Retryable: 408, 409 (optional), 429, 500-599 (+ network errors)
- Respect Retry-After (seconds + HTTP date)
- Exponential backoff w/ jitter, max attempts default = 3 (1 initial + 2 retries)
- Timeout model: connect + read + overall (overall default 60s; connect 10s; read 60s)
- Streaming: no automatic retry after first byte/event; surface partial output + guidance on idempotency

Allison Grant: Thanks. SRE POV: standardizing is good, but we need to be careful w/ retry storms.
- cap max backoff (e.g., 20s) and add a small floor (e.g., 250ms)
- ensure jitter is full jitter (random between 0 and base), not +/- 10% (the latter still herds)

Allison Grant: Also want explicit: 500-599 *except* 501? (rare but typically terminal) and 509? (not real but some proxies emit). Might be better to say 500-599, but allow SDKs to treat unknown 5xx as retryable.

Sean Gallagher: +1 on backoff caps. We got bit before by clients doing immediate retries on 429.

Sean Gallagher: Can we tighten default attempts? 3 total seems fine for most, but for latency sensitive apps it can blow the SLO. I’d rather default 2 total (1 retry) for non-streaming? Or keep 3 but with overall timeout enforcement so it can’t go unbounded.

Ibrahim Khan: We can enforce overall timeout across attempts (budget-based) so retries stop when budget exhausted. That might address the “3 attempts” concern.

Henry Cho: From product side, we should avoid surprising behavior changes where users see longer tail latencies. If we keep 3 total, docs should say “retries are best-effort within timeout budget.”

Ethan Park: Platform note: we’re in the middle of making Retry-After consistent on 429 across gateways. Assume it will be present most of the time, but SDKs still need fallback behavior when header is missing.

Ethan Park: Please also mention request_id propagation requirements. If SDKs retry, we want both:
- each attempt has its own request_id
- SDK error includes the last request_id + (ideally) a list of attempt request_ids (or at least retry_count)

Allison Grant: +1. For dashboards we’re standardizing fields: request_id, retry_count, timeout_ms, retry_after_ms (if present), final_status.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Auth nuance: do NOT retry 401/403. Also make sure we don’t retry on invalid API key errors that come back 400 w/ a specific error code.

Ibrahim Khan: Yup, doc currently says “never retry on 4xx except 408/409/429” but I can add explicit callouts for 401/403 and “invalid_auth” error code.

Marcus Lin: Security review ask: please include a note that SDKs must not log Authorization header or API key values in retry debug logs. We’ve seen “request headers: { ... }” get printed.

Marcus Lin: Also for timeout errors, error messages should not echo env var contents. Sounds obvious but we should spell it out in standard.

Ibrahim Khan: Noted. Will add a “redaction requirements” section and link to the auth/secret handling guidance.

Dana Lo: TS SDK current behavior: fetch will throw TypeError on network issues and AbortError on timeouts. We can map those to a common “NetworkError” and “TimeoutError” but we need standard definitions.

Ibrahim Khan: Proposed taxonomy section includes: TimeoutError, RateLimitError, APIError (status + code), NetworkError, StreamError. Each has retryable: boolean.

Mateusz Zielinski: Go SDK: net/http timeout vs context deadline is tricky. If you set both, you can double-cancel and get confusing errors. Standard should say “overall timeout is authoritative; connect/read are best-effort.”

Allison Grant: Can we include recommended backoff formula in the doc? Makes it harder for teams to invent their own.

Ibrahim Khan: Sure. Current suggestion:
```
base = min(cap, initial * 2^(attempt-1))
sleep = rand(0, base)  // full jitter
```
Plus if Retry-After present: sleep = max(sleep, retry_after)

Sean Gallagher: Please don’t do max() blindly. If Retry-After is huge (some gateways send minutes), client could hang forever unless bounded by overall timeout. So: sleep = min(remaining_budget, max(jitter, retry_after)).

Ibrahim Khan: Good catch. Will update to “bounded by remaining overall timeout budget.”

Ethan Park: Also 409 is a footgun. Some 409s are safe (lock contention) but others indicate semantic conflict. If we include 409 as retryable, we should gate it behind an explicit opt-in.

Henry Cho: Agree. 409 retry default feels risky and can cause duplicate side effects depending on endpoint.

Ibrahim Khan: I’ll change: default retryable = 408, 429, 500-599; 409 only if endpoint is documented idempotent OR user opts in.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: That plus idempotency guidance for POSTs is important. Even with “safe endpoints,” customers often put side effects behind the call.

Allison Grant: Streaming: I like “no automatic retries after first byte.” We should explicitly say: if disconnect happens before any tokens/events received, SDK MAY retry if request is idempotent (or user sets idempotency key). Otherwise surface partial output and error.

Mateusz Zielinski: +1. In Go, once we start reading the stream, replaying is messy. Better to surface a typed StreamDisconnectedError with request_id and bytes_read/tokens_received if possible.

Dana Lo: TS streaming: we can count events. We should standardize event types and what counts as “first byte” (headers vs first data chunk). I’d treat “first SSE event/data chunk” as the boundary.

Ibrahim Khan: Let’s define boundary as “after response body consumption begins / after first stream event is emitted to user.” Before that, it’s effectively a non-streaming request and retry is safe-ish.

Monica Patel: This thread is exactly what we needed. Can we land doc as standard this week so the SDK parity work can cite it? We’ve got PRs queued that are waiting on “what are defaults.”

Sean Gallagher: I’m ok approving if we incorporate:
- overall timeout budget across attempts
- full jitter + caps
- explicit non-retry list (401/403/400 invalid_auth)
- 409 opt-in only

Allison Grant: Same. And please include observability fields + recommendation that SDK logs include retry_count + final_status at INFO when retries happen (but no secrets).

Ethan Park: Platform signoff: add a note that SDKs should parse Retry-After both delta-seconds and HTTP-date; and fallback to backoff if invalid. We’ll keep working on gateway consistency.

Ibrahim Khan: Action items:
1) Update retryable set + 409 opt-in language
2) Add “budget-based retries” section w/ formula + remaining_budget cap
3) Add security redaction requirements
4) Add request_id + retry_count requirements
I’ll push v1.0 in Confluence and then create ADR links for parity epic.

Monica Patel: :thumbsup: once v1.0 is up, I’ll reference it in ENG-5121 + ping language owners to implement exactly as written.

Allison Grant: :eyes: please @ me on the final page; I’ll also link to the rollout dashboards spec.

Ibrahim Khan: Will do. Expect update EOD tomorrow.

Sean Gallagher: One more: please call out that retry on 429 must respect Retry-After, and if absent use backoff with a minimum delay (e.g., >= 250ms). That’s our main protection.

Ibrahim Khan: Added to the checklist. Thanks all.

source.messages:
ArchReviewBot: New architecture review request posted by @Omar El-Sayed. Please review within 48h. Links: Confluence (design) https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/x/kv-compaction , Draft ADRs https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/x/adr-compaction-budget + https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/x/adr-eviction-policy

Omar El-Sayed: Hey folks, requesting arch review feedback on KV compaction allocator changes. Main asks:
1) allocator invariants we should codify (what must never be violated)
2) correctness boundaries for relocation (what we guarantee vs what scheduler must guarantee)
3) rollback plan / kill-switch safety (what happens mid-relocation)

PR is still WIP but design is pretty stable. @Noah Patel can you take first pass?

Noah Patel: Yup. Skimming now. Quick clarifying q: are we allowing relocation of *active* sequences (in decode) or only “paused at boundary” sequences?

Samir Qureshi: Current proposal: relocation only when seq enters “movable” state at scheduler boundary (prefill->decode boundary or between decode steps). We don’t relocate while a decode kernel is reading KV.

Sofia Ivanova: +1. Scheduler hook will mark a batch boundary. We can do it after token emission / before next decode step. For continuous batching, we treat “step” as the scheduling quantum.

Noah Patel: Good. Then the core correctness question becomes: what is the exact handshake between scheduler and allocator during that boundary? Need a crisp invariant like “no kernel holds pointers into KV arena during relocation”. How is that enforced? (epoch, refcount, lock, etc)

Samir Qureshi: Mechanism is per-seq state + epoch barrier. Scheduler sets seq.state = MOVING and increments kv_epoch; decode path checks epoch at step start. We also take a short allocator lock for page table swap.

Noah Patel: Please write the invariants explicitly in doc/ADR. Something like:
- I1: For a given sequence, its KV mapping is only mutated while seq.state ∈ {MOVING, EVICTING}.
- I2: A seq can enter MOVING only when it is not present in any in-flight batch (no outstanding GPU work referencing its KV).
- I3: After relocation, mapping swap is atomic wrt readers at step boundary (single pointer swap / generation bump).

Also define what “not present in any in-flight batch” means in your runtime (CPU-side queue vs GPU completion).

Julian Moore: +1 on “GPU completion” nuance. Our trace spans today show decode launch->completion can overlap next scheduler tick for other seqs. Need explicit sync point for the moving seq.

Sofia Ivanova: Implementation detail: we already have per-batch CUDA event tracking for streaming. For MOVING seq we’ll wait on its last decode event (not global sync). That’s in ENG-4824 notes.

Noah Patel: Great. Then I’d like to see a statement: “Relocation requires waiting on last_decode_event(seq)”. Put that in correctness boundary.

Omar El-Sayed: Makes sense. We’ll add it to design + tests.

Noah Patel: Next: allocator invariants.
What’s the unit of allocation? pages/blocks? And are we guaranteeing no partial block moves?

Samir Qureshi: Arena is split into fixed-size pages (currently 64KB). Seqs have a page list per layer/head. Compaction moves whole pages and rewrites the mapping table. We never move partial pages.

Noah Patel: Then add:
- I4: A page is owned by exactly one seq at a time OR in free list; no shared pages.
- I5: Free list correctness: a page can appear in free list at most once; transitions are linearizable.
- I6: Mapping table is source of truth; any cached pointer must be derivable + invalidated on epoch bump.

Also please define what happens with “prefix cache” pages if applicable. Are they under same allocator? (I saw mention of cached prefixes being preemptible.)

Omar El-Sayed: Prefix cache uses same arena but separate namespace/owner type. We tagged those pages as owner=PREFIX_CACHE and have separate eviction priority. Compaction can move them too, but eviction can drop them.

Lena Fischer: On correctness boundaries: eviction of prefix cache is fine, eviction of active sequences is not (unless scheduler cancels seq). We should be explicit that “eviction” for sequences = “preempt” which is a higher-level decision.

Noah Patel: Exactly. Language matters. I’d suggest:
- “Eviction” only applies to cacheable artifacts (prefix cache, speculative prefill results)
- “Preemption” applies to sequences, and requires scheduler-level cancellation semantics + client-visible behavior

Do you ever “evict” a sequence’s KV to CPU? If not, keep it out of scope.

Omar El-Sayed: Not in this phase. Only drop prefix cache / low-priority cached artifacts. Seqs are either kept resident or rejected at admission.

Mateo Alvarez: +1 keep it narrow. If we say “evict sequences” it’ll get misread as swap-to-host.

Allison Grant: Observability angle: please also define invariant around metrics emission during rollback. If kill-switch flips mid-move, what metrics do we expect? (don’t want half-updated counters)

Samir Qureshi: Counters are per-step. We increment “bytes_moved” after successful mapping swap + verification. If abort, we record “compaction_aborts_total” and don’t add bytes moved.

Noah Patel: Good. On rollback plan / kill-switch:
- Can kill-switch be flipped at runtime without restart?
- What happens if it’s flipped while a seq is MOVING?

Omar El-Sayed: Kill-switch is config propagated via control-plane; runtime reads it every N seconds (default 5s). Behavior:
- If disabled, scheduler stops selecting new relocation work.
- If a relocation is in progress, we complete the current “page copy + swap” step then stop (don’t start a new step).

Noah Patel: That’s sane. But you need to articulate the state machine so “stop” doesn’t leave dangling pages.
Propose explicit guarantee:
- G1: At any point during relocation, there exists exactly one valid mapping (either old or new). We do not publish the new mapping until copy is complete.
- G2: If disabled mid-step, allocator reverts to old mapping (or continues to new) but never leaves mixed mapping.

How do you prevent partial publish? (I assume a single mapping pointer swap.)

Samir Qureshi: Yes, mapping swap is a single atomic pointer update to per-seq page table + generation bump. Copy occurs into fresh pages, then we flip. Only after flip we free old pages.

Julian Moore: Do we have a post-copy verification? (like checksum?) Probably too expensive, but maybe a debug mode.

Samir Qureshi: In debug builds we can optionally run a lightweight sanity check (page table sizes + bounds) and poison old pages before free. Not checksumming content.

Noah Patel: That’s fine; document it as “debug-only guardrail”.

Sofia Ivanova: For scheduler boundary, we also need to mention we won’t relocate if the seq is in “STREAMING_PARTIAL” state (client is mid-stream) unless we have the event barrier. But we do have per-seq event barrier, so it should be ok. Still, I’ll add a note that relocation is disallowed when we can’t attach an event (legacy path).

Noah Patel: Please call that out as a correctness boundary: “compaction requires CUDA event tracking enabled”. If there are fallback codepaths, they should opt-out.

Lena Fischer: Backpressure integration: if fragmentation high and compaction budget exhausted, we may still OOM. What’s the policy? We should ensure admission control triggers before allocator hard OOM.

Omar El-Sayed: Proposed: if frag_ratio > threshold AND free_pages < min_free_pages, we signal scheduler “KV pressure” which tightens admission and can trigger prefix cache eviction first. Compaction is best-effort, not the only safety.

Noah Patel: Good. Make that explicit under “correctness boundaries”: compaction is not relied on for safety; admission control remains primary OOM prevention.

Allison Grant: On rollout/rollback criteria: do we have a hard metric-based rollback? (p99 spike correlated with compaction time)

Omar El-Sayed: Yes, SRE is defining it, but initial suggestion: rollback if p99 decode latency +15% for 10m AND compaction_time_ms_p99 > budget AND eviction_rate > threshold. Still tuning.

Mateo Alvarez: We’ll put exact thresholds in SRE guardrails doc. Design doc should just state: “feature flagged, canary, auto-rollback wired to tail latency + compaction metrics.”

Noah Patel: Last request: tests.
Please add a stress test that hits cancellation during relocation (classic hazard). Even if scheduler sets MOVING only at boundaries, cancellations can happen asynchronously.

Samir Qureshi: Already on the list (ENG-4827). We’ll model: cancel arrives after copy but before mapping swap; and cancel after swap but before freeing old pages.

Noah Patel: Perfect. Summarizing review feedback:
- Write explicit invariants I1-I6 + guarantees G1-G2 in doc
- Define boundary conditions: requires per-seq GPU completion event; opt-out legacy paths
- Clarify vocabulary: eviction=cache artifacts; sequences are never evicted (only admitted/rejected/cancelled)
- Kill-switch semantics: stop selecting new work; finish current step; never publish partial mapping
- Add cancellation stress tests around swap/free window

Once those are in, I’m comfortable with the architecture direction.

Omar El-Sayed: Awesome, thanks. I’ll update the Confluence page + ADR text and ping back for final sign-off.

Noah Patel: Sounds good. :thumbsup:

Allison Grant: +1, please tag me when metric definitions are finalized so we can align names with the standard.

Omar El-Sayed: Will do.
