- What happens if it’s flipped while a seq is MOVING?

Omar El-Sayed: Kill-switch is config propagated via control-plane; runtime reads it every N seconds (default 5s). Behavior:
- If disabled, scheduler stops selecting new relocation work.
- If a relocation is in progress, we complete the current “page copy + swap” step then stop (don’t start a new step).

Noah Patel: That’s sane. But you need to articulate the state machine so “stop” doesn’t leave dangling pages.
Propose explicit guarantee:
- G1: At any point during relocation, there exists exactly one valid mapping (either old or new). We do not publish the new mapping until copy is complete.
- G2: If disabled mid-step, allocator reverts to old mapping (or continues to new) but never leaves mixed mapping.

How do you prevent partial publish? (I assume a single mapping pointer swap.)

Samir Qureshi: Yes, mapping swap is a single atomic pointer update to per-seq page table + generation bump. Copy occurs into fresh pages, then we flip. Only after flip we free old pages.

Julian Moore: Do we have a post-copy verification? (like checksum?) Probably too expensive, but maybe a debug mode.

Samir Qureshi: In debug builds we can optionally run a lightweight sanity check (page table sizes + bounds) and poison old pages before free. Not checksumming content.

Noah Patel: That’s fine; document it as “debug-only guardrail”.

Sofia Ivanova: For scheduler boundary, we also need to mention we won’t relocate if the seq is in “STREAMING_PARTIAL” state (client is mid-stream) unless we have the event barrier. But we do have per-seq event barrier, so it should be ok. Still, I’ll add a note that relocation is disallowed when we can’t attach an event (legacy path).

Noah Patel: Please call that out as a correctness boundary: “compaction requires CUDA event tracking enabled”. If there are fallback codepaths, they should opt-out.

Lena Fischer: Backpressure integration: if fragmentation high and compaction budget exhausted, we may still OOM. What’s the policy? We should ensure admission control triggers before allocator hard OOM.

Omar El-Sayed: Proposed: if frag_ratio > threshold AND free_pages < min_free_pages, we signal scheduler “KV pressure” which tightens admission and can trigger prefix cache eviction first. Compaction is best-effort, not the only safety.

Noah Patel: Good. Make that explicit under “correctness boundaries”: compaction is not relied on for safety; admission control remains primary OOM prevention.

Allison Grant: On rollout/rollback criteria: do we have a hard metric-based rollback? (p99 spike correlated with compaction time)

Omar El-Sayed: Yes, SRE is defining it, but initial suggestion: rollback if p99 decode latency +15% for 10m AND compaction_time_ms_p99 > budget AND eviction_rate > threshold. Still tuning.

Mateo Alvarez: We’ll put exact thresholds in SRE guardrails doc. Design doc should just state: “feature flagged, canary, auto-rollback wired to tail latency + compaction metrics.”

Noah Patel: Last request: tests.
Please add a stress test that hits cancellation during relocation (classic hazard). Even if scheduler sets MOVING only at boundaries, cancellations can happen asynchronously.

Samir Qureshi: Already on the list (ENG-4827). We’ll model: cancel arrives after copy but before mapping swap; and cancel after swap but before freeing old pages.

Noah Patel: Perfect. Summarizing review feedback:
- Write explicit invariants I1-I6 + guarantees G1-G2 in doc
- Define boundary conditions: requires per-seq GPU completion event; opt-out legacy paths
- Clarify vocabulary: eviction=cache artifacts; sequences are never evicted (only admitted/rejected/cancelled)
- Kill-switch semantics: stop selecting new work; finish current step; never publish partial mapping
- Add cancellation stress tests around swap/free window

Once those are in, I’m comfortable with the architecture direction.

Omar El-Sayed: Awesome, thanks. I’ll update the Confluence page + ADR text and ping back for final sign-off.

Noah Patel: Sounds good. :thumbsup:

Allison Grant: +1, please tag me when metric definitions are finalized so we can align names with the standard.

Omar El-Sayed: Will do.

source.content:
Priya Natarajan: Want to sanity check the enforcement point for Optimize quality gates. Where should the “block apply if gates not passed” live? Optimize API vs rollout controller vs both.

Aditya Rao: From product POV we need the one-click Apply in Optimize to be safe-by-default. So Optimize should block it if gates fail / missing.

Paula Mendes: +1 but note: rollout controller can still promote a canary config even if Optimize UI is blocked, depending on how config gets written. We need a clean coupling there too.

Jordan Lee: Threat model: any place that can mutate route config (Optimize auto-apply, one-click, internal tools, maybe manual API) must be gated. If we only gate in rollout controller, someone can “apply” to 100% without a rollout. If we only gate in Optimize, someone can bypass via config service.

Mateo Alvarez: Runtime angle: even if config gets through, we want runtime to reject *known unsafe combos* (e.g. quant profile mismatch for GPU SKU). But quality regressions aren’t always “unsafe” in the strict sense.

Noah Patel: Right. Runtime guardrails are for invariants. Quality gates are contextual, baseline-delta etc. That logic belongs in control-plane.

Chloe Martin: What’s the “source of truth” API for config writes today? Is it Optimize service -> config store? Or rollout controller -> config store?

Ethan Park: Today: Optimize proposes diff; for apply it calls config service directly (with some policy checks). Rollouts is another pathway that writes to the same config service w/ staged traffic allocation.

Jordan Lee: That suggests config service (or policy engine in front of it) is the only place we can guarantee coverage.

Michael Nguyen: But gating requires async evals. Config service is currently synchronous-ish. If we put full gating there it becomes a coordinator/state machine.

Priya Natarajan: We already have a gate-run state machine in Optimize service design. The question is whether config service is “dumb storage” or enforces policy.

Sean Gallagher: From SRE: defense-in-depth. I’d rather have 2 checks than bet on one service. Also: failure mode where rollout controller has a bug and promotes anyway is scary.

Allison Grant: +1. Also want consistent audit events. If enforcement is split, we need consistent logging fields (gate_run_id, decision, actor, override_id).

Paula Mendes: Can rollout controller just require “quality_gate_status=PASSED or OVERRIDDEN” before promoting beyond canary? That’s a simple guard.

Jordan Lee: Yup. But also we need to prevent “apply to 100%” paths that bypass rollouts.

Aditya Rao: For hosted API routes, one-click apply goes through Optimize. Auto-apply too. But we know there are manual config changes sometimes (support / incident). Those would bypass Optimize unless we route them.

Michael Nguyen: We can route all mutations through config service anyway. The question is where does the policy decision happen.

Jordan Lee: Proposal:
1) Optimize service owns gate run orchestration (kick eval-harness, aggregate, store GateRun record).
2) Optimize service enforces at its “apply config” endpoint (block unless GateRun PASSED/OVERRIDDEN).
3) Rollout controller enforces at promotion (block unless GateRun PASSED/OVERRIDDEN for that config version).
4) Config service adds a lightweight check: if request includes `requires_quality_gate=true` then it rejects writes without a gate attestation.

Chloe Martin: “Gate attestation” meaning a signed token / reference id?

Jordan Lee: Doesn’t have to be crypto-signed v0. Could be just `gate_run_id` + server-side lookup w/ service-to-service auth. But we should treat it like an authz decision.

Ethan Park: If config service has to look up gate_run_id, it needs to talk to Optimize DB or a shared store. That’s a coupling.

Michael Nguyen: Alternatively config service just stores `gate_run_id` field on the config revision and policy engine validates in the caller (Optimize/rollout). But then config service can’t stop manual writes.

Sean Gallagher: Manual writes are exactly the “uh oh” path. We can’t rely on humans doing the right thing.

Mateo Alvarez: Another angle: can we force all writes to go through rollout controller for production routes? Then the controller becomes the choke point.

Paula Mendes: We considered that, but Optimize has non-rollout “apply now” for dev/test routes and some low-risk tiers. Also rollout controller is optimized for staged traffic, not for “set caching knob for staging env now”.

Noah Patel: And it still doesn’t handle internal tooling that might write directly.

Priya Natarajan: So seems like:
- Optimize must enforce at its API boundary (DX correctness)
- rollout controller must enforce at promotion (staged safety)
- config service should have *some* enforcement so bypass paths are blocked

Allison Grant: + also suggest a “deny by default” flag at the config schema level for the 3 config types in scope (batching/caching/quant profile). If diff touches those fields, policy requires a gate.

Jordan Lee: Yes. Deterministic rule based on diff. That’s basically ENG-3930 static validation + policy.

Aditya Rao: What about overrides? Needs to work across all three enforcement points.

Jada Williams: Override record should be first-class and time-bound. Then each enforcement point checks `effective_status = PASSED || (OVERRIDDEN && override.expires_at > now)`.

Michael Nguyen: And we should store “applied_under_override=true” on the config revision for audit/search.

Paula Mendes: Rollout controller also needs to halt promotion if override expires mid-rollout.

Sean Gallagher: If override expires mid-rollout, we should freeze at current canary % and page/notify (not auto-rollback unless we decide that policy).

Mateo Alvarez: Runtime should be tolerant: if rollout freezes, runtime keeps serving. But any auto-rollback needs to be cautious to avoid thrash.

Jordan Lee: Re: coupling: I’m ok with config service calling Optimize to validate `gate_run_id` (or reading from shared “gate runs” table) as long as it’s cached + has clear failure behavior.

Ethan Park: Failure behavior matters: if Optimize is down, do we block all config writes? That could hurt incident response.

Sean Gallagher: For production routes: yes, block by default, but allow break-glass path that’s extremely restricted + loud audit. Otherwise we’ll recreate the near-miss we just had.

Allison Grant: Break-glass should emit a distinct audit event + metric (and alert). Not just “override”. Different semantics.

Priya Natarajan: This is converging on defense-in-depth + break-glass. Jordan can you capture as ADR updates?

Jordan Lee: Yep. I’ll update ADR-0231 draft to specify enforcement layers:
- Optimize Apply endpoint hard-block
- Rollout promotion hard-block
- Config service policy check on protected fields requiring gate attestation
- Break-glass role for SRE oncall with mandatory reason + auto-expiration

Aditya Rao: Works for me. For MVP we can implement config-service check as “reject if protected fields changed and no gate_run_id unless break-glass”.

Paula Mendes: And rollout controller only needs a read-only check: it refuses to promote without gate status.

Chloe Martin: One more: we should ensure the UI doesn’t show “Apply” enabled if gate status is stale. Eg: if a new baseline was generated.

Michael Nguyen: We can bind GateRun to a config baseline hash + diff hash. If mismatch, enforcement treats it as NOT_VALID.

Noah Patel: :thumbsup: that also avoids someone reusing a passed gate_run_id for a different diff.

Priya Natarajan: Great. Action items: Jordan ADR update + policy sketch; Paula rollout controller check requirement; Michael/Jada audit fields + override semantics; Sean/Allison define break-glass alerting. End.

source.messages:
Noah Patel: Dropping ADR draft here for final nitpicks before I mark “in review” -> “accepted”: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/x/ADR-override-order

Noah Patel: TL;DR: how we ship the hardware tuning profile pack + override precedence (fleet defaults vs per-pool vs per-deploy vs per-route/customer), plus rollback semantics. Would love eyes from Infra/SRE + anyone who’s been burned by config drift.

Ethan Park: skimming now. first nit: the precedence list in section 3 reads like “later wins” but then section 4 implies “fleet defaults override customer overrides for safety”. those are contradictory.

Ethan Park: can we explicitly state: (a) which layers are allowed to override which keys (key-level allowlist), and (b) whether safety clamps are applied after merge. otherwise readers will assume you can override anything via env var.

Benji Okafor: +1. I also think “env vars highest precedence” is too blunt. We’ve been using env as emergency toggle, but in Dedicated/Private it becomes a footgun (random helm chart sets env, overrides everything silently).

Benji Okafor: Suggest: env vars only for a small set of debug/kill-switch knobs (e.g. DISABLE_PROFILE_PACK, FORCE_PROFILE_ID), not arbitrary scheduler settings.

Diego Santos: from deployment compatibility angle: Hosted will use control-plane pushed config, Dedicated/Private via helm values + config file mount. if we keep “env always wins”, we’re creating different behavior across modes b/c not all modes have env injection in the same place.

Diego Santos: we should define override sources in a way that maps cleanly:
- runtime baked defaults
- packaged profile pack
- external profile bundle (optional)
- deployment config (control plane / helm values)
- request-level overrides (route/customer)
- emergency toggles
and then specify priority.

Noah Patel: good call. I was trying to keep it simple but yeah, it reads inconsistent.

Quentin Harris: another nit: the ADR says “profile version pinned per deployment” but doesn’t say what happens if runtime doesn’t support that profile version.

Quentin Harris: we need to specify compatibility check behavior: fail hard vs warn + fallback. for Private, fail hard might brick a cluster during upgrade; for Hosted, failing hard is scary too.

Neha Kapoor: +1 please don’t “fail hard” by default. SRE preference: startup should be conservative:
- if explicit override requested (FORCE_PROFILE_ID/profile_version pinned) and incompatible -> fail fast (user asked for it)
- if auto-selected profile incompatible -> log loudly + fall back to safe baseline profile (or runtime defaults)

Neha Kapoor: also want rollback semantics called out: “disable profile pack for pool X” should be reversible without redeploy (i.e. control plane flag / config map change). ADR currently reads like “ship inside runtime binary” which makes rollback = new runtime deploy.

Irene Choi: infra side: we can do both, but we have to say what’s default. baked-in pack is good for Private/offline installs, but Hosted needs a way to hotfix profiles without waiting for runtime release train.

Irene Choi: maybe state: “runtime includes last-known-good pack; control plane can optionally mount/serve newer pack; selection chooses external pack if signed + compatible.”

Connor O'Brien: release train concern: if ADR encourages “external bundle can override baked pack”, we need a clear ownership + signoff gate. otherwise teams will bypass runtime release process.

Connor O'Brien: ask: who is allowed to publish external pack versions? Runtime kernels/scheduling only? and do we require perf-canary green on all SKUs before promotion?

Paula Mendes: from canary/rollback requirements: please add explicit “blast radius” notes. e.g.
- default path is “enable new pack in canary pool only”
- rollback trigger thresholds (p95, error rate, OOM) should flip DISABLE_PROFILE_PACK at pool scope
- per-customer override should never be the first rollback lever during incident (too slow)

Marissa Cole: product/UX nit: ADR says “per-route override wins” but we should be careful: customers will expect per-route config to be stable even if fleet defaults change.

Marissa Cole: can we state “customer-visible overrides are sticky + audited” and that we’ll expose “effective profile + source” in console? otherwise Support is going to get murdered.

Benji Okafor: config drift risk: ADR should explicitly mention “normalized defaults” problem.

Benji Okafor: right now we have runtime hard-coded defaults + profile pack defaults. if both exist, people change one and forget the other -> drift. proposal: runtime hard-coded defaults are minimal safe baseline; all tuning lives in pack; runtime logs effective config at boot.

Ethan Park: yes pls. ADR should require:
1) single source of truth for default values (pack)
2) runtime prints “effective config” (or at least key diffs) + profile id/version
3) validation rejects unknown keys (to prevent silent no-ops)

Diego Santos: also “override order” should be deterministic and observable. i.e. metrics tag: selected_profile_id, selected_profile_version, override_source (auto/bundle/control-plane/helm/env).

Neha Kapoor: +1000 on observability. and please call out that we need an oncall-friendly toggle:
- disable profile selection entirely (fallback to baseline)
- disable only external bundle, keep baked-in
- force baseline profile (per pool)

Noah Patel: ok, proposed edits based on this thread:
- rewrite precedence section as an ordered list with “who can override what” (key allowlist + safety clamps applied last)
- split “env vars” into “emergency toggles only”
- add compatibility behavior matrix (explicit override vs auto-select)
- clarify distribution: baked-in LKG pack + optional externally served pack with signoff
- add config drift mitigation requirements: single source of truth + startup logs + effective-config dump + metrics

Noah Patel: I’ll push a revision today and tag you all. Anything else blocking acceptance?

Quentin Harris: one more nit: “per-customer override” should be clearly separated from “per-route override” in ADR language. per-customer might apply across multiple routes + models; per-route is more granular. readers will conflate.

Irene Choi: +1. also mention GPU SKU detection variability (string variants) -> if detection ambiguous, we should prefer baseline rather than guessing and picking wrong profile.

Paula Mendes: not blocking, but please include “roll forward vs rollback” guidance: if pack is bad, disable quickly; if only one knob is bad, prefer hotfix in external bundle + canary before re-enabling.

Connor O'Brien: as long as external bundle path has a clear promotion gate + ownership, I’m good.

Neha Kapoor: same. make sure ADR doesn’t sound like “expected behavior == guarantee” re perf. :thumbsup:

Noah Patel: noted. will update wording to “expected behavior / safe-by-default” and tie guarantees to SLO docs not ADR.

Marissa Cole: +1. also please add a note that console will show “effective profile” but doesn’t promise a fixed latency number.

Noah Patel: cool. pushing update. thanks all.

Irene Choi: +1. also mention GPU SKU detection variability (string variants) -> if detection ambiguous, we should prefer baseline rather than guessing and picking wrong profile.

Paula Mendes: not blocking, but please include “roll forward vs rollback” guidance: if pack is bad, disable quickly; if only one knob is bad, prefer hotfix in external bundle + canary before re-enabling.

Connor O'Brien: as long as external bundle path has a clear promotion gate + ownership, I’m good.

Neha Kapoor: same. make sure ADR doesn’t sound like “expected behavior == guarantee” re perf. :thumbsup:

Noah Patel: noted. will update wording to “expected behavior / safe-by-default” and tie guarantees to SLO docs not ADR.

Marissa Cole: +1. also please add a note that console will show “effective profile” but doesn’t promise a fixed latency number.

Noah Patel: cool. pushing update. thanks all.

source.messages:
Claire Dubois: Dropping ADR-0172 draft here for review (metrics + run manifest schema). Would love eyes on: schema fields, versioning, and how we encode workload params + env capture without making it impossible to diff / store. Doc: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/x/ADR-0172-draft

Claire Dubois: Current proposal is 2 JSON artifacts per run:
1) `run_manifest.json` (inputs + env)
2) `results.json` (metrics + aggregations)

Claire Dubois: Biggest open questions called out in ADR:
- Do we version the schema (semver) vs a date stamp?
- Workload params: flatten vs nested (e.g., sequence length bins / distributions)
- Env capture: how strict is “required” (driver/CUDA, clocks, container digest, git SHA, region, etc.)

Rina Kobayashi: +1 on splitting manifest vs results. For harness it’s way easier to treat manifest as immutable. But we need to be careful that results references manifest by hash or filename, otherwise dashboards get messy.

Allison Grant: Reading. Two immediate comments:
1) metrics definitions need to be explicit about boundary (server-only vs client-observed). We already have confusion in SLO land.
2) env capture: please include “measurement boundary” fields too, not just driver versions.

Sean Gallagher: For env capture, we should distinguish “must for reproducibility” vs “nice to have”. Otherwise CI is going to fail constantly when a field is missing on some runner.

Noah Patel: On schema versioning: I’d prefer `schema_version: 1` + `schema_revision` semver-ish. Date stamp is ambiguous (and people will cargo-cult it).

Mateo Alvarez: Agree w/ Noah. Also: if we’re going to gate releases on this, we need backwards compatibility story. E.g., runtime adds new scheduler knob → manifests should still validate.

Jonas Weber: For workload params: pls keep them structured, not flattened. If we flatten everything into `workload_param_*` it’ll be unreadable. But we should be consistent about naming and units.

Logan Wright: Dashboarding/data pipeline angle: nested is ok as long as we have a stable path for key fields we group by. E.g. `model.name`, `hardware.gpu_sku`, `region`, `workload.id`, `workload.mode` should be top-level or at least fixed paths.

Connor O'Brien: On CI gating: validator should allow “unknown fields” so we can extend without breaking old runners. But we should lock down required fields.

Sahana Iyer: +1 and please keep the schema compatible with GitHub Actions artifacts size constraints. If env capture includes giant dumps it will blow up. (Put big stuff in separate artifacts and reference by URL/path.)

Claire Dubois: ok, let’s go point by point. Starting w/ versioning: ADR currently proposes
- `manifest_schema_version`: "0.1.0" (semver)
- `results_schema_version`: "0.1.0"
- `workload_suite_version`: "2026q1.v1" (string)
Feedback: are we ok with semver strings? Or do we want integer + changelog?

Noah Patel: Semver strings are fine, but define what increments mean. E.g. major = breaking validation/backwards incompatible, minor = new optional fields, patch = doc/typo.

Allison Grant: +1. And define a migration rule: when we bump major, we need a converter script in repo (even if best-effort).

Rina Kobayashi: From harness: semver string is easiest. We can embed it in filenames too (`run_manifest.v0.1.0.json`) but not required.

Sean Gallagher: Please keep schema versions independent from workload suite versions. Workload suite will change often; schema shouldn’t.

Claire Dubois: Next: workload params. Current sketch:
```
"workload": {
  "id": "chat_streaming",
  "variant": "prod_mix_v1",
  "mode": "streaming",
  "seed": 12345,
  "request_mix": {
    "chat": 0.65,
    "tool": 0.20,
    "summarize": 0.10,
    "other": 0.05
  },
  "distributions": {
    "prompt_tokens": {"p50": 180, "p90": 900, "max": 4096},
    "output_tokens": {"p50": 220, "p90": 800, "max": 2048}
  },
  "concurrency": {"target": 64, "ramp_seconds": 90}
}
```
Question: do we allow arbitrary keys under `distributions`? Or do we standardize on bins/histograms only?

Jonas Weber: For representativeness we often need bins, not just p50/p90/max. p50/p90 hides multimodal mixes. I’d propose we support two forms:
- `histogram_bins`: explicit bin edges + weights
- `summary`: p50/p90/max for quick glance

Logan Wright: From pipeline, histogram bins are fine but please keep the structure consistent. Something like:
`"histogram": {"bins": "0-32,33-64,...", "weights": "0.1,0.2,..."}` is ugly. Better:
```
"prompt_tokens": {
  "type": "histogram",
  "bins": "0-64;65-128;129-256",
  "weights": "0.12;0.34;0.54"
}
```
But I know ADR wants JSON not CSV strings…

Allison Grant: We can keep arrays in JSON but remember our file generation constraints? (Wait—this is internal, arrays are fine in the manifest itself. The Slack-exported file is separate.)

Rina Kobayashi: +1 arrays are fine in the manifest in GitHub. In this thread we’re just agreeing on structure.

Mateo Alvarez: Standardize what matters to perf: prompt token distribution, output token distribution, and arrival process. Everything else optional.

Sean Gallagher: Also need to specify caching scenario. There was a whole thread about cold vs warm. I’d like explicit:
- `cache_mode: cold|warm_prefix|warm_kv` (or similar)
Otherwise results are incomparable.

Connor O'Brien: +1, and if cache_mode impacts gating thresholds it must be a tag in results.

Claire Dubois: Great call. ADR has a footnote but not explicit field. Adding `cache_mode`.

Claire Dubois: Now env capture. Proposed required:
- runtime git SHA + build id
- container image digest
- region + cluster
- GPU sku + count
- driver version
- CUDA version
- runtime flags (scheduler policy id)

Optional:
- GPU clocks locked? (yes/no + value)
- NUMA / CPU pinning
- kernel selection dump path
- tracing artifacts paths

Sean Gallagher: I’d push GPU clocks + power settings into “required” for baseline runs at least. Otherwise variance will be huge and we’ll waste weeks. It can be optional for developer local runs.

Sahana Iyer: How do we encode “required for baseline runs” vs “optional for local”? maybe `run_type: baseline|canary|local` and validator enforces more fields based on run_type.

Allison Grant: Please don’t make validator super complex. But we can do conditional requirements with JSON schema `if/then`. That’s acceptable.

Noah Patel: +1. Keep it simple: `run_intent: baseline` triggers strictness.

Rina Kobayashi: I can implement conditional schema checks in the validator easily (we’re using ajv). We already do this for “streaming requires stream metrics”.

Jonas Weber: Also for env capture: include model config that affects quality/perf: quantization profile, max context, tensor parallel degree, dtype, kv cache dtype, etc.

Mateo Alvarez: +1 and scheduler knobs. But we need a line somewhere; otherwise manifest becomes a dump of every config option. Maybe:
- `config_fingerprint`: hash of full config
- `config_effective`: include only the subset that impacts perf (documented)

Logan Wright: Hash is helpful for de-duping. But dashboards need human-readable fields. So include both hash + a curated set.

Claire Dubois: ok. Drafting: `effective_config` section with curated keys + `full_config_ref` as artifact link.

Allison Grant: Make sure the curated keys include measurement boundary, like:
- `ttft_definition`: `server_first_token_generated` vs `first_byte_on_wire`
- `tpot_definition`: `server_token_interval` (exclude client read)
If we don’t record that, people will “fix” bugs and accidentally change definitions.

Rina Kobayashi: +1. In harness we can set those constants and write them into manifest.

Connor O'Brien: For results: propose we output both raw samples (maybe capped) + aggregated percentiles? Or only percentiles?

Sean Gallagher: For gating, we need percentiles. For triage, raw samples help a lot (to see multimodal). But raw samples get big.

Sahana Iyer: Could do: results has percentiles + a separate `samples.parquet` artifact. Results includes URL. Keep JSON small.

Claire Dubois: Works. ADR already suggests results JSON should be <= ~1MB.

Noah Patel: Another subtlety: multi-model fallback. If the runtime falls back to another kernel/model variant, we should record that. Otherwise baseline might accidentally include fallback path.

Mateo Alvarez: That’s in PR-21519 (kernel selection dump). In manifest we can include `kernel_selection: {"mode": "record", "sample_rate": 0.05}` + results include summary counts.

Rina Kobayashi: I’ll add a results field like `kernel_fallback_rate` and `top_fallback_reasons`. That will help catch silent regressions.

Allison Grant: Careful: “top reasons” is a list. But for JSON results, list is fine. For dashboards we might need it as tags? Might be too high-cardinality. Maybe keep it as a drilldown link.

Logan Wright: +1. High-cardinality reasons will explode metrics. Put detailed reasons in artifact, keep a single scalar in results (e.g., fallback_rate) and a boolean `fallback_occurred`.

Claire Dubois: Summarizing decisions from this thread:
- Versioning: semver strings for manifest/results schema. Define major/minor/patch semantics + provide converter for major bumps.
- Workload params: structured `workload` object; support histograms (bins + weights) AND summary stats; add explicit `cache_mode`.
- Env capture: add `run_intent` to allow conditional required fields; baseline intent requires clock/power settings.
- Config: include curated `effective_config` + `config_fingerprint` + reference to full config artifact.
- Measurement boundary: record TTFT/TPOT definitions in manifest.
- Results: percentiles in JSON; raw samples in separate artifact.

Claire Dubois: I’ll update ADR text + schema sketch accordingly today. If anyone objects, please reply in-thread by EOD tomorrow.

Sean Gallagher: Looks good. Please ping me when you add the baseline strictness part, I want to make sure we capture the exact fields SRE cares about (clocks, placement, isolation).

Allison Grant: +1. Also add a short section in ADR for “metrics tag cardinality guidelines” so people don’t try to put fallback reasons as tags.

Rina Kobayashi: ack. I’ll take an action item to update validator once ADR lands. (PR-21432 will need a follow-up.)

Connor O'Brien: Thanks. Once schema is stable-ish, I’ll wire the version fields into perf-canary gating config so we can refuse unknown majors.

Claire Dubois: Perfect. Closing loop after edits.

Mateo Alvarez: +1 and scheduler knobs. But we need a line somewhere; otherwise manifest becomes a dump of every config option. Maybe:
- `config_fingerprint`: hash of full config
- `config_effective`: include only the subset that impacts perf (documented)

Logan Wright: Hash is helpful for de-duping. But dashboards need human-readable fields. So include both hash + a curated set.

Claire Dubois: ok. Drafting: `effective_config` section with curated keys + `full_config_ref` as artifact link.

Allison Grant: Make sure the curated keys include measurement boundary, like:
- `ttft_definition`: `server_first_token_generated` vs `first_byte_on_wire`
- `tpot_definition`: `server_token_interval` (exclude client read)
If we don’t record that, people will “fix” bugs and accidentally change definitions.

Rina Kobayashi: +1. In harness we can set those constants and write them into manifest.

Connor O'Brien: For results: propose we output both raw samples (maybe capped) + aggregated percentiles? Or only percentiles?

Sean Gallagher: For gating, we need percentiles. For triage, raw samples help a lot (to see multimodal). But raw samples get big.

Sahana Iyer: Could do: results has percentiles + a separate `samples.parquet` artifact. Results includes URL. Keep JSON small.

Claire Dubois: Works. ADR already suggests results JSON should be <= ~1MB.

Noah Patel: Another subtlety: multi-model fallback. If the runtime falls back to another kernel/model variant, we should record that. Otherwise baseline might accidentally include fallback path.

Mateo Alvarez: That’s in PR-21519 (kernel selection dump). In manifest we can include `kernel_selection: {"mode": "record", "sample_rate": 0.05}` + results include summary counts.

Rina Kobayashi: I’ll add a results field like `kernel_fallback_rate` and `top_fallback_reasons`. That will help catch silent regressions.

Allison Grant: Careful: “top reasons” is a list. But for JSON results, list is fine. For dashboards we might need it as tags? Might be too high-cardinality. Maybe keep it as a drilldown link.

Logan Wright: +1. High-cardinality reasons will explode metrics. Put detailed reasons in artifact, keep a single scalar in results (e.g., fallback_rate) and a boolean `fallback_occurred`.

Claire Dubois: Summarizing decisions from this thread:
- Versioning: semver strings for manifest/results schema. Define major/minor/patch semantics + provide converter for major bumps.
- Workload params: structured `workload` object; support histograms (bins + weights) AND summary stats; add explicit `cache_mode`.
- Env capture: add `run_intent` to allow conditional required fields; baseline intent requires clock/power settings.
- Config: include curated `effective_config` + `config_fingerprint` + reference to full config artifact.
- Measurement boundary: record TTFT/TPOT definitions in manifest.
- Results: percentiles in JSON; raw samples in separate artifact.

Claire Dubois: I’ll update ADR text + schema sketch accordingly today. If anyone objects, please reply in-thread by EOD tomorrow.

Sean Gallagher: Looks good. Please ping me when you add the baseline strictness part, I want to make sure we capture the exact fields SRE cares about (clocks, placement, isolation).

Allison Grant: +1. Also add a short section in ADR for “metrics tag cardinality guidelines” so people don’t try to put fallback reasons as tags.

Rina Kobayashi: ack. I’ll take an action item to update validator once ADR lands. (PR-21432 will need a follow-up.)

Connor O'Brien: Thanks. Once schema is stable-ish, I’ll wire the version fields into perf-canary gating config so we can refuse unknown majors.

Claire Dubois: Perfect. Closing loop after edits.

source.text:
Ravi Menon: dropping the WIP ADR/design link here for review (enforcement points + policy eval contract). would love eyes esp on trade-offs + auditability: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/x/hot-route-capacity-protection-adr-wip

Ravi Menon: key question: where do we enforce tier-aware protection? API gateway vs routing service vs runtime scheduler (or layered). constraints: low latency overhead, deterministic/explainable decisions for enterprise escalations, no new cascading failure mode.

Ethan Park: from control plane view, I’m leaning layered:
- gateway: static-ish rate limits/quotas + consistent 429 schema
- routing: admission control when we have fleet pressure signals (queue depth / gpu saturation)
- runtime: last-ditch backpressure to avoid total collapse
biggest risk is adding per-request policy network hop.

Vikram Singh: +1 on layered, but we need to be super clear what executes on the hot path. if gateway calls policy-engine over RPC per request, we’re dead.
what’s the expected overhead target? i’d say <= ~1ms p50 added, and importantly no p99 blowups under pressure.

Jordan Lee: policy eval plan in the draft is “local eval w/ cached policy snapshot”, not synchronous RPC.
- control plane publishes policy bundles (signed) to gateway + routing
- deterministic evaluation in-process
- decision log emitted async (sampling configurable)

auditability: we can guarantee “same inputs => same output” via versioned bundles + stable rules.

Amara Diallo: from gateway side: yes please keep it in-process. we can load the bundle into the existing limiter module.
also we need consistent 429 reasons:
- quota exceeded (window)
- rate limit exceeded (burst)
- overload shedding (admission)
- upstream backpressure (runtime)
if we mix 429/503 without clear semantics, support is going to hate us.

Allison Grant: on metrics/tags: do NOT tag by customer_id in the primary counters. we already got burned w cardinality.
route_group + tier + decision_reason + region should be enough for dashboards.
for audit, log the customer/tenant details in a separate sampled structured log stream (or on-demand query) rather than in metrics.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: also: tier attribution can’t come from client headers. needs to be derived from auth context (API key -> tenant -> contract tier) and then passed through as signed internal context.
if routing is doing admission based on tier, make sure it can’t be spoofed.

Peter Holtz: concern w “routing does admission”: when routing is degraded (or policy bundle stale), do we fail open or fail closed?
for enterprise SLO protection, i’d rather fail closed for best-effort and fail open for enterprise (i.e., preserve enterprise capacity even if policy eval is busted).
need explicit fallback behavior in ADR.

Mina Haddad: routing angle:
- we can gate best-effort first once queue depth crosses threshold
- we can also redirect best-effort to alternate pools/regions (when allowed)
- enterprise routes: prefer strict admission vs silent fallback unless product signs off (quality changes)
question: do we allow model fallback for /v1/chat/completions enterprise? or only for embeddings/rerank?

Rafael Mendes: SRE asks:
1) how do we debug “why was I 429’d” on-call? need a clear reason code + linkable decision context.
2) if admission control flips on due to pressure, we need an event + annotation on dashboards.
3) runbook should say: check queue depth, shed rate by tier, and which component is shedding (gateway vs routing vs runtime).

Sofia Ivanova: runtime perspective: runtime backpressure is necessary but we should avoid making it the primary enforcement. by the time runtime sees it, latency is already bad.
we can export scheduler_lag + queue_depth and also expose a “reject new work” signal upstream, but that needs to be coarse to avoid thrash.

Vikram Singh: +1. runtime shedding should be the circuit breaker, not the policy engine.
also: please define “capacity signal contract” (names, update cadence, smoothing). if routing is reading a noisy signal, we’ll oscillate and create weird tail latency.

Jordan Lee: auditability details:
- every decision includes: policy_bundle_version, route_group_id, tier, decision (allow/throttle/shed), reason_code
- include a “decision_id” hash over inputs so support can reference it
- store full input tuple in sampled logs only (request_id, tenant_id, route, model, limits)

Amara Diallo: for headers in 429, proposal:
- `x-redwood-retry-after-ms`
- `x-redwood-limit-reason` (enum)
- `x-redwood-policy-version`
body includes same fields + human copy.
need to confirm if we can return retry-after for overload shedding (sometimes answer is “don’t retry immediately”).

Allison Grant: pls avoid per-request “retry-after” that changes wildly; we should bucket/smooth it. otherwise clients retry in lockstep.
maybe return `retry-after` only for quota/rate limits; for overload return a fixed backoff suggestion.

Peter Holtz: on fail open/closed: i want crisp:
- if policy bundle missing/invalid: gateway defaults to existing global limits (safe), routing defaults to “protect enterprise” (shed best-effort earlier)
- if capacity signals missing: routing should degrade to static per-tier budgets, no fancy shaping.

Benji Okafor: rollout/ops notes:
- feature flag at gateway + routing separately
- allowlist enterprise tenants first
- we need rollback switch that *immediately* disables routing admission (since that’s most likely to surprise customers)
- policy bundle version should be pinned per region so we can roll forward/back.

Mina Haddad: question on latency overhead: if gateway does quota + rate check + policy eval, what’s the per-request CPU cost? we should benchmark.
Vikram can we get an agreed budget? (i’ll add to design acceptance criteria)

Vikram Singh: budget suggestion:
- added gateway overhead: p50 < 0.5ms, p99 < 2ms
- routing admission check: should be constant-time and in-process (no extra store read per request)
anything requiring redis roundtrips is out.

Ravi Menon: this is super helpful. i’ll update ADR with:
- layered enforcement (gateway quotas/rate + routing admission + runtime circuit breaker)
- explicit fail open/closed behavior (protect enterprise by default)
- audit log vs metrics cardinality split
- latency budgets + “no synchronous RPC” rule

Rafael Mendes: can we also add an internal debug endpoint spec somewhere? on-call will need a way to ask “given tenant X + route Y, what would policy do?” without tailing logs for an hour.

Jordan Lee: we have a draft for a restricted “policy explain” endpoint (admin-only). will add to ADR as follow-up item; needs authz + audit trail.

Amara Diallo: +1. support asks for “explain 429” all the time. even a console-only tool would help.

Allison Grant: make sure any explain endpoint is rate limited + audited; we don’t want it to become another hot path.

Ravi Menon: action items i’m capturing:
- (Ravi) update ADR w the above + post in #architecture for final sign-off
- (Jordan) add concrete audit fields + sampling guidance
- (Amara) propose final 429 reason codes + headers
- (Allison/Rafael) clarify metrics vs logs + dashboard annotations

Ravi Menon: will repost revised draft tomorrow AM.

source.content:
Dylan Brooks: Dropping ADR draft for review: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ARCH/ADR-0147+Offline+artifact+signing+scheme

Dylan Brooks: Key bits I want eyes on today:
- signature envelope choice (embedded vs detached, format)
- canonicalization for manifest
- compat/migration from unsigned bundles

Jordan Lee: Read through. I like the direction (sign manifest, manifest carries hashes for everything). I’m still worried about JSON canonicalization being a footgun.

Marcus Lin: +1. If we’re going to sign “manifest.json”, we need to be very explicit that we sign a canonical byte representation, not “whatever json.Marshal outputs today”.

Connor O'Brien: from release eng POV: easiest is detached signature file sitting next to manifest. e.g.
- manifest.json
- manifest.sig
- signing-key-id.txt (or key id inside sig)

Connor O'Brien: if we embed signature inside the manifest, people will reserialize and break it when inspecting/modifying. Detached avoids that.

Ethan Park: installer side: detached is easier too. We can validate manifest.json bytes exactly as shipped, then validate hashes referenced in it. If signature is embedded, we have to do “manifest minus signature field” which gets messy.

Jordan Lee: ADR currently says “JSON manifest is canonicalized using JCS (RFC 8785) and signed; signature is stored as detached file manifest.sig with envelope containing alg + keyid + signature bytes”. That seems ok, but:
- are we confident we can implement RFC 8785 consistently in Go + Rust (installer has components in both)?

Ethan Park: For installer we’re mostly Go, but we do have a small Rust lib in the verifier crate. I’d rather not write our own canonicalizer.

Marcus Lin: If we pick JCS, we should pick libraries with test vectors and add golden tests in CI. Also explicitly state: no floats, no NaN, no duplicate keys.

Allison Grant: +1 on no floats. Also specify UTF-8 only, no BOM. We’ve been burned by “manifest.json saved by windows tooling” before.

Connor O'Brien: Another idea: sign the raw bytes of manifest.json as included in the tarball/zip. That avoids canonicalization entirely.

Jordan Lee: But then determinism across builds suffers unless manifest generation itself is deterministic. Still doable, but the signature would be tied to exact serialization, not semantic content.

Marcus Lin: Signing raw bytes is fine if:
1) we guarantee our bundle builder produces deterministic manifest bytes (stable ordering, stable whitespace, stable newline)
2) we never “pretty print” in one place and “compact” in another

Marcus Lin: Canonicalization is mainly to make signature stable across different serializers, but if we own the producer, we can just enforce an exact format.

Connor O'Brien: +100. We own the producer (CI build step). For air-gapped customers, the only requirement is verifier can reproduce the same bytes that were signed. It reads file off disk.

Ethan Park: raw bytes approach is simplest in verifier: read manifest.json bytes, verify signature, then parse json into struct for checks. No canonicalization in verifier.

Jordan Lee: ok but then ADR needs to say: manifest.json must be emitted in a deterministic format.

Jordan Lee: e.g. canonical json generation rules:
- keys sorted lexicographically
- no insignificant whitespace
- newline at EOF (or explicitly no newline)

Connor O'Brien: We can do “compact JSON, sorted keys, UTF-8, LF newline, no trailing spaces” and lock it.

Hanae Suzuki: If we avoid certs/PKI, we avoid time validity issues too. ADR mentions x509 cert chain as option; please don’t. Air-gapped envs + time skew = support nightmare.

Marcus Lin: Agree. For offline, prefer Ed25519 public key pinned in installer trust store. No notBefore/notAfter semantics.

Ethan Park: For key rotation we still need key IDs + trust store update flow. But we can avoid “cert not yet valid” class of failures.

Allison Grant: Envelope format question: are we doing a JSON envelope for signature metadata? Like:
```
{"keyid":"redwood-release-2026q1","alg":"ed25519","sig":"base64..."}
```

Jordan Lee: ADR draft currently uses a small JSON envelope (call it "sig.json") rather than a raw signature blob.

Connor O'Brien: I’m ok with that as long as *that* file is also deterministic. But do we need metadata at all? keyid can be derived by trying keys.

Marcus Lin: Metadata is helpful for audit + debuggability. Also lets us hard-fail on unknown alg rather than guessing.

Ethan Park: +1 metadata. In preflight output we want:
- signing key id
- manifest digest
- verification result

Ethan Park: makes support bundles way easier.

Jordan Lee: If we do JSON envelope, we’re back to canonicalization for envelope though.

Connor O'Brien: Envelope file can be very small and we can also sign the manifest, not the envelope. The envelope just carries signature bytes. But then attacker could change keyid/alg fields. Not critical if verifier ignores them.

Marcus Lin: Better pattern: envelope is what’s signed *over* manifest bytes? Hmm.

Marcus Lin: I think simplest secure approach:
- signature is computed over manifest bytes
- signature file contains (keyid, alg, signature)
- verifier checks: alg allowed, keyid exists in trust store, verify(signature, manifest_bytes)
- keyid/alg are not “trusted” until signature verifies anyway

Jordan Lee: That’s fine. The metadata is used only after verification.

Allison Grant: Also please document exact base64 variant (std vs url-safe, padded vs not). People will implement parsers wrong otherwise.

Jordan Lee: good catch. we’ll specify RFC 4648 standard base64 w/ padding.

Connor O'Brien: Compatibility: ADR says installers >= X will require signatures for bundles >= Y. We need a clean cutoff.

Ethan Park: installer can:
- accept unsigned bundles only with `--break-glass-unsigned-bundle` and emit scary audit event
- by default, fail closed

Marcus Lin: The ADR should state default is fail-closed on unsigned for *new* bundle format. For legacy bundles, we can allow a short migration window but we need a clear GA policy.

Dylan Brooks: ok, so action items:
1) revise ADR to remove JCS dependency; instead specify deterministic manifest.json serialization by the bundle builder
2) prefer Ed25519 pinned keys (no x509 chain)
3) keep detached signature file w/ small JSON envelope and strict encoding rules
4) update migration section: unsigned only via break-glass + audit warning

Connor O'Brien: + add rationale for detached signature: avoids self-referential signing and prevents “customer reserialize breaks signature” issues.

Hanae Suzuki: + add note about key IDs + rotation (trust store can contain multiple keys). We’ll need that for the installer story.

Jordan Lee: I’ll push ADR edits today and re-request review. Also will add explicit format spec:
- manifest.json: deterministic compact JSON, sorted keys
- manifest.sig: JSON envelope, compact, sorted keys, LF

Marcus Lin: Please add a couple threat-model notes in ADR too:
- replay/downgrade handled by signed version + installer state
- tamper detection via hashes in manifest
- unknown key = fail closed

Ethan Park: :thumbsup: once ADR updates, we’ll align preflight UX + error messages to match.

Dylan Brooks: cool. I’ll mark this thread resolved once updated ADR is up. Thanks all.

source.text:
Rafael Mendes: pulling in #architecture folks for a decision we keep circling. where does the source-of-truth for the public status page component registry live? (component ids, display names, owner, mapping rules, rollout flags). options: repo config vs Confluence page.

Christine Wong: +1. current pain is drift: confluence has “proposed” list, incident-bot has a partial copy, dashboards use slightly diff names.

Irene Choi: repo wins by default for “source of truth” if it’s consumed by tooling. confluence should be a rendered view, not authoritative.

Allison Grant: agree in principle. from observability side: we need the registry to drive dashboard generation + alert labels. we can validate in CI if it’s in git.

Miles Thornton: if it’s in Confluence, the only “validation” is social. also harder to roll back a bad change mid-incident.

Rafael Mendes: concerns I’ve heard:
- oncall needs an easy way to see/edit component list
- product/docs want a human-readable canonical description
- security/legal wants review for anything that changes customer-facing language

Ethan Park: oncall editing in Confluence sounds nice until it breaks automation. we should not be editing customer-facing components during an incident anyway (other than status updates). component taxonomy changes should be planned.

Dev Patel: from Support POV, the main thing is: if the public labels change, support macros and customer comms need to match. having it in git is fine as long as there’s an approval step / heads up.

Logan Wright: if we put it in repo, we can generate the confluence page from it (or at least link it). but the source should be machine-readable.

Christine Wong: what repo? incident-bot repo? observability-pack? main redwood mono?

Allison Grant: prefer a neutral/shared place. observability-pack already has dashboard config + the schema work in flight. also ties to the “signals driving status” standard.

Devon Price: incident-bot just consumes it. i’d rather not host the registry there.

Mina Haddad: needs to be accessible to routing/region taxonomy too. if it’s in observability-pack, is that okay for runtime/platform folks?

Irene Choi: yes if we do proper CODEOWNERS and reviews. we already have cross-team config in repos.

Rafael Mendes: proposal:
1) source-of-truth registry in git (observability-pack) as `statuspage/component-registry.json`
2) JSON schema + CI validation (ids stable, required fields present, no dup display names per region group, etc)
3) Confluence page becomes “published view” that mirrors the registry + additional narrative, but explicitly NOT canonical

Miles Thornton: +1 and add: keep a changelog section in the file header or a `CHANGELOG.md` so we can answer “when did this component split happen?”

Elliot Price: docs angle: if the definitions are in JSON, we can still have docs that explain “what does Hosted API – US/East mean” and link to the registry. but i don’t want docs to be blocked by code review every time we tweak wording.

Michael Grant: legal concern is not the location, it’s control. if a change modifies customer-facing language (display names, descriptions), we need an approval gate. otherwise we risk inconsistent promises.

Naomi Feldman: security/compliance: +1 to auditability. git gives an audit trail. Confluence edits are harder to lock down and review consistently.

Christine Wong: so maybe separate “immutable ids + owner + mapping” vs “long-form description”? the description field could be optional / lightweight.

Allison Grant: i’d still keep a short description in registry for UI/tooling, but not a marketing blurb. anything more verbose lives in docs/confluence.

Dev Patel: review requirement suggestion:
- any change that adds/removes a component OR changes public display_name requires SRE + Support signoff
- changes to ownership/contact can be lighter weight

Irene Choi: also need infra/platform signoff when mapping rules change (service->component). it can impact paging + dashboards.

Rafael Mendes: ok, let’s be concrete on review rules.
Draft:
- CODEOWNERS for `/statuspage/component-registry.json` includes `@eng-sre` + `@eng-platform` + `@observability`
- if `public_display_name` or `public_grouping` changes: require `@support` reviewer + ping `#support`
- if `mapping_rules` changes: require `@eng-platform` reviewer (and `@incident-bot` as FYI)
- if `description` changes only: regular CODEOWNERS ok, but keep it factual

Miles Thornton: add a “rollout flag” in registry (e.g., `enabled: false` until dashboards + incident-bot are ready). prevents partial launch.

Devon Price: +1. incident-bot can read only enabled components.

Logan Wright: CI checks could include “no enabled component without dashboard link” to avoid half-wired entries.

Christine Wong: how do we handle Confluence? i can update the existing taxonomy page to say “generated from registry” and link to the file + ADR.

Elliot Price: i can work with Wei to adjust wording in templates to refer to component ids consistently. but please don’t make Confluence the place people go to copy/paste names.

Naomi Feldman: request: document who can approve emergency changes and how to revert. i’d rather we never do it, but reality.

Rafael Mendes: emergency changes: treat as normal PR with expedited reviewers. no direct pushes. if Sev0 and we absolutely must correct a mislabel, IC can request fast-track review from oncall SRE + one of (Infra/Obs). but ideally we only change status updates, not taxonomy.

Michael Grant: that’s acceptable. add note: any change that alters customer-facing wording during an active incident needs comms lead awareness.

Allison Grant: we should capture all of this in an ADR so it’s not “Slack law.”

Rafael Mendes: yep, i’ll write ADR-014 today with:
- decision: source-of-truth in git (observability-pack)
- confluence is informational mirror
- review requirements + CODEOWNERS
- rollout + validation strategy

Christine Wong: can we also explicitly state: component IDs never change once enabled? only add new + deprecate old.

Irene Choi: +1. stable ids are key for metrics continuity.

Dev Patel: and deprecation should have a window (e.g., 30 days) so support can update macros.

Miles Thornton: (reactions: :thumbsup: x4)

Rafael Mendes: sounds like consensus: repo as source-of-truth, Confluence as mirror. i’ll post ADR draft in this thread for final comments.

Allison Grant: works. once ADR lands, we can unblock the schema PR + mapping work.

Devon Price: +1 please tag me on ADR. i’ll align incident-bot consumption to new path.

Christine Wong: i’ll update the taxonomy page with a banner once ADR is approved.

Rafael Mendes: closing the loop: target to finalize ADR by EOD tomorrow so ENG-4821 can proceed without ambiguity.

source.text:
Karthik Rao: quick design check-in on rollout metadata schema v2. idea: keep runtime wire payload compact, add an optional signed enrichment blob for private installs and for rich clients. want to discuss backwards compatibility, tie-break rules for overlapping pins, and telemetry probe shape.

Zoe Park: love the signed enrichment idea for Private. quick Q: do we need both expires_at ISO and ttl_seconds? i'd prefer single canonical field to avoid conversions in SDKs.

Karthik Rao: agree we should pick one. proposal: canonical on-wire uses ttl_seconds (int) to keep JSON tiny; control plane can surface expires_at in console/ops UI. SDKs can compute expiration if they want human-readable.

Ben Cho: careful: older SDKs may not read ttl_seconds if field name changes. we should include a tiny compatibility hint: "sv" (schema version) integer. older SDKs ignoring unknown fields will be fine; new SDKs use sv to validate.

Mateo Alvarez: tie-breakers: when two pins overlap, deterministic resolver should be: tenant_override > priority_score > earliest_created_ts > lexical_origin_id. compute priority_score server-side. do not put priority on-wire except optional debug.

Karthik Rao: agreed. we'll keep priority server-computed. but include optional debug fields: origin_id and dbg_priority (int). flagged optional so normal clients skip.

Zoe Park: about enrichment signing: propose we add an optional field "enrichment_sig" with base64(sig) and "enrichment_payload" base64(json) so host-side runtime can verify integrity. make the signing key rotation-friendly by including key_id header.

Ben Cho: sounds good. for hosted we don't require signature but we do version the enrichment so SDKs can tolerate missing enrichment. also note: for Private we must document offline key provisioning flow.

Mateo Alvarez: telemetry: add a lightweight probe called rollout_probe: {last_seen_pin, probe_ts_unix, sdk_semver}. keep it sampled (1%) and tolerant of missing fields. this helps measure adoption and stale pins.

Karthik Rao: nice. on collision resolution, we should expose a deterministic resolver endpoint for debugging: /v1/rollouts/resolve?tenant=... returns the chosen pin + resolution trace (list of short tokens describing reasons). for privacy, trace tokens are short codes, not full texts.

Zoe Park: one more: consider binary compacting for devices with bandwidth constraints (e.g., edge runtimes). we could offer an alternative compact encoding (msgpack/base64) but only behind an accept-encoding header. default remains JSON.

Ben Cho: operational concern: TTL semantics. if sdk ignores ttl_seconds, server must enforce a grace window (default 48h) after ttl expiry but mark pins expired in telemetry. tenants can configure grace. must avoid accidental long-lived pins.

Mateo Alvarez: migration plan idea: rollout schema v2 behind a feature flag. step 1: control plane emits sv=2 alongside sv=1 payloads for 2 weeks; step 2: SDK opt-in parse v2 fields; step 3: default switch. sample telemetry to check >95% SDKs can parse.

deploy-bot: rollout-schema-v2 integration tests: 62/64 cases passed. failures: legacy parser test, signed-enrichment missing-key handling.

Karthik Rao: action list: (a) finalize wire fields: sv, model, variant, pin_mode, ttl_seconds, optional: origin_id, dbg_priority, enrichment_payload, enrichment_sig, probe_sample; (b) write Private signing spec + key rotation + provisioning; (c) implement server resolver-trace endpoint; (d) add grace-window config and default to 48h; (e) telemetry probe sampling plan; (f) doc migration plan and SDK changes.

Zoe Park: I'll draft the signing spec and the provisioning playbook.

Ben Cho: I'll add grace-window server defaults and the resolver endpoint sketch.

Mateo Alvarez: I'll wire up the telemetry probe proto and the migration gating checks.

Karthik Rao: i'll assemble the PR for the wire schema + examples and tag you all. thanks.

Ben Cho: sounds good. for hosted we don't require signature but we do version the enrichment so SDKs can tolerate missing enrichment. also note: for Private we must document offline key provisioning flow.

Mateo Alvarez: telemetry: add a lightweight probe called rollout_probe: {last_seen_pin, probe_ts_unix, sdk_semver}. keep it sampled (1%) and tolerant of missing fields. this helps measure adoption and stale pins.

Karthik Rao: nice. on collision resolution, we should expose a deterministic resolver endpoint for debugging: /v1/rollouts/resolve?tenant=... returns the chosen pin + resolution trace (list of short tokens describing reasons). for privacy, trace tokens are short codes, not full texts.

Zoe Park: one more: consider binary compacting for devices with bandwidth constraints (e.g., edge runtimes). we could offer an alternative compact encoding (msgpack/base64) but only behind an accept-encoding header. default remains JSON.

Ben Cho: operational concern: TTL semantics. if sdk ignores ttl_seconds, server must enforce a grace window (default 48h) after ttl expiry but mark pins expired in telemetry. tenants can configure grace. must avoid accidental long-lived pins.

Mateo Alvarez: migration plan idea: rollout schema v2 behind a feature flag. step 1: control plane emits sv=2 alongside sv=1 payloads for 2 weeks; step 2: SDK opt-in parse v2 fields; step 3: default switch. sample telemetry to check >95% SDKs can parse.

deploy-bot: rollout-schema-v2 integration tests: 62/64 cases passed. failures: legacy parser test, signed-enrichment missing-key handling.

Karthik Rao: action list: (a) finalize wire fields: sv, model, variant, pin_mode, ttl_seconds, optional: origin_id, dbg_priority, enrichment_payload, enrichment_sig, probe_sample; (b) write Private signing spec + key rotation + provisioning; (c) implement server resolver-trace endpoint; (d) add grace-window config and default to 48h; (e) telemetry probe sampling plan; (f) doc migration plan and SDK changes.

Zoe Park: I'll draft the signing spec and the provisioning playbook.

Ben Cho: I'll add grace-window server defaults and the resolver endpoint sketch.

Mateo Alvarez: I'll wire up the telemetry probe proto and the migration gating checks.

Karthik Rao: i'll assemble the PR for the wire schema + examples and tag you all. thanks.

source.text:
Victor Alvarez: Kicking off arch review thread re: VPC endpoints defaults + DNS behavior for the alpha Terraform modules. Main open question: do we enable private DNS on interface endpoints by default, and how does installer validate prerequisites?

Irene Choi: +1. We need something that “just works” for typical enterprise private subnets, but doesn’t silently break when customer has custom DNS / private hosted zones.

Nina Petrova: From the Private installer side: the top failure mode in pilots is ECR pull failing (no endpoint + no NAT) and then people assume it’s “Redwood broken”. We should bias defaults toward endpoints ON.

Marcus Lin: Security angle: prefer endpoints over NAT egress, esp for regulated. But if we do endpoints, we need to be careful about endpoint policies + SG rules so we’re not punching wide holes.

Victor Alvarez: Current proposal (module defaults):
- S3: Gateway endpoint attached to private route tables
- ECR API + ECR DKR: Interface endpoints
- STS: Interface endpoint
- CloudWatch Logs: Interface endpoint
- KMS: Interface endpoint (optional?)
- EC2 + Autoscaling + ELB: optional / depends on if they run EKS private endpoint + node lifecycle needs
- SSM: optional (nice-to-have for ops)

Enable `private_dns_enabled=true` on interface endpoints by default.

Hanae Suzuki: DNS gotchas: enabling private DNS for ECR endpoints can conflict if customer has a private hosted zone for `ecr.<region>.amazonaws.com` (seen in some locked-down shops). But I think default ON is still correct, just document override.

Simran Kaur: In reference architecture doc we say “no public egress required for image pulls” if endpoints exist. That implicitly assumes private DNS is enabled, otherwise resolving `*.dkr.ecr...` still goes public, right?

Victor Alvarez: Yep. Without private DNS, clients need to use the endpoint-specific DNS names (the long `vpce-xxxx` names). Nobody does that for ECR.

Sean Gallagher: Operationally: installer should fail fast if it can’t pull images / reach control plane deps. Don’t wait until half the cluster is up.

Rafael Mendes: Agree. We should bake preflight checks into installer (or separate “network verify” command). Like: “from inside subnet, can you resolve and connect to ECR, STS, S3?”.

Nina Petrova: Small nuance: installer itself runs wherever customer runs it (often a CI runner or a laptop via VPN). So checks that rely on being inside private subnet can be misleading.

Bruno Silva: We can do AWS API checks too though. Like describe endpoints in VPC and validate they exist + private DNS enabled. That doesn’t prove DNS resolution inside VPC, but catches 80%.

Irene Choi: Can we do both?
1) AWS API validation (DescribeVpcEndpoints + attributes)
2) If installer is running on an instance in the VPC (common in enterprise), do runtime DNS/connectivity checks.

Victor Alvarez: That’s what I was thinking.

Marcus Lin: On “private DNS enabled by default”: +1, but we should also validate VPC flags: `enableDnsSupport` and `enableDnsHostnames`. If those are off, private DNS won’t work even if endpoints exist.

Victor Alvarez: Good call. Terraform module can set those on the VPC resource, but if customer is using an existing VPC we need to either enforce or document.

Kira Thompson: Docs request: spell out “if you bring-your-own VPC, you must have enableDnsSupport/Hostnames = true” and “if you have custom DHCP options / on-prem DNS, check resolver rules.” This comes up every discovery.

Simran Kaur: Also EKS private endpoint mode: for private cluster, you still need certain AWS APIs reachable from nodes (ECR/STS/EC2). If we don’t include EC2 endpoint, node bootstrap can break depending on how hard they lock egress.

Nina Petrova: For Redwood Private, we assume EKS (at least in alpha reference arch). Nodes definitely call EC2 APIs during bootstrapping + CNI. If no NAT, you need more endpoints than just ECR.

Victor Alvarez: So maybe the “required” endpoint set for alpha should be bigger:
- ecr.api
- ecr.dkr
- s3 (gateway)
- sts
- logs
- ec2
And “recommended”:
- kms (if KMS-encrypted stuff)
- autoscaling (if managed nodegroups)
- elasticloadbalancing (if using NLB/ALB)
- monitoring (CloudWatch metrics)
- ssm + ssmmessages + ec2messages (for SSM)

Marcus Lin: Careful with “required”: if they allow NAT egress, they can skip endpoints. But for “no public egress” posture, yes, that’s the required set.

Sean Gallagher: Can we encode that as installer output?
- Detect NAT route to IGW? (well, NAT implies route to NAT in private RT)
- If no NAT present, then enforce endpoints set
- If NAT present, warn if endpoints missing but don’t fail

Bruno Silva: NAT detection via AWS APIs is possible (describe route tables for `0.0.0.0/0 -> nat-...`). Not perfect but good.

Rafael Mendes: Another check: verify endpoints are in the same subnets as the cluster nodes (or at least reachable). We’ve seen endpoints created only in one AZ/subnet and then cross-AZ traffic gets weird / costs.

Victor Alvarez: Module default will create interface endpoints in all private subnets (one per AZ). But yes, installer can verify `SubnetIds` covers expected.

Hanae Suzuki: Endpoint SG rules: we should keep it simple. The endpoint ENIs should allow 443 inbound from node SG (or from private subnet CIDRs). Least privilege is nice, but if we get it wrong we strand people.

Marcus Lin: Least privilege suggestion: allow 443 from “cluster nodes SG” + “installer/bastion SG” if provided. Avoid CIDR-wide if we can. But we need an escape hatch.

Irene Choi: For alpha, I’d rather have a working default than ultra-tight SG logic that’s fragile. But we can still do SG-to-SG.

Victor Alvarez: Proposed endpoint SG default:
- Ingress: tcp/443 from `var.allowed_client_security_group_ids` (default: node SG, and optionally a shared “private clients” SG)
- Egress: allow all (AWS endpoints are managed anyway)

Kira Thompson: How do we explain private DNS risk to customers? We should include a doc note:
- If you already manage private hosted zones for `amazonaws.com` names, set `private_dns_enabled=false` and configure Route53 resolver rules accordingly.

Simran Kaur: + link to AWS docs on endpoint private DNS + split-horizon. (People mix it up with “private hosted zone”.)

Nina Petrova: Installer-side: please add explicit error strings. Something like:
"Missing VPC endpoint for ECR (com.amazonaws.<region>.ecr.dkr). If your private subnets have no NAT egress, image pulls will fail. Fix: enable VPC endpoint + private DNS or add NAT/proxy."

Sean Gallagher: Can we include a one-liner command in the error that works from inside cluster?
`nslookup <acct>.dkr.ecr.<region>.amazonaws.com`
and
`curl -I https://api.ecr.<region>.amazonaws.com`

Bruno Silva: We can put that into troubleshooting section. Preflight can also run a basic DNS resolution check if it’s inside VPC.

Victor Alvarez: Rough installer preflight pseudo-output:
```
[network] vpc_id=vpc-1234
[network] dns_support=true dns_hostnames=true
[network] nat_detected=false
[network] checking required endpoints (no NAT detected)
  - ecr.api: OK (vpce-aaa, private_dns=true, subnets=3)
  - ecr.dkr: FAIL (not found)
  - sts: OK (vpce-bbb, private_dns=true)
  - logs: OK (vpce-ccc, private_dns=true)
  - s3 gateway: OK (vpce-ddd, rt_associations=3)
Result: FAIL. Missing endpoints: ecr.dkr
```

Marcus Lin: Looks good. Also check endpoint policy is not overly restrictive? Hard to validate generically. Maybe just warn if policy is non-default and we see explicit Deny?

Irene Choi: That might be overreach for alpha. But we can at least surface “endpoint policy present” in debug output.

Rafael Mendes: One more DNS item: if they use on-prem DNS via inbound resolver endpoints, and they forward `amazonaws.com` zones, they can break private DNS. Installer should tell them: “check Route53 resolver rules / DHCP options domain-name-servers.”

Hanae Suzuki: +1. We can’t introspect that fully, but we can add a targeted warning when private DNS is enabled and VPC has custom DHCP options set (domain-name-servers not AmazonProvidedDNS).

Victor Alvarez: Nice. That’s detectable.

Nina Petrova: Action items from this thread:
1) Module defaults: endpoints ON, private DNS ON for interface endpoints, S3 gateway for S3
2) Document override for private DNS + hosted zone conflicts
3) Installer preflight: describe endpoints + privateDnsEnabled + VPC DNS flags + NAT detection; fail if missing required endpoints when no NAT
4) Troubleshooting text for DNS resolution + on-prem resolver forwarding pitfalls

Irene Choi: Works for me. Victor can you capture in ADR comment + ping Bruno (module) + Nina (installer) so we’re aligned?

Victor Alvarez: Yep. I’ll summarize + add to the PR notes. :thumbsup:

Sean Gallagher: + please make sure preflight runs before any expensive provisioning steps. We want fast feedback.

Victor Alvarez: Noted. Closing this out unless more concerns.
