source.content:
Priya Natarajan: Dropping the ADR draft here for the autoscaling bounds work. Main thing I want eyes on: precedence ordering + determinism/auditability requirements. If we agree today, we can mark “proposed -> accepted” and unblock ENG tickets.

Priya Natarajan: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ARCH/ADR+-+Autoscaling+bounds+precedence+and+safety+invariants (draft)

Ethan Park: skimmed. +1 on “clamp desired with hard min/max after computing desired from signals” and explicitly recording pre- and post-clamp.

Jordan Lee: the policy eval section looks right. One nit: can we be explicit that platform defaults are only used when customer bounds unset? I’ve seen folks interpret “defaults” as “we can override customer max in emergencies”.

Vanessa Ortiz: yeah, please make it crystal: max is a hard cap. even “reactive overload” shouldn’t exceed it. if we need to protect the platform, we shed/route elsewhere, not violate the cap.

Rafael Mendes: +1. Also determinism: we need a stable ordering so audit logs match what actually happened (and can be replayed). Suggest we call out:
- compute `requested_desired`
- apply safety floor (min)
- apply safety ceiling (max)
- apply cooldown/dampening
- emit `bounded_desired` + reason codes

Mina Haddad: from routing side: we need a reliable “bounded-at-max” signal so we can degrade (fallback model/region) instead of letting queues grow forever. ADR mentions it but could add that this is the supported mitigation vs exceeding max.

Ravi Menon: Agree with Mina. Also: precedence between predictive vs reactive. I like the draft’s “reactive overrides predictive when saturation/queue indicates predictor is wrong” BUT ensure it’s still deterministic. Maybe add: predictive is an input to the desired calc, not a separate control loop.

Allison Grant: observability callout is good. I’d love a very short list of required fields for decision logging so dashboards can join/aggregate without guessing. E.g. `reason=CLAMPED_MAX`, `reason=COOLDOWN`, `reason=PREDICTION_STALE`.

Benji Okafor: re: Jordan’s nit, I can add a “Precedence” bullet list like:
1) customer hard bounds (if set)
2) platform recommended range (advisory only)
3) platform defaults (only when customer bounds unset)
That might remove ambiguity.

Vanessa Ortiz: :thumbsup: and please add explicit statement: “no automated system may violate customer max; if capacity insufficient to meet min, system enters degraded state + alerts”.

Rafael Mendes: yep, and on unmet min: call out expected behavior. We’ve been burned by silent failure modes. I want:
- metric: `min_unmet`
- alert after N minutes
- audit event “MIN_UNMET_REGION_SHORTAGE”
- runbook link

Priya Natarajan: good points. I’ll add a section on “Min bound cannot be met (region shortage)” + required surfacing.

Jordan Lee: another small thing: “determinism” should include “same inputs => same outputs” AND “record the inputs.” If prediction comes from service, we should record prediction metadata (forecast timestamp + horizon) or we can’t explain decisions later.

Sergio Costa: +1. For dashboards, if we don’t capture `forecast_age_seconds` we’ll end up with “why did it do that?” every time predictions go stale.

Ethan Park: on the “reactive overrides predictive” language — maybe make it: predictor provides a suggested desired, but reactive signals can raise/lower requested_desired within the normal compute step. Then clamp. That’s implementable and keeps audit semantics clean.

Ravi Menon: yup. “arbitration within computeDesired()” vs “override after the fact.” As long as reasons are emitted.

Mina Haddad: and if bounded at max + queue is rising, routing needs a single boolean we can trust. We can integrate with `capacity_state=BOUNDED_MAX`.

Allison Grant: ADR currently mentions “record why” but not a controlled vocabulary. Can we agree on a small enum for reason codes? Otherwise every service will invent strings.

Benji Okafor: I can align with the metric naming doc patterns. For audit event: `decision_reason` as comma-separated stable tokens (no freeform). Example: `PREDICTIVE_INPUT,REACTIVE_SATURATION,CLAMPED_MAX,COOLDOWN_BYPASSED`.

Rafael Mendes: careful w/ comma-separated. but stable tokens is what we need. We can parse it.

Vanessa Ortiz: another edge case: customer sets min=0 accidentally. do we allow? product probably wants guardrails. ADR can say “min=0 allowed only if explicitly set and account is enabled for scale-to-zero” (if we even allow it). Otherwise we risk repeating the null-prediction-to-zero bug.

Priya Natarajan: I don’t want to reopen scope, but we can note: “scale-to-zero is not supported for Dedicated; min=0 rejected (validation)”. That matches what we’ve been telling customers.

Jordan Lee: +1. Put it in validation + invariants.

Ethan Park: ok. Benji, you can update the ADR with:
- explicit precedence list
- determinism: record inputs (forecast metadata) + stable ordering
- unmet-min behavior and degraded state
- “max is never violated” statement
- reason code vocabulary (high level)

Benji Okafor: yep, will push edits today. I’ll tag you all for a final :eyes:.

Priya Natarajan: cool. If no objections after Benji’s update, I’ll mark accepted EOD tomorrow and link it in the epic.

Rafael Mendes: works. I’ll also make sure SRE readiness checklist references the degraded/min-unmet path.

Vanessa Ortiz: :thumbsup: thanks all

Mina Haddad: :thumbsup: 

Allison Grant: :thumbsup:

Ethan Park: Dropping ADR-007 for review: “KMS client wrapper + failure policy” (draft in Confluence). Goal is to standardize timeouts/retries/error taxonomy + circuit breaker hooks across control-plane + data-plane. Would love eyes from Security + SRE + Obs before we call it.

Diego Santos: +1. Specific things we need a decision on: (a) wrapper API surface + where it lives in monorepo, (b) default timeout + max retry budget, (c) what errors are “fail closed” vs “degrade”, (d) caching constraints (esp around key metadata) + eviction.

Marcus Lin: Reading now. At first pass: I like standardization + explicit non-goals. One concern: language around “fallback/degrade” can get misread as “continue without encryption.” We should be super explicit that encrypt/decrypt never proceeds without KMS-backed unwrap (or whatever envelope step) succeeding.

Hanae Suzuki: ^ +1. Also can we tighten the security requirements on IAM scoping? I see “least privilege” referenced but not spelled out. Would be good to link to the baseline IAM standard (or at least note “runtime decrypt role” vs “rotation/admin role” separation).

Sean Gallagher: From SRE side: biggest risk is retry amplification → KMS throttles → we storm ourselves. The ADR needs a hard cap on retries *and* a global budget per request. Also: circuit breaker behavior should be consistent across services so we don’t get partial weirdness.

Allison Grant: Observability: please include required tags/dimensions for metrics + tracing (service, env, region, kms_provider, operation=Decrypt/Encrypt/GenerateDataKey, error_category). If we don’t lock that down now it’ll diverge.

Logan Wright: +1 on tags. Also need clarity: are we emitting latency histograms at wrapper level only, or also per SDK client call? Wrapper-level is fine but we should standardize span names too.

Benji Okafor: One nit: ADR currently says “timeouts default 10s” (I think). That feels high for synchronous request path. For control-plane maybe ok, but data-plane token path can’t stall that long. Maybe two profiles?

Jordan Lee: Re: IAM, we should explicitly require key ARN scoping + actions scoping. For AWS, we should avoid wildcarding `kms:*` (obvious), but also avoid `kms:Decrypt` on all keys in the account. If the wrapper uses key IDs provided by config, we can scope to that key ARN + enforce it in validation.

Ethan Park: Thanks all. A few replies:
- Agree on “never continue unencrypted.” We intended “degrade” = return explicit error to caller / trip breaker, not bypass crypto.
- On two profiles: we were hoping for one default with explicit override for control-plane ops that can tolerate more latency.

Sean Gallagher: Can we codify that as “profiles” in the ADR though? Otherwise teams will just dial up defaults.

Diego Santos: Maybe: wrapper takes a `KmsPolicy` with `class=request_path|background` (or similar). Default `request_path` is strict + low timeout. Background tasks can have larger timeouts.

Nadia Rahman: +1. Also please specify metric namespacing. We’ve been converging on `redwood.kms.*` across services.

Allison Grant: Here’s what I’d like to see in ADR language (minimum):
- `redwood.kms.latency_ms` histogram
- `redwood.kms.errors_total` counter w/ `error_category`
- `redwood.kms.throttles_total`
- all tagged with `service`, `env`, `region`, `provider`, `operation`
- trace span: `kms.<operation>` under the request span

Logan Wright: And log fields: `kms.operation`, `kms.key_arn` (or a redacted/stable identifier), `kms.request_id` (if provider returns one), `kms.error_category`, `kms.http_status` where applicable.

Marcus Lin: Please be careful about logging raw key ARNs in Private deployments. Some customers consider ARNs sensitive-ish (less so than key material but still). Could we log a stable hash + last 6 chars or something? Or make it configurable.

Hanae Suzuki: Agree. Also, for auditability, we need to log *who/what* changed wrapper config (timeouts/retries) and any rotation toggles, but that might be ADR-008 / audit logging PR. Still worth a callout that wrapper config changes are “security-relevant config”.

Benji Okafor: Another design question: caching. ADR says “cache key metadata 5m”. But rotation will bite us if we cache the wrong things. I’d prefer “cache only non-sensitive metadata + respect provider TTL if any,” and for key version resolution we should always allow refresh.

Diego Santos: The intent was: cache *client* objects + auth tokens, not key material; and cache DescribeKey-ish metadata short-lived. But yeah we need to be explicit.

Sean Gallagher: Also: circuit breaker open state should be per-(provider,region,operation) not global for all KMS calls, or else a noisy Encrypt path could take down Decrypt. But maybe we do per-service instance + per operation.

Ethan Park: Proposed changes to ADR based on feedback:
1) Clarify degrade semantics: “fail closed for cryptographic operations; degrade = controlled failure + breaker/backpressure, never bypass envelope.”
2) Add two explicit policy profiles:
   - `request_path`: timeout 2s, max attempts 2 (1 retry), total budget 3s, jittered backoff
   - `background`: timeout 10s, max attempts 4, total budget 30s
   Defaults are `request_path` unless caller opts into background.
3) Add required telemetry conventions (metrics, tags, span naming, log fields) per Allison/Logan.
4) Caching constraints: cache *client* + non-sensitive metadata; never cache plaintext key material; key-version resolution must support refresh and must not pin beyond rotation window.
5) Add IAM callout + link to infra baseline doc; require separate principals for runtime decrypt vs rotation/admin.

Jordan Lee: On (2) I’d like “max attempts 2” to be a hard guardrail for request path; allow override only with explicit “unsafe override” flag that is blocked in prod by default.

Sean Gallagher: +1. Put the guardrail in ADR. Otherwise someone will “temporarily bump retries” during an incident and create an even bigger incident.

Allison Grant: Love the explicit profiles. Also include “retry on which errors” list. We should not retry on AccessDenied / InvalidCiphertext. Retry on throttling + transient 5xx + network timeouts.

Nadia Rahman: We should standardize error taxonomy names. I saw “PermissionDenied” in one section and “AccessDenied” elsewhere.

Diego Santos: ADR draft uses a mix. Let’s pick the canonical set. Suggest: `AccessDenied`, `NotFound`, `Throttling`, `Timeout`, `InvalidCiphertext`, `DependencyUnavailable`, `Unknown`.

Marcus Lin: Works for me. `InvalidCiphertext` should explicitly trigger rollback/dual-read behavior (or at least surface clearly). Not necessarily in this ADR, but mention it’s a “security+correctness critical” signal.

Benji Okafor: For caching: can we add a specific statement: “wrapper will not cache data keys (DEKs) across requests”? I’ve seen teams get cute with that.

Hanae Suzuki: +1. “No cross-request DEK caching.” (KV cache is unrelated; but docs need to avoid confusion.)

Ethan Park: Ack. Will add.

Priya Natarajan: Skimmed. This is the right direction. My bar for approval:
- No ambiguity that encryption/decryption fails closed.
- Retry budget + guardrails called out.
- Clear breaker behavior and blast radius (per operation).
- Telemetry conventions + error taxonomy locked.
If you can roll these into the ADR text today, I’m comfortable with “Approved w/ changes applied” so teams can start implementing.

Ethan Park: Pushed edits to ADR-007 draft (same link). Highlights:
- Added “fail closed” section + examples.
- Added `request_path` vs `background` profiles w/ defaults + prod guardrail note.
- Added retryable/non-retryable table.
- Added circuit breaker scope definition: per (service instance, provider, region, operation).
- Added telemetry spec (metrics/spans/log fields) w/ required tags.
- Added explicit “no cross-request DEK caching” constraint.
Can folks re-check the diff?

Sean Gallagher: Re-checked. Looks good. One small request: explicitly say breaker open returns a typed error category (`DependencyUnavailable`) so callers don’t treat it as `Timeout` and keep retrying above the wrapper.

Diego Santos: +1, we can standardize that wrapper returns `KmsError{category=DependencyUnavailable, retryable=false}` when breaker is open.

Allison Grant: Telemetry section looks good now. Please ensure `error_category` uses the canonical casing we picked (AccessDenied not PermissionDenied). Mentioned in the spec now.

Logan Wright: Span naming is fine. Thanks for adding provider request id to log fields “if available.”

Marcus Lin: Security wording is much better. Thanks for the explicit “never bypass envelope” statement. IAM note/link is sufficient for ADR, with the baseline doc carrying details.

Hanae Suzuki: +1. Also appreciate the “no DEK caching” line.

Benji Okafor: Caching constraints now match what we need. I’m good.

Jordan Lee: Guardrail section is good. “Unsafe override requires prod config allowlist + change ticket” — nice.

Architecture Bot: Review checklist: quorum reached (Security + SRE + Observability + Platform). Pending: final approval signal.

Priya Natarajan: Approved (with the edits as reflected in current ADR draft). Please link the follow-on implementation tickets in the ADR footer once they’re created, and call out the prod-override process in the runbook.

Ethan Park: Amazing, thanks everyone. I’ll mark ADR-007 “Accepted” and wire the implementation links (wrapper lib + metrics + breaker tickets). Also will add a short “prod override” note to the on-call runbook once that’s drafted.

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: please loop SRE when rollout plan is ready. We’ll want canary + dashboard gates.

Allison Grant: :thumbsup: and ping obs when you’re ready to wire dashboards; we can template the panels off the spec.

Marcus Lin: Thanks all. I’ll make sure the key management standard references this ADR so new call sites follow it by default.

source.thread:
Christine Wong: We need to settle admission control layering for hosted overload work. Where do we put the “hard stop” circuit breaker(s): gateway, routing, runtime, or all three? I’m worried about partial brownouts (policy store slow, routing evaluator slow) turning into a retry storm.

Ethan Park: IMO gateway must be first line. If we wait for runtime signals, we’ve already burned CPU in gateway + control plane + routing. Also the gateway is the only place we can consistently return stable semantics (429/503 + Retry-After) across routes.

Sofia Ivanova: +1 on early shed, but runtime needs its own backpressure. Gateway doesn’t see prefill/decode queues or KV cache pressure accurately. If runtime is saturated, accepting at gateway and then timing out is worse.

Mina Haddad: Routing layer complicates it. If gateway makes a decision without seeing “can I fail over to another region/model?”, it may shed requests that could have been served elsewhere. But also, routing is one of the things that can brownout.

Rafael Mendes: SRE view: we need a hierarchy that degrades safely. If any dependency is sick, we should shed *before* the sick dependency becomes a fanout amplifier. Partial brownout failure mode we’ve seen: routing latency spikes -> requests pile up -> clients retry (no Retry-After) -> traffic doubles -> gateway CPU saturated.

Christine Wong: Exactly. Also if policy engine is slow/unavailable, and gateway blocks waiting on policy, we’re dead. If gateway bypasses policy, we might blow tenant limits.

Benji Okafor: Policy eval must be “fast path only” and have safe defaults. We can’t make the critical request path depend on a network hop to policy store. We can do cached tier defaults + last-known-good tenant overrides in gateway memory (or sidecar) with bounded TTL.

Jordan Lee: Agree. We can treat policy fetch like config distribution, not request-time dependency. If config is stale, apply conservative (more restrictive) defaults. That avoids letting a bad brownout turn into “unlimited traffic”.

Allison Grant: For observability: if we have multiple shedding points, we need a clear taxonomy: `shed_layer=gateway|routing|runtime` and `reason=...` with stable cardinality. Otherwise dashboards will be a mess.

Ethan Park: Proposal:
1) Gateway admission always runs.
  - Local token bucket + concurrency caps per tenant/tier/route.
  - Short-circuit on “global breaker open” for region.
2) Routing *only* runs for requests admitted.
  - Routing can open a breaker on downstream region/model health and return a controlled overload response (or fallback).
3) Runtime rejects if its queue depth / saturation says “no”.
  - Runtime returns a typed overload reason; gateway maps to stable error code.

Sofia Ivanova: That works if runtime can reject quickly and consistently. We can implement “admission to scheduler queue” as the boundary. If queue depth > threshold or token/sec saturation > X, reject with `OVERLOADED_RUNTIME_QUEUE`.

Mina Haddad: The loop prevention is the tricky part. If routing tries fallback N times and each target is overloaded, we can accidentally multiply load. Need “at most 1 fallback attempt” and never “bounce” across regions.

Peter Holtz: +1. Also we should not do “fallback on any 5xx”. Only on explicit overload signals (breaker open / shed). Otherwise partial brownout of one runtime looks like generic 500 and routing will thrash.

Rafael Mendes: Retry storm angle: client behavior. If we return 503 without Retry-After, some clients will hammer. We need consistent headers from gateway regardless of where the overload was detected.

Christine Wong: So gateway should be the single egress for responses? Practically some paths bypass gateway (internal) but for hosted API yes. Gateway can add `Retry-After` + `x-redwood-correlation-id` always.

Ethan Park: Yes. We can standardize response mapping in gateway. But runtime also needs to provide a *fast* reject without waiting on slow code paths.

Sofia Ivanova: Runtime cannot rely on “gateway will shed enough”. We’ll still see internal traffic (health checks, perf-canary, partner routes) and bursts that slip past due to cache staleness. Runtime backpressure is non-negotiable.

Benji Okafor: For the policy brownout concern: we should avoid synchronous policy fetches. Config distribution mechanism should push updates. In worst case, config is missing -> apply “tier default” and “deny unknown tenant overrides”. Conservative.

Jordan Lee: One more: if the policy engine itself is down, routing shouldn’t need it. Routing can evaluate fallback rules based on a snapshot too.

Mina Haddad: Routing snapshot is ok but must be bounded. If we’re using stale “region capacity ok” from 5 minutes ago during an incident, we might send traffic into a black hole.

Rafael Mendes: That’s acceptable if routing has *live* health signals that are independent (e.g., runtime exported saturation + recent error rate). “Policy” is config; “health” is live. Don’t mix.

Allison Grant: This is where feedback loops happen. If routing uses “recent 503 overload” as input and then shifts load, you can get oscillations. We should have smoothing / hysteresis. At minimum: breaker open requires sustained overload for N seconds, and close requires M seconds healthy.

Christine Wong: Hysteresis at each layer could add latency before recovery though.

Peter Holtz: Better slow close than flappy close. Flapping breaker = clients retry + routing shifts + runtime churn. We can tune close hysteresis separately.

Ethan Park: Partial brownout scenario: routing service p95 goes from 20ms -> 800ms. What happens?
- Gateway times out routing call?
- Or gateway blocks and queues?

Mina Haddad: Gateway must have a hard budget for routing (e.g., 50ms). If exceeded, treat as “routing unavailable” and either:
(a) return `503 ROUTING_UNAVAILABLE` with Retry-After, OR
(b) bypass routing and go to tenant’s pinned default model/region.

Rafael Mendes: Prefer (b) only if it doesn’t violate tenant isolation/SLA. If a tenant is pinned to us-east and us-east is the problem, bypassing routing might be worse.

Christine Wong: Could do (b) only when there’s a safe pinned target and the target is locally healthy; otherwise (a).

Sofia Ivanova: From runtime side, we can export a “local health” bit that’s cheap: queue depth + memory pressure. Gateway can consult that without calling routing.

Benji Okafor: But “consult” needs to be careful. If gateway calls a runtime health endpoint per request, that’s another dependency. Should be via metrics/streamed signals cached in gateway.

Allison Grant: Exactly. Pull-per-request becomes a DoS vector. Prefer push-based aggregated signals (e.g., per cluster/region) at ~1s cadence.

Jordan Lee: So layering becomes:
- Gateway: local enforcement + cached regional breaker state + cached tier defaults.
- Routing: optional, within strict latency budget.
- Runtime: definitive backpressure at queue admission.

Mina Haddad: And routing fallback only when receiving explicit overload from primary OR when primary breaker open. Not on timeouts.

Rafael Mendes: We also need a guardrail: if gateway starts shedding >X% for Y minutes, page oncall and auto-post to #incidents. (This was requested by oncall folks.)

Christine Wong: Another edge: if gateway sheds due to concurrency caps, and clients retry immediately, we still get storm. So Retry-After values matter. Do we standardize per reason? Like 1s for short spikes, 10s when breaker open?

Ethan Park: We can do:
- token bucket empty / per-tenant limit -> 429, Retry-After: small (e.g., 1-2s) + recommend exponential backoff.
- regional breaker open / runtime saturated -> 503, Retry-After: 5-10s.
We should also include `error.code` in body to help SDKs.

Sofia Ivanova: Caution: if we tell everyone 10s and then all retry at once, you get a thundering herd. Need jitter in SDK guidance. But header can still be a baseline.

Peter Holtz: Also “Retry-After” is seconds or HTTP date. Use seconds. And always set it. Missing header was a big source of bad client behavior previously.

Allison Grant: Metric requirement: count of responses missing Retry-After when `error.class=overload`. Alert on >0. :)

Christine Wong: Ok, summarizing decisions (please object):
- Multi-layer admission control, but with strict responsibilities.
- Gateway = first line + canonical response mapping. No synchronous policy store calls.
- Routing = bounded latency budget; fallback only on explicit overload/breaker signals; 1 attempt max.
- Runtime = definitive backpressure at scheduler admission.
- Hysteresis in breakers to avoid flapping.
- Push/cached health signals, not per-request health calls.

Rafael Mendes: +1. Also add an explicit “routing partial brownout” handling path: if routing budget exceeded, gateway returns `503 ROUTING_UNAVAILABLE` (with Retry-After) rather than timing out to client.

Mina Haddad: +1 but want the bypass-to-pinned behavior as an option for enterprise tenants with strict pinning and known-good default. We can gate by policy.

Benji Okafor: That can be a policy flag. Default off.

Sofia Ivanova: +1. Runtime team will define which signals are “admission critical” vs “diagnostic only” to avoid noisy metrics driving the breaker.

Ethan Park: I’ll draft the ADR update with this layering + failure modes (partial brownout, retry storm, routing loop). Will tag @Christine Wong @Rafael Mendes @Mina Haddad for review.

Allison Grant: Please include required metric dimensions and a note on cardinality guardrails. We’ve been burned.

Christine Wong: Cool. Action items:
- Ethan: ADR draft + gateway routing budget proposal.
- Sofia: runtime admission boundary + error reason mapping.
- Mina/Peter: fallback attempt limit + loop prevention spec.
- Benji/Jordan: config distribution approach + “policy engine brownout safe defaults”.

Rafael Mendes: Adding: we should run a tabletop for “routing brownout + client retries” once canary is on. Will coordinate with SRE.

Christine Wong: :thumbsup: Will link this thread in the doc.

source.messages:
Quentin Harris: Kicking off schema design thread for model metadata v1. Main open questions:
1) source of truth: model registry vs control plane DB?
2) public vs internal fields separation (and how hard we enforce)
3) backwards compat / semver semantics for model “versions” vs “variants”
4) deprecation semantics (announce vs removal vs EOL) esp for Private bundles

Ethan Park: +1. Control plane is currently the “truth” for serving config (routing, availability), registry is the “truth” for catalog (model family, tokenizer, quant profiles). If we pick two truths we’re gonna keep repeating this project.

Michael Nguyen: Agree. Also reminder: migrations are already scary. If we decide registry is truth, control plane should be read-through + cache and only store serving-state (promotions/rollout state) and maybe some denormalized fields.

Dylan Brooks: From Console POV: we need a single API that says “this model version changed” + a stable record to diff. If the diff pulls from 3 places it will be impossible to generate consistent release notes.

Jada Williams: Question: can we define the schema as “registry-owned record” and then have control plane append a separate “deployment availability + rollout eligibility” sub-record keyed by deployment? Otherwise we’re mixing static metadata (tokenizer, compat) with dynamic state (rollout phase, region enablement).

Quentin Harris: That’s the tension. Schema doc I drafted assumes a single record with sections:
- identity (model_id, variant_id, quant_profile)
- version (semver-ish)
- compatibility (tokenizer hash, runtime min version, supported features)
- notes (public/internal)
- lifecycle (status: active/deprecated/removed + dates)
- availability (hosted/dedicated/private flags + region list)
But availability feels like control plane truth.

Diego Santos: Multi-deployment consistency: availability differs by mode (Hosted can be “on” earlier; Private later due to bundle cadence). If registry is global truth, we need per-deployment timelines + “planned” states or we’ll publish notes that look like we shipped in Private when we didn’t.

Naomi Feldman: On public/internal: we need hard enforcement that internal never leaks. I’d prefer explicit fields rather than “same field but redaction rules”. Like `public_summary` vs `internal_summary`, `public_risks` vs `internal_risks`.

Marcus Lin: +1. Compliance angle: we can’t rely on UI filtering alone. We need data-model-level separation + validation in CI.

Sahana Iyer: CI validation idea: JSON Schema enforces that anything under `public.*` is safe. Also enforce that `internal.*` fields are never referenced by the changelog generator’s public template path.

Connor O'Brien: Release eng take: don’t overcomplicate the pipeline. If we have “public/internal” as parallel subtrees, the generator can do:
- public notes feed = only `public.*` plus safe derived fields
- internal feed = union of both
Then we can add a CI check that fails if `public.*` is empty for a promotion event (unless event type is internal-only).

Logan Wright: For eventing: if we want auditability and release notes, we should separate:
- “metadata changed” event (includes schema_hash + diff pointers)
- “version promoted” event (this is the customer-visible moment)
Release notes should attach to promotion, not every edit.

Ethan Park: +1000. Also source of truth: I’m leaning registry is truth for the “static” record. Control plane is truth for “state”, but state should be computed off registry record IDs (not duplicated).

Michael Nguyen: If control plane stores availability/rollout in its DB, then release notes generation must join registry + control plane state at promotion time. That’s fine if we treat promotion as a control plane action.

Quentin Harris: So… propose split:
A) Registry owns ModelMetadata (immutable-ish except new version records)
B) Control plane owns DeploymentState (regions, rollouts, eligibility)
C) Changelog generator consumes events emitted by control plane at promotion time; event references registry record(s) via IDs + schema_hash

Jada Williams: That works as long as the “public vs internal” fields live in registry. Otherwise we’re going to have “internal reasons” in control plane state that leak via events.

Naomi Feldman: Careful: “why promoted/rolled back” is usually internal. Support wants it, customers don’t. We should ensure event has `reason_internal` separate from `reason_public` and generator only uses the public one.

Marcus Lin: Audit logging wants the internal reason though. But audit logs are not the same as customer-visible release notes. We can emit two events:
- audit event (internal, full payload, retained)
- release-note event (public-safe, minimal)
Or one event with strict classification labels.

Olga Petrov: Prefer one event schema with classification per field (e.g. `visibility=public|internal`). But we need to make sure downstream consumers enforce it consistently.

Sahana Iyer: One event with classification is tricky in JSON Schema because it becomes nested/complex fast. Two events is simpler to validate.

Dylan Brooks: Console can handle two feeds. We already have separate RBAC surfaces: customer-facing release notes vs internal admin/audit view. So splitting seems fine.

Diego Santos: Re backwards compatibility: how are we defining “breaking”? For customers pinned to `model@1.2.3`, we should never swap weights/tokenizer under the same version. So version must be content-addressed or we guarantee immutability.

Quentin Harris: Agree. Rule should be:
- `model_version` is immutable once promoted to any environment.
- any change to weights/tokenizer/runtime requirements => new version.
- patch/minor/major semantics: major = behavior/compat change (tokenizer change, output format changes, tool calling schema changes), minor = perf/quality changes within compat envelope, patch = bugfixes/infra-only changes that don’t affect outputs? (though output can still drift with kernel changes).

Jordan Lee: Rollout policy engine needs a machine-readable “risk” and “compat” signal. If we only have human-written notes, we can’t gate rollouts.

Ethan Park: +1. We need fields like:
- `compat.tokenizer_id` + `compat.tokenizer_rev`
- `compat.api_features` (supports_json_mode, supports_tool_calling, etc)
- `rollout.risk_tier` (low/med/high)
- `rollout.eligible_for_auto_promotion` boolean

Jada Williams: But make sure those are public? Risk tier might be ok to publish as “Low/Medium/High risk” but internal criteria shouldn’t show.

Naomi Feldman: Public can have “risk: low/medium/high” + generic explanation. Internal can include “triggered by kernel swap, new kv cache manager, etc”.

Connor O'Brien: Deprecations: we need a standard timeline. Suggest:
- status: active -> deprecated (with `deprecated_at`, `removal_earliest_at`)
- removal: removed (no longer served in Hosted)
- for Private: “supported until bundle N” or “supported through date X”
We should avoid promising exact dates in public notes unless we’re sure.

Olga Petrov: Enterprise asks for minimum 90-day deprecation notice. But reality: security issues can shorten that. We need semantics for “emergency deprecation”.

Marcus Lin: If emergency, we can set `deprecation.reason = security` and allow shorter windows. But again: careful what’s public. “Security update” yes, but not details.

Michael Nguyen: Also: backwards compat constraints for Private bundles. Private customers can be 2-3 versions behind. We must keep metadata for removed models available for audit + offline release notes even if Hosted no longer serves them.

Diego Santos: +1. Registry should retain historical versions forever (or at least very long). Removal means “not available” not “deleted”.

Sahana Iyer: Validation rule we should add: `lifecycle.removed_at` cannot be set without `lifecycle.deprecated_at` unless `deprecation.emergency=true`.

Quentin Harris: Summarizing emerging consensus:
- Registry owns immutable per-version metadata records (including public/internal notes)
- Control plane owns deployment/region/rollout state and emits promotion events referencing registry record IDs
- Two event streams: audit (internal full), release-note (public-safe minimal)
- Versions immutable; semver-ish with clear rules; compatibility + risk fields are machine-readable
- Deprecation semantics: active/deprecated/removed with dates; removal != delete; Private gets additional “bundle mapping” fields

Dylan Brooks: Works for Console. Next: we need to decide exact shape of `public` vs `internal` in schema. I’m in favor of explicit trees: `notes_public`, `notes_internal`.

Naomi Feldman: Please avoid “freeform markdown everywhere”. We’ll need structured fields for templates (summary, impact, action_required, rollback_guidance).

Connor O'Brien: +1. Release notes generator needs stable slots. Suggest public template fields:
- `summary`
- `customer_impact`
- `action_required` (optional)
- `rollback` (optional)
- `references` (docs links)
And internal adds:
- `change_owner`
- `reason_internal`
- `incident_links`
- `eval_run_ids`

Jordan Lee: Also include `compat.replaces` / `fallbacks_to` list for automated fallback policies.

Ethan Park: Ok. Quentin can you capture this in ADR language? Specifically “source of truth and publishing workflow”.

Quentin Harris: Yep, I’ll write up and tag @Ethan Park @Marcus Lin @Naomi Feldman for review.

Marcus Lin: :thumbsup: and please include explicit statement: “No internal-only fields may be present in public artifacts, including docs build outputs.”

Sahana Iyer: I’ll draft the CI checks list for schema validation + redaction tests. If we do two event types, I can add a fixture test ensuring public templates reject internal fields.

Logan Wright: If you need a canonical field list for event classification, I can provide from analytics platform side. Also recommend adding idempotency key: `event_id = hash(model_id, version, action, ts)`.

Michael Nguyen: One more: for “source of truth”, can we state explicitly: control plane can *not* mutate registry metadata, only reference it. Only registry PR flow can add new versions / change metadata.

Ethan Park: Yes please. That’s the only way we avoid a write-write split brain.

Quentin Harris: Cool. I’ll incorporate. Thanks all.

preview:
Quentin Harris: Kicking off schema design thread for model metadata v1. Main open questions:
1) source of truth: model registry vs control plane DB?
2) public vs internal fields separation (and how hard we enforce)
3) backwards compat / semver semantics for model “versions” vs “variants”
4) deprecation semantics (announce vs removal vs EOL) esp for Private bundles

Ethan Park: +1. Control plane is currently the “truth” for serving config (routing, availability), registry is the “truth” for catalog (model family, tokenizer, quant profiles). If we pick two truths we’re gonna keep repeating this project.

Michael Nguyen: Agree. Also reminder: migrations are already scary. If we decide registry is truth, control plane should be read-through + cache and only store serving-state (promotions/rollout state) and maybe some denormalized fields.

Dylan Brooks: From Console POV: we need a single API that says “this model version changed” + a stable record to diff. If the diff pulls from 3 places it will be impossible to generate consistent release notes.

Jada Williams: Question: can we define the schema as “registry-owned record” and then have control plane append a separate “deployment availability + rollout eligibility” sub-record keyed by deployment? Otherwise we’re mixing static metadata (tokenizer, compat) with dynamic state (rollout phase, region enablement).

Quentin Harris: That’s the tension. Schema doc I drafted assumes a single record with sections:
- identity (model_id, variant_id, quant_profile)
- version (semver-ish)
- compatibility (tokenizer hash, runtime min version, supported features)
- notes (public/internal)
- lifecycle (status: active/deprecated/removed + dates)
- availability (hosted/dedicated/private flags + region list)
But availability feels like control plane truth.

Diego Santos: Multi-deployment consistency: availability differs by mode (Hosted can be “on” earlier; Private later due to bundle cadence). If registry is global truth, we need per-deployment timelines + “planned” states or we’ll publish notes that look like we shipped in Private when we didn’t.

Naomi Feldman: On public/internal: we need hard enforcement that internal never leaks. I’d prefer explicit fields rather than “same field but redaction rules”. Like `public_summary` vs `internal_summary`, `public_risks` vs `internal_risks`.

Marcus Lin: +1. Compliance angle: we can’t rely on UI filtering alone. We need data-model-level separation + validation in CI.

Sahana Iyer: CI validation idea: JSON Schema enforces that anything under `public.*` is safe. Also enforce that `internal.*` fields are never referenced by the changelog generator’s public template path.

Connor O'Brien: Release eng take: don’t overcomplicate the pipeline. If we have “public/internal” as parallel subtrees, the generator can do:
- public notes feed = only `public.*` plus safe derived fields
- internal feed = union of both
Then we can add a CI check that fails if `public.*` is empty for a promotion event (unless event type is internal-only).

Logan Wright: For eventing: if we want auditability and release notes, we should separate:
- “metadata changed” event (includes schema_hash + diff pointers)
- “version promoted” event (this is the customer-visible moment)
Release notes should attach to promotion, not every edit.

Ethan Park: +1000. Also source of truth: I’m leaning registry is truth for the “static” record. Control plane is truth for “state”, but state should be computed off registry record IDs (not duplicated).

Michael Nguyen: If control plane stores availability/rollout in its DB, then release notes generation must join registry + control plane state at promotion time. That’s fine if we treat promotion as a control plane action.

Quentin Harris: So… propose split:
A) Registry owns ModelMetadata (immutable-ish except new version records)
B) Control plane owns DeploymentState (regions, rollouts, eligibility)
C) Changelog generator consumes events emitted by control plane at promotion time; event references registry record(s) via IDs + schema_hash

Jada Williams: That works as long as the “public vs internal” fields live in registry. Otherwise we’re going to have “internal reasons” in control plane state that leak via events.

Naomi Feldman: Careful: “why promoted/rolled back” is usually internal. Support wants it, customers don’t. We should ensure event has `reason_internal` separate from `reason_public` and generator only uses the public one.

Marcus Lin: Audit logging wants the internal reason though. But audit logs are not the same as customer-visible release notes. We can emit two events:
- audit event (internal, full payload, retained)
- release-note event (public-safe, minimal)
Or one event with strict classification labels.

Olga Petrov: Prefer one event schema with classification per field (e.g. `visibility=public|internal`). But we need to make sure downstream consumers enforce it consistently.

Sahana Iyer: One event with classification is tricky in JSON Schema because it becomes nested/complex fast. Two events is simpler to validate.

Dylan Brooks: Console can handle two feeds. We already have separate RBAC surfaces: customer-facing release notes vs internal admin/audit view. So splitting seems fine.

Diego Santos: Re backwards compatibility: how are we defining “breaking”? For customers pinned to `model@1.2.3`, we should never swap weights/tokenizer under the same version. So version must be content-addressed or we guarantee immutability.

Quentin Harris: Agree. Rule should be:
- `model_version` is immutable once promoted to any environment.
- any change to weights/tokenizer/runtime requirements => new version.
- patch/minor/major semantics: major = behavior/compat change (tokenizer change, output format changes, tool calling schema changes), minor = perf/quality changes within compat envelope, patch = bugfixes/infra-only changes that don’t affect outputs? (though output can still drift with kernel changes).

Jordan Lee: Rollout policy engine needs a machine-readable “risk” and “compat” signal. If we only have human-written notes, we can’t gate rollouts.

Ethan Park: +1. We need fields like:
- `compat.tokenizer_id` + `compat.tokenizer_rev`
- `compat.api_features` (supports_json_mode, supports_tool_calling, etc)
- `rollout.risk_tier` (low/med/high)
- `rollout.eligible_for_auto_promotion` boolean

Jada Williams: But make sure those are public? Risk tier might be ok to publish as “Low/Medium/High risk” but internal criteria shouldn’t show.

Naomi Feldman: Public can have “risk: low/medium/high” + generic explanation. Internal can include “triggered by kernel swap, new kv cache manager, etc”.

Connor O'Brien: Deprecations: we need a standard timeline. Suggest:
- status: active -> deprecated (with `deprecated_at`, `removal_earliest_at`)
- removal: removed (no longer served in Hosted)
- for Private: “supported until bundle N” or “supported through date X”
We should avoid promising exact dates in public notes unless we’re sure.

Olga Petrov: Enterprise asks for minimum 90-day deprecation notice. But reality: security issues can shorten that. We need semantics for “emergency deprecation”.

Marcus Lin: If emergency, we can set `deprecation.reason = security` and allow shorter windows. But again: careful what’s public. “Security update” yes, but not details.

Diego Santos: +1. Registry should retain historical versions forever (or at least very long). Removal means “not available” not “deleted”.

- `eval_run_ids`

source.messages:
Connor O'Brien: Kicking off ADR review for Runtime LTS branching/versioning. Main question: do we maintain a dedicated `runtime-1.18-lts` branch (with patches), or do tags-only on mainline (e.g., `v1.18.1-lts` pointing at selected commits)? Looking for eyes on tradeoffs: dependency mgmt, long-term maint cost, and customer pinning semantics (Private/Helm).

Noah Patel: Tags-only feels attractive on paper but I’m worried it collapses the “stable baseline” story. If main keeps moving (scheduler/kernels), tags-only means we’re constantly re-validating a new head for every patch.

Mateo Alvarez: +1. LTS promise is “slower-change”. If we cut from main each month, that’s not LTS, that’s just regular release train with a different label.

Sahana Iyer: From CI/build: tags-only is painful for reproducible builds. We need deterministic toolchain + pinned deps + provenance artifacts. If mainline build scripts evolve, an older tag may not be buildable without archaeology. A branch lets us freeze build plumbing for that line.

Sean Gallagher: SRE angle: if a Sev1 hits on an LTS customer, we want minimal diff from last known-good. With tags-only you’re effectively pulling in unrelated deltas just to get the fix, unless you do some elaborate cherry-pick dance anyway.

Rafael Mendes: Also operationally: release branch lets us run separate canary + SLO dashboards scoped to that line. If it’s just tags on main, it gets ambiguous what “line” a customer is on when we’re debugging.

Hiro Tanaka: Kernel side: we already talked about freezing autotune defaults. That’s basically “branch behavior”. Doing it via tags-only means we’re trying to keep incompatible defaults coexisting on main which is a mess.

Sofia Ivanova: Scheduling: same issue. There are heuristics we explicitly *don’t* want in LTS (experimental batching / load shedding tweaks). Branch gives us a clean “no, not in this line” answer.

Devon Price: The only upside I see for tags-only is fewer branches to babysit + less backport conflict resolution. But… we’re still backporting, just not calling it that.

Quentin Harris: Compatibility matrix / packaging: Private installer and Helm need a stable image tag scheme. It’s much clearer if it’s `runtime:1.18.1-lts` built from `runtime-1.18-lts` branch with a corresponding git tag. Tags-only on main is doable but makes provenance and “what changed since last patch” harder to explain.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: Security: branch helps with vulnerability response. We can scope patches to the supported line, document CVEs, and avoid unrelated changes. For audit, “supported branch + signed artifacts + SBOM” is a familiar enterprise pattern.

Talia Benson: Trying to capture: branch = clearer product semantics + stable build/CI config + minimal-diff backports; tags-only = lower overhead but undermines baseline and forces wide diffs over time.

Connor O'Brien: Counterpoint: branch cost. We’ll need to keep dependencies (CUDA toolchain, NCCL, third party libs) pinned and occasionally uplift them for security fixes anyway. Does branch make that worse?

Sahana Iyer: It makes it explicit, not worse. Either way we need patch-level uplift for security. With a branch we can track “toolchain uplift” as an LTS change category and gate it with perf + determinism checks.

Noah Patel: Dependency mgmt detail: runtime has some “generated kernels / codegen outputs” that can drift. If main updates generator, tags-only means backports might require re-running generator with a newer version (non-repro). Branch can freeze generator version and keep outputs stable.

Hiro Tanaka: +1. Also “micro-architecture tuning tables” change fairly often. We can freeze + document override knobs for LTS, rather than chasing main updates.

Sean Gallagher: Maintenance cost question: how many lines are we expecting concurrently? If it’s 1 active LTS (1.18) and maybe a short overlap during cutover (1.19), that’s manageable. If we promise 3 active LTS lines, different story.

Mateo Alvarez: Plan is single active LTS at a time, 12 months support window, overlap for 4–6 weeks during next LTS branch cut. We should write that in ADR to cap the sprawl.

Rafael Mendes: On-call/escalation: branch lets us attach “mandatory patch” flags to a line. For tags-only, there’s a risk customers pin to an older tag and never take the security update because it doesn’t look like a “patch train”.

Devon Price: Tooling: backport labels + cherry-pick bot assumes a target branch. Tags-only would mean cherry-picking into… what exactly? You’d still need a staging branch or release branch to land the patch before tagging.

Connor O'Brien: Good point: our automation tickets (backport bot, release branch CI) basically assume `runtime-*-lts` exists.

Quentin Harris: Customer semantics: Dedicated/Private want to say “we run runtime-1.18-lts, patch 3” and have that map to artifacts, checksums, notes, and support policy. Branch name shows up in internal dashboards too.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: Also aligns with compliance norms: “maintenance branch with security backports” is a thing auditors understand. We can map CVE remediation to a branch and tag.

Noah Patel: So what’s the main argument *against* branch? Mostly backport conflicts and long-term drift. But drift is controlled by policy: only Sev1/Sev2/security, minimal diffs, perf gates.

Sofia Ivanova: Conflicts: we’ll get some, but scheduler has good test coverage now + we’re adding targeted smoke tests on release branches. That should reduce regressions even when conflicts happen.

Sahana Iyer: If we do branch, we need strict rules for merging *from* LTS back to main (or not). I suggest: never merge LTS into main, only cherry-pick from main to LTS. Otherwise we create weird history.

Connor O'Brien: Agree. LTS is downstream only.

Sean Gallagher: Another nuance: perf gate stability. If tags-only, perf gates would have to run on main head for each patch, and you’ll get more variance (different kernels/heuristics). Branch helps keep perf noise down and makes regressions easier to attribute.

Mateo Alvarez: Sounds like consensus: dedicated LTS branch is the right call, with explicit constraints:
- only one active LTS line
- downstream-only cherry-picks
- pinned build/toolchain for reproducibility
- clearly documented dependency uplift policy (security-driven)

Talia Benson: Action items I’m hearing:
1) Connor to update ADR text to explicitly reject tags-only and call out why (baseline stability + build reproducibility + customer pinning).
2) Sahana to add a short section on build determinism and why branch matters.
3) Quentin to review the pinning semantics wording for Private/Helm.

Connor O'Brien: Yup. I’ll push ADR edits today and re-request sign-off. If anyone feels strongly we should keep tags-only as a fallback, speak now.

Devon Price: No objection. Maybe include one line: tags still exist for each patch (`1.18.1-lts`), but they’re cut from the LTS branch.

Connor O'Brien: Good call.

Rafael Mendes: +1 and add “waiver process” reference for perf gates (SRE approval) so people don’t bypass it under pressure.

Connor O'Brien: Noted.

Mateo Alvarez: :thumbsup: Let’s land it. We can revisit if branch maintenance cost explodes, but right now branch is the only way to make the promise real.

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: 

Dr. Aisha Rahman: :thumbsup: Please make sure ADR mentions security OOB patches can be cut directly on LTS branch with tightened approval.

Connor O'Brien: Will do. Thanks all.

title: #architecture: 1741247710
preview:
Connor O'Brien: Kicking off ADR review for Runtime LTS branching/versioning. Main question: do we maintain a dedicated `runtime-1.18-lts` branch (with patches), or do tags-only on mainline (e.g., `v1.18.1-lts` pointing at selected commits)? Looking for eyes on tradeoffs: dependency mgmt, long-term maint cost, and customer pinning semantics (Private/Helm).

Noah Patel: Tags-only feels attractive on paper but I’m worried it collapses the “stable baseline” story. If main keeps moving (scheduler/kernels), tags-only means we’re constantly re-validating a new head for every patch.

Mateo Alvarez: +1. LTS promise is “slower-change”. If we cut from main each month, that’s not LTS, that’s just regular release train with a different label.

Sahana Iyer: From CI/build: tags-only is painful for reproducible builds. We need deterministic toolchain + pinned deps + provenance artifacts. If mainline build scripts evolve, an older tag may not be buildable without archaeology. A branch lets us freeze build plumbing for that line.

Sean Gallagher: SRE angle: if a Sev1 hits on an LTS customer, we want minimal diff from last known-good. With tags-only you’re effectively pulling in unrelated deltas just to get the fix, unless you do some elaborate cherry-pick dance anyway.

Rafael Mendes: Also operationally: release branch lets us run separate canary + SLO dashboards scoped to that line. If it’s just tags on main, it gets ambiguous what “line” a customer is on when we’re debugging.

Hiro Tanaka: Kernel side: we already talked about freezing autotune defaults. That’s basically “branch behavior”. Doing it via tags-only means we’re trying to keep incompatible defaults coexisting on main which is a mess.

Sofia Ivanova: Scheduling: same issue. There are heuristics we explicitly *don’t* want in LTS (experimental batching / load shedding tweaks). Branch gives us a clean “no, not in this line” answer.

Devon Price: The only upside I see for tags-only is fewer branches to babysit + less backport conflict resolution. But… we’re still backporting, just not calling it that.

Quentin Harris: Compatibility matrix / packaging: Private installer and Helm need a stable image tag scheme. It’s much clearer if it’s `runtime:1.18.1-lts` built from `runtime-1.18-lts` branch with a corresponding git tag. Tags-only on main is doable but makes provenance and “what changed since last patch” harder to explain.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: Security: branch helps with vulnerability response. We can scope patches to the supported line, document CVEs, and avoid unrelated changes. For audit, “supported branch + signed artifacts + SBOM” is a familiar enterprise pattern.

Talia Benson: Trying to capture: branch = clearer product semantics + stable build/CI config + minimal-diff backports; tags-only = lower overhead but undermines baseline and forces wide diffs over time.

Connor O'Brien: Counterpoint: branch cost. We’ll need to keep dependencies (CUDA toolchain, NCCL, third party libs) pinned and occasionally uplift them for security fixes anyway. Does branch make that worse?

Sahana Iyer: It makes it explicit, not worse. Either way we need patch-level uplift for security. With a branch we can track “toolchain uplift” as an LTS change category and gate it with perf + determinism checks.

Noah Patel: Dependency mgmt detail: runtime has some “generated kernels / codegen outputs” that can drift. If main updates generator, tags-only means backports might require re-running generator with a newer version (non-repro). Branch can freeze generator version and keep outputs stable.

Hiro Tanaka: +1. Also “micro-architecture tuning tables” change fairly often. We can freeze + document override knobs for LTS, rather than chasing main updates.

Sean Gallagher: Maintenance cost question: how many lines are we expecting concurrently? If it’s 1 active LTS (1.18) and maybe a short overlap during cutover (1.19), that’s manageable. If we promise 3 active LTS lines, different story.

Mateo Alvarez: Plan is single active LTS at a time, 12 months support window, overlap for 4–6 weeks during next LTS branch cut. We should write that in ADR to cap the sprawl.

Rafael Mendes: On-call/escalation: branch lets us attach “mandatory patch” flags to a line. For tags-only, there’s a risk customers pin to an older tag and never take the security update because it doesn’t look like a “patch train”.

Devon Price: Tooling: backport labels + cherry-pick bot assumes a target branch. Tags-only would mean cherry-picking into… what exactly? You’d still need a staging branch or release branch to land the patch before tagging.

Connor O'Brien: Good point: our automation tickets (backport bot, release branch CI) basically assume `runtime-*-lts` exists.

Quentin Harris: Customer semantics: Dedicated/Private want to say “we run runtime-1.18-lts, patch 3” and have that map to artifacts, checksums, notes, and support policy. Branch name shows up in internal dashboards too.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: Also aligns with compliance norms: “maintenance branch with security backports” is a thing auditors understand. We can map CVE remediation to a branch and tag.

Noah Patel: So what’s the main argument *against* branch? Mostly backport conflicts and long-term drift. But drift is controlled by policy: only Sev1/Sev2/security, minimal diffs, perf gates.

Sofia Ivanova: Conflicts: we’ll get some, but scheduler has good test coverage now + we’re adding targeted smoke tests on release branches. That should reduce regressions even when conflicts happen.

Sahana Iyer: If we do branch, we need strict rules for merging *from* LTS back to main (or not). I suggest: never merge LTS into main, only cherry-pick from main to LTS. Otherwise we create weird history.

Connor O'Brien: Agree. LTS is downstream only.

Sean Gallagher: Another nuance: perf gate stability. If tags-only, perf gates would have to run on main head for each patch, and you’ll get more variance (different kernels/heuristics). Branch helps keep perf noise down and makes regressions easier to attribute.

Mateo Alvarez: Sounds like consensus: dedicated LTS branch is the right call, with explicit constraints:
- only one active LTS line
- downstream-only cherry-picks
- pinned build/toolchain for reproducibility
- clearly documented dependency uplift policy (security-driven)

Talia Benson: Action items I’m hearing:
1) Connor to update ADR text to explicitly reject tags-only and call out why (baseline stability + build reproducibility + customer pinning).
2) Sahana to add a short section on build determinism and why branch matters.
3) Quentin to review the pinning semantics wording for Private/Helm.

Connor O'Brien: Yup. I’ll push ADR edits today and re-request sign-off. If anyone feels strongly we should keep tags-only as a fallback, speak now.

Devon Price: No objection. Maybe include one line: tags still exist for each patch (`1.18.1-lts`), but they’re cut from the LTS branch.

Connor O'Brien: Good call.

Rafael Mendes: +1 and add “waiver process” reference for perf gates (SRE approval) so people don’t bypass it under pressure.

Connor O'Brien: Noted.

Mateo Alvarez: :thumbsup: Let’s land it. We can revisit if branch maintenance cost explodes, but right now branch is the only way to make the promise real.

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: 

Dr. Aisha Rahman: :thumbsup: Please make sure ADR mentions security OOB patches can be cut directly on LTS branch with tightened approval.

Connor O'Brien: Will do. Thanks all.
