2) resolve quant setting (enabled? which profile?)
3) compatibility check using selected model variant + hardware class + max_context
4) if incompatible -> set resolution outcome to fallback baseline

Noah Patel: +1. Also sequence length constraints: profiles might be validated only up to N ctx. If request ctx > N, we should fallback rather than attempting to run and risking correctness/perf.

Mateo Alvarez: That should be in compatibility matrix (profile declares `max_context_tokens` or similar). Runtime has the true request ctx at execution time; if request exceeds, runtime must fallback.

Marissa Cole: Product requirement: when fallback happens (incompatibility), customer should see it in Console + be able to alert on it. Otherwise they’ll think they’re saving money but they aren’t.

Dylan Brooks: Console can show “enabled but falling back X%” at route level. Need consistent signal name + reason.

Naomi Feldman: On audit: if kill switch overrides, that should also be a logged event with actor (SRE) and reason (incident ticket link maybe).

Connor O'Brien: We can do that. Also would like a “runbook” link in ADR so we’re aligned. I’ll drop the existing rollback doc once it’s merged.

Talia Benson: Action items before review:
- Priya/Jordan/Ravi: propose final decision on alias vs pinned profile id (sounds pinned)
- Noah/Mateo: propose runtime last-resort fallback behavior + metrics
- Naomi/Marcus: confirm RBAC/audit requirements (who can toggle, what gets logged)
- Dylan/Chloe: confirm Console flow: preview -> write pinned id -> show warnings

Priya Natarajan: I’ll add comments in ADR today.

Jordan Lee: I’ll comment with determinism language + avoid alias resolution at request time.

Noah Patel: I’ll add a section to ADR for runtime responsibilities vs policy responsibilities, and the “no re-query” rule.

Marcus Lin: I’ll add security notes. One ask: ensure profile artifacts are integrity checked (signing / checksum) and failures trigger fallback.

Talia Benson: Great. See you Thu unless conflicts. :thumbsup:

(DeployBot): Reminder: Architecture Review "Quantization Profile Enablement + Fallbacks" scheduled Thu 11:00 PT (Zoom link in calendar invite).
text:
Talia Benson: Kicking off a thread to coordinate the architecture review for route-level quantization enablement + fallback behavior. Need sign-off this week to keep the schema/API/routing work unblocked.

Talia Benson: ADR draft is here (WIP): https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ARCH/ADR-0137-Route-level-quantization-profile-enablement

Talia Benson: Proposed review slot: Thu 11:00–11:45 PT. Attendees: Platform (Priya/Ethan/Mina/Ravi/Jordan), Runtime (Mateo/Noah), Console (Dylan/Chloe), Security/RBAC (Naomi/Marcus), PM (Marissa). If you can’t make it, pls comment on the ADR.

Marissa Cole: +1. Main thing from Product side: must be explicitly opt-in per route for v1, no global “tenant default enables everything.” Also needs “easy rollback” story that Support can execute.

Priya Natarajan: Thanks Talia. Read through ADR quickly. Overall direction seems right. I want to make sure we’re crisp on:
- what exactly is stored in route config (profile_id vs profile_ref vs alias)
- where compatibility is enforced (policy engine vs runtime)
- deterministic resolution (same request config => same profile)

Ethan Park: I can make Thu.
Question: ADR says “route references a profile ref string” and examples show both `recommended` and a versioned id. We should avoid anything that can change under customer feet.

Mina Haddad: +1. If we allow `recommended` alias, then “recommended” moving to a new version changes behavior without an explicit config write. That’s basically hidden rollout.

Ravi Menon: Agree. My suggestion: v1 should only allow explicit versioned `quant_profile_id` (immutable). If we need an alias later, treat it as a separate field with “pinning” semantics.

Jordan Lee: Determinism hat on: any alias that’s resolved at request time based on “latest” breaks determinism unless we snapshot it into resolved policy at write time.

Noah Patel: Runtime side: we can handle either, but if policy engine resolves to an explicit immutable id before the request hits the runtime, it’s cleaner. Runtime should never call “what’s the newest profile” mid-request.

Mateo Alvarez: Agree w/ Noah. Runtime should consume resolved result: {enabled, profile_id, reason_if_fallback}. Everything else belongs in control plane.

Dylan Brooks: Console UX question: customers will ask “just use recommended.” But we can still show “recommended” in UI and when they click enable, we write the pinned versioned ID. That gives UX simplicity without the hidden change risk.

Chloe Martin: That’s workable for backend too. Console can call a “preview” endpoint that returns which profile id would be pinned + compatibility warnings.

Naomi Feldman: For audit/compliance: we need the audit event to include the exact pinned profile id and diff. “Enabled recommended” is not sufficient for change control.

Marcus Lin: +1. Also need clarity on who can enable/disable this. Ideally a separate permission (route config write but with elevated capability). At minimum, record actor + route + old/new profile.

Talia Benson: Sounds like we’re converging on: UI may say “recommended” but route config stores immutable `quant_profile_id` (or `default`). I’ll add that as a decision point in ADR.

Priya Natarajan: On compatibility enforcement: ADR currently says policy engine checks model/hardware/seq length and falls back to baseline. Runtime also has guardrails.
We should decide: which layer is authoritative? (I’d like policy engine to be authoritative; runtime should still validate to protect itself.)

Jordan Lee: Agree. Policy eval should produce a single resolved decision, including fallback, to avoid oscillation.
Also: if runtime independently decides to fallback, we must avoid a feedback loop where policy keeps trying and runtime keeps rejecting.

Ravi Menon: +1 on “no oscillation.” I propose we include a `resolution_outcome` in the resolved policy: `APPLIED | FALLBACK_INCOMPATIBLE | DISABLED | KILL_SWITCH`.
Runtime logs it but shouldn’t re-resolve.

Noah Patel: Runtime still has to protect against mismatched artifacts / corrupted profile / unsupported kernel path. In those cases we should fail closed to baseline and emit metrics.
But we should NOT attempt to re-query policy. Just do local fallback.

Mateo Alvarez: Exactly. Runtime local fallback is last resort; also needs a counter so SRE can alert when it happens.

Connor O'Brien: From release/oncall POV: we need a clear kill switch behavior in the ADR:
- global kill switch disables application everywhere (even if route is enabled)
- should be fast propagating (minutes)
- should still keep system healthy (fallback baseline)

Mina Haddad: Routing view: if kill switch is on, route selection should still pick the same model variant; only precision/quant mode changes.
Do not redirect to different region/tier unless we’re already in a failure mode.

Ethan Park: Another detail: caching.
- route config service caching resolved settings is good
- but we must ensure config updates invalidate cache quickly
- and resolved decision is included in request context so runtime doesn’t do extra calls

Chloe Martin: +1. Our control plane endpoint can return “resolved quant settings” with a TTL + etag. Router can cache on route_id + model_variant + region.

Priya Natarajan: Fallback semantics: ADR says “fallback to baseline precision.” Need to define baseline precisely (e.g., model’s default serving mode). Also define what happens if baseline also not available (separate normal routing fallback).

Ravi Menon: Suggest: quantization fallback is orthogonal. If quant profile incompatible/unavailable -> serve with route’s normal model variant at baseline precision. If the model itself is unavailable -> normal routing fallback logic (existing).

Jordan Lee: We should explicitly state order of operations in ADR:
1) route -> model variant selection (existing)
2) resolve quant setting (enabled? which profile?)
3) compatibility check using selected model variant + hardware class + max_context
4) if incompatible -> set resolution outcome to fallback baseline

Noah Patel: +1. Also sequence length constraints: profiles might be validated only up to N ctx. If request ctx > N, we should fallback rather than attempting to run and risking correctness/perf.

Mateo Alvarez: That should be in compatibility matrix (profile declares `max_context_tokens` or similar). Runtime has the true request ctx at execution time; if request exceeds, runtime must fallback.

Marissa Cole: Product requirement: when fallback happens (incompatibility), customer should see it in Console + be able to alert on it. Otherwise they’ll think they’re saving money but they aren’t.

Dylan Brooks: Console can show “enabled but falling back X%” at route level. Need consistent signal name + reason.

Naomi Feldman: On audit: if kill switch overrides, that should also be a logged event with actor (SRE) and reason (incident ticket link maybe).

Connor O'Brien: We can do that. Also would like a “runbook” link in ADR so we’re aligned. I’ll drop the existing rollback doc once it’s merged.

Talia Benson: Action items before review:
- Priya/Jordan/Ravi: propose final decision on alias vs pinned profile id (sounds pinned)
- Noah/Mateo: propose runtime last-resort fallback behavior + metrics
- Naomi/Marcus: confirm RBAC/audit requirements (who can toggle, what gets logged)
- Dylan/Chloe: confirm Console flow: preview -> write pinned id -> show warnings

Priya Natarajan: I’ll add comments in ADR today.

Jordan Lee: I’ll comment with determinism language + avoid alias resolution at request time.

Noah Patel: I’ll add a section to ADR for runtime responsibilities vs policy responsibilities, and the “no re-query” rule.

Marcus Lin: I’ll add security notes. One ask: ensure profile artifacts are integrity checked (signing / checksum) and failures trigger fallback.

Talia Benson: Great. See you Thu unless conflicts. :thumbsup:

source.body:
Ethan Park: pulling folks into this thread from the installer workstream — we need to land rollback semantics + schema migration strategy this week so Connor can cut the next Private train. current ADR draft is here: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ARCH/adr-private-upgrade-plan-and-rollback-contract

Ethan Park: main open question: what do we *guarantee* on rollback when migrations have run + pods are partially updated? i want something deterministic and enforceable by the installer.

Michael Nguyen: +1. the hard part is DB schema. if we do irreversible migrations (drops, data backfills that change meaning), rollback becomes “restore from backup” only.

Benji Okafor: installer/state machine can do “rollback hooks” but it can’t invent down-migrations. we need to decide contract: either (a) only expand/contract migrations w/ backward compatibility window, or (b) rollback = restore snapshot and accept data loss since last backup.

Connor O'Brien: for release eng, i need a crisp line for GA: is rollback always possible via installer command? or “best-effort depending on migration class”? because that changes gating + comms.

Sean Gallagher: from SRE POV: we need to reduce the class of rollbacks that turn into 2am archaeology. if it’s “restore snapshot” only, we *must* enforce pre-upgrade backup, and we should assume some downtime during restore.

Ethan Park: right. what i’m leaning toward: in-place upgrade, rollback is supported if:
1) upgrade didn’t reach “commit point” (data plane pinned to old, CP not upgraded), OR
2) after commit, rollback requires backup restore for DB + config state

Michael Nguyen: can we define “commit point” as “schema migrations applied”? that’s clean. before migrations: revert is essentially helm rollback + installer state rewind.

Benji Okafor: careful: CP deploy can happen before migrations (depending on chart ordering). also we have config persistence changes in CP that might write new fields.

Quentin Harris: compatibility matrix perspective: if we require expand/contract, we can keep CP N compatible w/ DB schema N-1 (and DP can lag too). but that’s an explicit policy we need to enforce in CI.

Connor O'Brien: yeah, but “expand/contract for everything” is a big dev tax. what’s the minimal bar for v1? we scoped N-1 -> N only.

Rafael Mendes: also partial upgrades are the real failure mode in the wild. e.g., chart upgrade applied, some deployments updated, then migration job OOMs, and now the system is half new/half old.

Sean Gallagher: ^ exactly. rollback command needs to handle “partial chart rollout” + “partial migration.” idempotency matters.

Michael Nguyen: for DB migrations: we can enforce:
- migrations must be idempotent
- lock timeout defaults (so we fail fast rather than wedge)
- a pre-migration “schema guard” that blocks if current version != expected (prevents skipping)

Michael Nguyen: but that doesn’t solve down-migration. i recommend we *don’t* promise down-migrations. we promise either:
A) rollback without restore only if migrations are backward compatible, or
B) rollback via restore (snapshot) always.

Allison Grant: auditability angle: whatever we decide, we need to emit in audit logs: did we run migrations? did we hit commit? did we restore? and attach backup id + plan id.

Naomi Feldman: security/compliance POV: restore-based rollback is acceptable if it’s explicit and we have evidence (who triggered, what snapshot, encryption/KMS). but the doc must clearly call out potential data loss window.

Ethan Park: what about “dual write” / “write both schemas” as a pattern? seems heavy.

Michael Nguyen: dual-write is overkill for v1. the practical pattern is expand/contract:
- N migration: add columns/tables, don’t remove
- app code can read old + new
- contract (drop) only in N+1 or later

Benji Okafor: expand/contract is feasible for most config tables + control-plane metadata. but there are some “breaking” changes we’ve wanted (e.g., normalizing request logs). those would need a different path.

Connor O'Brien: we can set policy:
- default: expand/contract required for any schema touched during a release train that wants “helm rollback only”
- exception: if you need breaking migration, you must require backup restore rollback (block in release checklist unless docs updated + approval)

Sean Gallagher: + add an explicit “rollback mode” in the upgrade plan output:
- rollback_mode=\"in_place\" if no breaking migrations
- rollback_mode=\"restore_required\" if breaking migrations

Sean Gallagher: and the installer should surface this BEFORE upgrade start. no surprises mid-flight.

Ethan Park: love that. can we make it mechanized? e.g., migration tool emits metadata “reversible=false” or “requires_restore=true”.

Michael Nguyen: we can tag migrations with something like:
-- requires_restore: true
-- compatible_with_prev: true/false
and the upgrader can refuse unless backup completed.

Quentin Harris: plus version gating: if rollback_mode=restore_required then only allow N-1 -> N with a known backup provider (EBS/CSI/logical) configured.

Benji Okafor: installer already has prechecks framework now (pluggable). we can add a blocking check: “backup target configured + writable + encryption configured” when restore_required.

Connor O'Brien: ok but we also need to settle “partial upgrade” semantics. how do we detect what applied? helm history is not enough; jobs can succeed/fail.

Benji Okafor: proposal:
- installer writes an “upgrade state” object (local + persisted in cluster secret/CRD)
- each stage sets checkpoints: artifacts pulled, prechecks passed, backup done, CP upgraded, migrations run, DP upgraded
- rollback command consults state and runs the inverse plan for completed checkpoints.

Rafael Mendes: + make rollback idempotent. if you run rollback twice, it shouldn’t make things worse.

Michael Nguyen: for DB:
- if migrations didn’t start: do nothing
- if migrations started but not marked complete: assume unknown, force restore (because partial migration is the worst state)
- if migrations complete and compatible_with_prev=true: you *may* do in-place rollback (apps old version can run)
- if migrations complete and requires_restore=true: force restore

Sean Gallagher: yes. we should never try to “continue migration backward.” just restore.

Allison Grant: also need to define post-rollback verification: cluster health checks, DB schema version check, and a small control-plane readiness probe. and log them.

Naomi Feldman: please explicitly document what’s included in backup (db + config + secret refs) and what’s not (model cache, ephemeral logs). compliance customers will ask.

Ethan Park: noted. the backup doc covers most of that but we’ll align language.

Confluence Bot: Ethan Park shared a link: adr-private-upgrade-plan-and-rollback-contract (draft)

Connor O'Brien: action items i’m hearing:
1) Ethan/Michael update ADR with commit point definition + rollback modes
2) Benji add plan field rollback_mode + installer precheck to require backup when restore_required
3) Sean/Rafael provide SRE wording for “partial migration => force restore”

Sean Gallagher: yep. and add approval gate: any release w/ requires_restore migrations needs SRE + Release Eng signoff (ties to change management doc).

Quentin Harris: i’ll add a note to compatibility policy doc: “N control plane must be able to run against schema N-1 unless migration is flagged requires_restore.”

Michael Nguyen: i’ll draft migration tagging conventions today and post in #eng-platform. (reaction :thumbsup: from Ethan Park)

Ethan Park: cool. i’ll update ADR by EOD and @connor_obrien for review. goal is to stop bikeshedding in PR comments.

Connor O'Brien: :+1: once ADR is updated i’ll reference it in the release checklist and the PR template for migrations.

Rafael Mendes: one more: we should explicitly say model cache is not rolled back and may be cleared during rollback. ops needs to expect warmup latency after restore.

Sean Gallagher: +1, and runbook should include “post-rollback warmup + verify canary query.”

Ethan Park: agreed. capturing in ADR under “non-goals / expected side effects.”

Connor O'Brien: ok but we also need to settle “partial upgrade” semantics. how do we detect what applied? helm history is not enough; jobs can succeed/fail.

Benji Okafor: proposal:
- installer writes an “upgrade state” object (local + persisted in cluster secret/CRD)
- each stage sets checkpoints: artifacts pulled, prechecks passed, backup done, CP upgraded, migrations run, DP upgraded
- rollback command consults state and runs the inverse plan for completed checkpoints.

Rafael Mendes: + make rollback idempotent. if you run rollback twice, it shouldn’t make things worse.

Michael Nguyen: for DB:
- if migrations didn’t start: do nothing
- if migrations started but not marked complete: assume unknown, force restore (because partial migration is the worst state)
- if migrations complete and compatible_with_prev=true: you *may* do in-place rollback (apps old version can run)
- if migrations complete and requires_restore=true: force restore

Sean Gallagher: yes. we should never try to “continue migration backward.” just restore.

Allison Grant: also need to define post-rollback verification: cluster health checks, DB schema version check, and a small control-plane readiness probe. and log them.

Naomi Feldman: please explicitly document what’s included in backup (db + config + secret refs) and what’s not (model cache, ephemeral logs). compliance customers will ask.

Ethan Park: noted. the backup doc covers most of that but we’ll align language.

Confluence Bot: Ethan Park shared a link: adr-private-upgrade-plan-and-rollback-contract (draft)

Connor O'Brien: action items i’m hearing:
1) Ethan/Michael update ADR with commit point definition + rollback modes
2) Benji add plan field rollback_mode + installer precheck to require backup when restore_required
3) Sean/Rafael provide SRE wording for “partial migration => force restore”

Sean Gallagher: yep. and add approval gate: any release w/ requires_restore migrations needs SRE + Release Eng signoff (ties to change management doc).

Quentin Harris: i’ll add a note to compatibility policy doc: “N control plane must be able to run against schema N-1 unless migration is flagged requires_restore.”

Michael Nguyen: i’ll draft migration tagging conventions today and post in #eng-platform. (reaction :thumbsup: from Ethan Park)

Ethan Park: cool. i’ll update ADR by EOD and @connor_obrien for review. goal is to stop bikeshedding in PR comments.

Connor O'Brien: :+1: once ADR is updated i’ll reference it in the release checklist and the PR template for migrations.

Rafael Mendes: one more: we should explicitly say model cache is not rolled back and may be cleared during rollback. ops needs to expect warmup latency after restore.

Sean Gallagher: +1, and runbook should include “post-rollback warmup + verify canary query.”

Ethan Park: agreed. capturing in ADR under “non-goals / expected side effects.”

source.text:
Miles Thornton: quick write-up from the Incident Bot v2 arch review today. main question: can we ship deploy+config+sat signals without creating a second incident in the process :sweat_smile:

Miles Thornton: tl;dr proposal:
- bot -> diag-aggregator svc (new) -> (a) deploy-metadata svc, (b) config-diff svc, (c) telemetry-query svc
- aggregator does shaping + caching + rate limiting + degraded-mode behavior
- slack UX: 3 cards (Deploy / Config / Saturation) + “Open in Console” links

Rafael Mendes: +1 to putting all fan-out behind aggregator. oncall doesn’t care which upstream is sick, they just want “here’s what we know” and “here’s where to click”.

Logan Wright: biggest risk is telemetry load during real incidents. if #incidents has 40 ppl hammering “refresh context” we’ll DDOS ourselves.

Allison Grant: + paging loops. if bot causes extra latency in metrics pipeline, we’ll get more alerts, then more bot usage… spiral.

Ethan Park: question: is aggregator strictly read-only? any chance it triggers state (rollout pauses, routing changes) via interactive actions?

Miles Thornton: for v2, read-only. interactive actions are “expand diff”, “refresh”, “open dashboard”, “copy cli”. no “pause rollout” buttons.

Ravi Menon: read-only is good. we *should* still treat it as production-critical. during partial outages, it must fail predictably.

Devon Price: slack constraint: interactive action needs ACK in 3s. so aggregator has to respond fast or we do the pattern: immediate ack + “loading…” + async update.

Dylan Brooks: we’ve done the async update pattern in other bots. works, but we need idempotency keys so retries don’t post duplicate cards.

Connor O'Brien: deploy metadata path: we can provide last N deploys per service/region w/ rollout phase + canary status. join to PR/commit is possible but not always (hotfixes).

Benji Okafor: config diff: we can return “last 10 config mutations” and/or “diff between current and T-30m snapshot”. we need strong redaction hooks before anything hits Slack.

Marcus Lin: +1. we should assume Slack is not a secure sink. even in private channels. redaction must be server-side and testable.

Kevin Osei: and audit events. every call to config diff or privileged telemetry should emit an audit record (who/what incident/thread).

Naomi Feldman: from compliance side: we’ll want (a) scope-based access, (b) immutable audit trail, (c) clear break-glass rules. also define retention.

Miles Thornton: ok, diving into feedback requested:
1) data flow & boundaries
2) caching + coalescing
3) rate limits / circuit breakers
4) failure modes under partial outage

Ravi Menon: on (1): keep diag-aggregator dumb-ish. it should not encode “business logic” like routing decisions. it should *surface* current policy + pointers.

Mina Haddad: +1. if we embed policy eval in bot, we’ll drift from actual routing engine. better: bot shows pinned model, fallback chain, and “last routing policy change” link.

Jordan Lee: we can expose a “policy snapshot” endpoint that’s already computed by the control plane (safe subset). aggregator should fetch that.

Ethan Park: please make sure “safe subset” is explicit. policy configs can contain tenant annotations we don’t want sprayed in Slack.

Benji Okafor: for config diff, we’re planning allowlist-based rendering. everything else => “[redacted]”. secrets patterns are necessary but not sufficient.

Marcus Lin: exactly. regex-based “secret detection” will miss things and false-positive. allowlist primary, pattern-based secondary.

Allison Grant: on (2) caching: propose two layers?
- L1 in aggregator (in-memory) TTL 30-60s for interactive refresh spam
- L2 shared cache (redis) TTL 2-5m for same incident/thread to smooth bursts

Logan Wright: careful with caching telemetry. folks will treat it as real-time. if it’s 5m stale we need to label “as of” time and a refresh button.

Devon Price: UI-wise, we can show “Updated: 12:31:10Z” and “(cached)” in the card.

Rafael Mendes: + a note in degraded mode: “Live queries timing out; showing last known good context from 2m ago.”

Dylan Brooks: coalescing: yes. key should include {incident_id or thread_ts, service, region, tenant?}. if key is too broad we’ll leak tenant context.

Logan Wright: + on telemetry, avoid tenant in the query unless we need it. default to service/region aggregate for “saturation” card. tenant drill-down should be link out to Console where auth is stronger.

Miles Thornton: noted. current plan is:
- Saturation card = service/region/pool-level only
- Tenant-specific metrics only via deep link w/ RBAC

Aisha Bello: on (3) rate limits: we need budgets per thread and per user action.
- hard cap per thread: e.g. 6 refreshes / 5 min
- global cap in aggregator to protect upstreams
- backoff behavior: after cap hit, show message + links only

Allison Grant: also cap label cardinality. if telemetry query svc is asked for “by model, by tenant, by route, by status_code” we’re dead.

Logan Wright: we should define a strict set of queries the bot is allowed to run. like a fixed query catalog with parameters, not arbitrary promql.

Miles Thornton: yes, query catalog approach. aggregator calls telemetry-query svc with query_id + params (service/region/window). no raw query strings.

Ethan Park: +1. easier to permission and audit.

Kevin Osei: auditing: log (a) caller identity (bot service acct), (b) human user initiating action, (c) query_id, (d) params (redacted), (e) result size, (f) latency, (g) outcome (success/timeout/circuit-open).

Marcus Lin: and for config diff, log “fields returned” count maybe, plus whether any redactions occurred.

Benji Okafor: we can do “redaction_summary: N fields redacted” but keep it in audit logs not Slack.

Ravi Menon: on (4) partial outage behavior: define priorities.
- Must show *something* within ~10s (or slack will feel broken)
- If upstream slow: show cached + deep links
- If cached missing: show “manual commands” snippet (eg `rwctl deploys --service X --region Y`)

Connor O'Brien: for deploys, if deploy metadata svc is down, we can still pull from deploy event stream? maybe last-known state in redis.

Miles Thornton: we can cache deploy context longer (10-15m) since it doesn’t change second-to-second.

Allison Grant: but stamp it. “last deploy event seen at …”

Dylan Brooks: circuit breaker suggestion:
- per-upstream breaker in aggregator
- open when p95 latency > threshold or error rate > threshold
- when open: stop calling upstream for N seconds, serve cache + links

Logan Wright: yes. and telemetry-query svc should also have its own breaker to protect the TSDB.

Rafael Mendes: also, bot should never block incident channel during sev0. if it times out, it should say so plainly (not “unknown error”).

Devon Price: copy for error states:
- “Telemetry query timed out (10s). Showing cached context from 2m ago.”
- “Config diff unavailable: missing scope `config.diff.read`. Escalate via #help (INT ticket) or break-glass if Sev0/Sev1.”

Ethan Park: careful wording on break-glass. should route to the oncall/security process, not encourage random folks.

Naomi Feldman: agree. phrasing should be “Request elevated access via documented Sev0/Sev1 procedure (link).”

Kevin Osei: and the break-glass action itself must generate a high-signal audit event (and ideally page security-oncall if used outside sev0/1).

Miles Thornton: open question: should aggregator run in same cluster as bot or separate?

Logan Wright: separate. if bot gets wedged / restarts, we don’t want it taking cache + rate limiter with it.

Dylan Brooks: + separate deploy lets us roll diag changes without touching slack app bits. but keep it small.

Allison Grant: also consider: aggregator should have low dependency footprint. avoid calling 6 internal services. keep it to 3 + cache.

Priya Natarajan: platform POV: fine to add aggregator, but we need ownership clear. SRE owns? platform owns? shared?

Rafael Mendes: SRE can own runtime/SLIs, but data contracts live w/ respective teams. propose: SRE owns aggregator + bot, platform owns config/policy endpoints, telemetry owns telemetry-query svc, releases owns deploy metadata.

Miles Thornton: action items from review (please correct):
- [Logan] define query catalog + cost budgets; propose default windows + aggregations for saturation
- [Benji + Marcus + Naomi] finalize redaction + safe render allowlist + language for Slack
- [Allison + Aisha] rate limit + circuit breaker thresholds + “degraded mode” card standards
- [Connor] confirm deploy metadata schema + fallback plan when deploy svc unavailable
- [Kevin] audit event spec fields + retention
- [Devon + Dylan] slack async update + idempotency keys pattern for refresh/expand

Logan Wright: looks right. add: we should run a perf canary for telemetry-query latency as part of rollout.

Allison Grant: +1. and alert on “bot context generation latency” separately from core prod latency.

Ethan Park: please document permission scopes early. we’ve had surprises where bot can’t read prod config because role missing.

Miles Thornton: will add “scopes matrix” to the ADR. thanks all.

Marcus Lin: last ask: add tests that assert no forbidden fields can appear in Slack payload (golden tests). treat as security regression.

Benji Okafor: yes. we can add a suite w/ synthetic config that includes fake secrets + PII keys and assert output is redacted.

Rafael Mendes: :thumbsup: ship it (safely).

Rafael Mendes: SRE can own runtime/SLIs, but data contracts live w/ respective teams. propose: SRE owns aggregator + bot, platform owns config/policy endpoints, telemetry owns telemetry-query svc, releases owns deploy metadata.

Miles Thornton: action items from review (please correct):
- [Logan] define query catalog + cost budgets; propose default windows + aggregations for saturation
- [Benji + Marcus + Naomi] finalize redaction + safe render allowlist + language for Slack
- [Allison + Aisha] rate limit + circuit breaker thresholds + “degraded mode” card standards
- [Connor] confirm deploy metadata schema + fallback plan when deploy svc unavailable
- [Kevin] audit event spec fields + retention
- [Devon + Dylan] slack async update + idempotency keys pattern for refresh/expand

Logan Wright: looks right. add: we should run a perf canary for telemetry-query latency as part of rollout.

Allison Grant: +1. and alert on “bot context generation latency” separately from core prod latency.

Ethan Park: please document permission scopes early. we’ve had surprises where bot can’t read prod config because role missing.

Miles Thornton: will add “scopes matrix” to the ADR. thanks all.

Marcus Lin: last ask: add tests that assert no forbidden fields can appear in Slack payload (golden tests). treat as security regression.

Benji Okafor: yes. we can add a suite w/ synthetic config that includes fake secrets + PII keys and assert output is redacted.

Rafael Mendes: :thumbsup: ship it (safely).

source.content:
Jordan Lee: Dropping ADR draft for review (ADR-017). Main open question: should readiness be event-sourced vs stored as current state w/ updates. https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ARCH/ADR-017+Provision-to-ready+Readiness+State+Machine

Confluence Bot: Jordan Lee shared a page: ADR-017 Provision-to-ready Readiness State Machine

Benji Okafor: skimmed. the state machine itself looks fine (Allocated -> Provisioning -> Validating -> Ready / Quarantined). but the storage section is underspecified.

Ethan Park: +1. we need to be clear bc control plane + readiness svc are going to build on this for APIs + authz.

Vanessa Ortiz: from fleet side: we mostly need “what is the node’s current status + why” for ops + automation decisions. event sourcing feels like overkill unless we actually need replay.

Miles Thornton: (SRE hat) I like having a history trail. but “event-sourced system” is a loaded term.

Allison Grant: observability angle: if we rely on events as source of truth, we need guarantees about ordering + idempotency, or we’ll be spending a month debugging phantom transitions.

Jordan Lee: yep. that’s the question. one camp wants: append-only events and derive current state. other camp wants: write current state directly and optionally also write history.

Benji Okafor: tradeoffs:
- event-sourcing: great for audit + rebuild, but every consumer ends up implementing “reducer” logic and dealing with duplicates/out-of-order.
- current-state: simpler reads + simpler API contract, but you lose forensic detail unless you store transitions separately.

Rafael Mendes: also consider incident response. when a node is stuck, we need “last successful step + last error + timestamps”. having a transition log helps.

Vanessa Ortiz: agreed. but can we get that without full event sourcing? i.e. state table + transition table.

Ethan Park: IMO yes. keep canonical read model as “current state” in control plane (or readiness service) and also write an immutable transition record on state changes.

Allison Grant: +1. then metrics can rely on current state + timestamps, and deeper analysis can query transitions.

Miles Thornton: Another issue: event sourcing implies we can rebuild state from events. do we want that? bc if we ever miss events or have partial retention, rebuild is a lie.

Jordan Lee: right. plus we’ll have 2-3 producers: provisioning orchestrator, validation agent, and occasional operator override/quarantine. event ordering across producers is messy.

Benji Okafor: if we do events-only, we need:
- single writer or strict sequencing
- event ids + dedupe
- monotonic versioning
- strong constraints around allowed transitions
…and we still have to handle out-of-order delivery.

Rafael Mendes: paging story too: on call will look at “node is Validating for 47m” and the reason. they won’t replay a stream.

Vanessa Ortiz: i worry about flake loops. if validation retries 3x and emits 3 failure events, does the reducer bounce the node between states? would be confusing.

Allison Grant: exactly. you end up with “current state” anyway (what do we show in console), so you’re basically building a materialized view. might as well make it first-class.

Jordan Lee: one argument for event sourcing was: “it’s the only way to compute time-to-ready reliably.” but we can compute that from timestamps on the current-state object + transition records.

Miles Thornton: for SLI: we need allocated_ts, provisioning_start_ts, ready_ts (or quarantine_ts). Those can live on the node readiness record and be set exactly once.

Ethan Park: yes. store these timestamps as part of current state object. transitions table is nice-to-have for audit + debugging.

Benji Okafor: we should explicitly define authoritative writes:
- orchestrator calls readiness svc to set state transitions
- validation agent posts results (artifacts) but does NOT directly set Ready
- operator can set Quarantined with reason
Then readiness svc enforces allowed transitions.

Vanessa Ortiz: +1. validation agent shouldn’t be able to mark Ready just bc its own smoke test passed. it should report “validation result = pass/fail” and then orchestrator/ctrl-plane decides.

Allison Grant: and we should be careful not to build a “distributed consensus via Slack” system lol. need one component to decide.

Jordan Lee: what about “agent heartbeat is authoritative”? ADR draft says heartbeat contributes to readiness.

Ethan Park: heartbeat should be a signal (node is alive) but not the source of truth for the state machine. it can block transitions (e.g., can’t move to Validating if no heartbeat), but it shouldn’t mutate state directly.

Rafael Mendes: agreed. otherwise a heartbeat jitter would flip states and we’d page.

Miles Thornton: also: define handling for stale / out-of-date updates. ex: orchestrator retries a request, arrives late. we should reject if state_version has advanced.

Benji Okafor: yep: optimistic concurrency control.
- readiness record has state_version integer
- transition API requires expected_version
- server increments version on success
This is easier with current-state than with an event stream.

Jordan Lee: makes sense. so decision wording: “store current state as canonical; also persist append-only transition history for audit/debug; not event-sourced.”

Priya Natarajan: catching up. +1 to “current state canonical.” event sourcing sounds elegant but it’s too easy to end up with fragile semantics + painful backfills. we need boring + reliable.

Vanessa Ortiz: thank you. fleet ops needs boring.

Allison Grant: also note: if we have a transition table, please don’t tag every event with per-node random ids in metrics :grimacing: (cardinality). for logs yes, for metrics no.

Jordan Lee: will add note. (reaction :thumbsup: from Jordan Lee)

Ethan Park: ADR should also call out retention policy. transition history could be 30-90 days in DB, long-term in object store if needed.

Rafael Mendes: +1. SRE will want at least “last N transitions” without having to hunt in cold storage.

Miles Thornton: can we explicitly say: transition history is best-effort for debugging, but current state is source of truth? (so we don’t block readiness if transition write fails)

Benji Okafor: careful. if we say best-effort, audit/compliance folks may object. but for this sprint, we can make both writes in one transaction in readiness svc DB.

Priya Natarajan: do it in one txn if possible. if we can’t, then current-state must win and we accept partial history (but document it).

Jordan Lee: ok, action items for ADR update:
1) Decision: canonical current state in readiness service.
2) Add transition history table (append-only) written alongside state changes.
3) Define writer roles + authz (orchestrator sets state; agent posts validation results; operator overrides to Quarantined).
4) Add concurrency/versioning + out-of-order handling.
5) Notes: metrics cardinality constraints + retention.

Vanessa Ortiz: sounds good. pls also add “no direct Ready from agent” as explicit requirement.

Ethan Park: +1.

Jordan Lee: will update today and re-request approvals.

Priya Natarajan: (reaction :white_check_mark: from Priya Natarajan)

Allison Grant: ping me when updated, I’ll sanity check the metrics bit.

Rafael Mendes: and I’ll take a quick pass for oncall/incident response implications.

Benji Okafor: same, especially the API + storage section.

Jordan Lee: thanks all.

source.text:
Allison Grant: Need a decision today on audit event shape: flattened fields vs nested objects. Main constraint: SIEM compatibility + keep schema stable for v1.

Kevin Osei: +1. From customer reqs: Sentinel + Splunk both *can* take JSON, but nested stuff often ends up as a string blob unless you add parsing rules. Also dot-notation keys can get weird depending on pipeline.

Logan Wright: For coverage dashboard + queries, flat is easier. Nested means our metrics extraction has to handle multiple shapes (and people will inevitably log arbitrary JSON).

Nadia Rahman: But we still need a way to carry producer-specific details (e.g., policy diff summary, installer operator metadata) without bloating the canonical field list.

Marcus Lin: Security POV: we care about consistent actor/target + correlation first. Anything that breaks queries during an incident is a no.

Ethan Park: Control plane already has objects internally (`actor`, `target`, etc.). Flattening will be annoying but doable. My concern is “flattened forever” makes future evolution painful.

Allison Grant: Dropping some examples for discussion.

Allison Grant: Option A (nested-ish):
```json
{
  "schema_version": "1.0",
  "event_id": "01J...",
  "event_type": "rbac.role.update",
  "occurred_at": "2026-02-10T19:03:12.123Z",
  "tenant_id": "tnt_...",
  "actor": { "type": "user", "id": "usr_...", "email": "a@b.com" },
  "target": { "type": "role", "id": "role_..." },
  "request": { "request_id": "req_...", "trace_id": "trc_...", "source_ip": "1.2.3.4" },
  "extensions": { "policy_engine": { "diff": "..." } }
}
```

Allison Grant: Option B (flat):
```json
{
  "schema_version": "1.0",
  "event_id": "01J...",
  "event_type": "rbac.role.update",
  "occurred_at": "2026-02-10T19:03:12.123Z",
  "tenant_id": "tnt_...",
  "actor_type": "user",
  "actor_id": "usr_...",
  "actor_email_hash": "sha256:...",
  "target_type": "role",
  "target_id": "role_...",
  "request_id": "req_...",
  "trace_id": "trc_...",
  "source_ip": "1.2.3.4",
  "ext_policy_engine_diff": "..."
}
```

Kevin Osei: Strongly prefer Option B for SIEM export defaults. Customers can still parse JSON, but they shouldn’t have to.

Jordan Lee: Re: ext fields — can we use dotted names like `ext.policy_engine.diff`? Some SIEMs treat dot as nesting. Might be okay, but risky.

Kevin Osei: Yeah, Splunk: dotted fields become painful unless you normalize. Sentinel: depends on KQL ingestion rules. My vote: underscore prefix for namespacing: `ext_policy_engine_*`.

Hanae Suzuki: Also data classification/redaction is cleaner with flat + explicit fields. If we allow arbitrary nested “extensions”, people will accidentally drop raw payloads in there.

Ethan Park: Counterpoint: forcing everything into `ext_*` strings feels like a junk drawer. Can we at least constrain ext keys via registry (namespaces + allowed fields)?

Logan Wright: We can do that. Registry can include canonical fields + allowed extension keys per namespace. Telemetry can track unknown keys to catch drift.

Allison Grant: Another angle: query ergonomics. In incident response we want: `event_type=... actor_id=... target_id=... source_ip=...` without JSON-path.

Marcus Lin: +1. During an investigation, people will be in Splunk/Sentinel at 3am. Flat fields win.

Nadia Rahman: If we go flat, can we keep a stable top-level set and allow extensions but still avoid huge cardinality? Some fields like `diff` can explode.

Allison Grant: We’ll enforce cardinality constraints in the standard (cap lengths, forbid free-form blobs unless explicitly approved). For diffs we can store a summarized diff + optional hash pointer.

Jordan Lee: So for policy changes: `ext_policy_engine_diff_summary` + `ext_policy_engine_diff_sha256`? Then full diff goes to internal debug logs only, not audit.

Hanae Suzuki: Works for PII too. If someone wants `actor_email`, we say no; only `actor_email_hash` if needed for correlation.

Ethan Park: Implementation detail: control plane services already build an `AuditEvent` struct. If we pick flat, we should provide a lib that takes nested internal structs and outputs the flattened schema.

Allison Grant: That’s already in flight (schema library + validator). We can add helper methods: `SetActor(...)`, `SetTarget(...)`, `SetRequestContext(...)`, and `AddExtension(namespace, key, value)` which emits `ext_<namespace>_<key>`.

Kevin Osei: +1 please also add exporter normalization: if some legacy producer still emits `actor.id`, exporter maps to `actor_id` etc.

Logan Wright: For coverage dashboard: we’ll key off `event_type` + `schema_version` + `service_name` (also needs to be top-level). If `service_name` is nested we’re back to parsing.

Allison Grant: Good call: `service_name`, `service_version`, `deployment_mode` (hosted/dedicated/private) should be top-level.

Marcus Lin: Any objections to: “Canonical top-level stable fields + flat namespaced extensions, no nested objects in v1”? (Exceptions only if a field is *always* a scalar and widely supported.)

Ethan Park: I’m good with that as long as we: (1) reserve prefixes, (2) provide registry + lints, (3) document how to evolve without breaking.

Nadia Rahman: +1. Also let’s explicitly state “extensions are optional; consumers must not rely on them for core audit semantics.”

Kevin Osei: Works. From customer side we can say: stable top-level schema for SIEM; optional extensions for richer context.

Allison Grant: Decision: v1 will use **flattened top-level canonical fields** and allow **namespaced extensions as additional top-level fields** using `ext_<namespace>_<field>` (underscores, not dots). No nested JSON objects for actor/request/target; represent those as scalar fields (`actor_type`, `actor_id`, etc.). We’ll capture this in the ADR + logging standard and update the schema draft accordingly.

Logan Wright: :thumbsup: I’ll update the coverage extractor to whitelist `ext_*` but ignore them for the “required fields present” check.

Hanae Suzuki: :+1: I’ll add redaction guidance: extensions cannot include secrets/PII; require review for any new `ext_*` key.

Kevin Osei: I’ll note in exporter docs: if customer wants nested output, they can transform downstream, but Redwood export is flat by default.

Allison Grant: Cool. I’ll post a follow-up with the reserved namespaces list (console, control_plane, policy_engine, private_installer, gateway, exporter) and the lint rule proposal.

source.content:
Ravi Menon: We need to settle enforcement boundary for hosted data residency. Where do we *actually* block? gateway vs smart routing vs downstream services. Biggest risk is some fallback path still crosses region.

Mina Haddad: routing layer is the only place that truly knows where a request is going. gateway can validate inputs but if routing later decides “US failover” we’re toast.

Amara Diallo: gateway is where we can make the contract deterministic + consistent errors (streaming/non-streaming). but agreed, gateway alone can’t stop cross-region if routing ignores it.

Jordan Lee: IMO: policy decision happens once at edge (gateway) -> then routing must be constrained by that decision. treat residency decision as immutable context.

Marcus Lin: +1 immutable. Also need to define “customer content” boundary. prompts, messages, completions, embeddings input, tool args, uploaded files. Those must not leave pinned region. Some metadata can.

Ravi Menon: question: do we consider model fallback as “moving customer content” if we re-run generation in another region w/ same prompt? yes, that’s literally shipping content.

Mina Haddad: yep. fallback across region must be disabled unless policy allows it explicitly.

Sean Gallagher: failure modes: what if EU region has capacity incident? historically we auto-fail to us-east. With pinning we’ll start blocking traffic. Are we okay failing closed?

Naomi Feldman: product expectation: for EU-only customers, we block rather than violate. But error must be super clear and actionable.

Brian Murphy: for error contract, my preference is: gateway returns 409 + error_code RESIDENCY_POLICY_VIOLATION. include request_id + allowed_regions + attempted_region.

Amara Diallo: 409 works for “policy conflict”. 403 is also common. ADR leaning 409 iirc.

Jordan Lee: enforcement points proposal:
1) gateway loads policy + validates request region hint (if provided) and attaches `x-rw-residency-context`.
2) routing enforces *hard* constraints: selected_region ∈ allowed_regions.
3) downstream systems only rely on region-local infra (logs/traces stores, queue, etc.)

Elena Popov: can we rely on a header for “immutable context”? internal header can be overwritten if some service is sloppy.

Amara Diallo: internal context should be in signed token / mTLS metadata, not user-provided. gateway sets it, services treat as authoritative. We can also strip any inbound `x-rw-*` headers.

Marcus Lin: stripping inbound is mandatory. attackers can set their own allowed_regions otherwise.

Logan Wright: re downstream: telemetry pipeline currently ships some traces cross-region for centralized processing. For this project we said enforcement includes logs/traces boundaries. we need a plan there.

Sean Gallagher: +1. we currently have “global” S3 bucket for some request/response samples (redacted-ish). residency means those *cannot* be captured outside region.

Ravi Menon: what if routing can’t find capacity in allowed regions? do we block w/ 409 OR do we return 503?

Brian Murphy: difference is important:
- policy violation: request is asking for something outside allowed set OR system attempted cross-region fallback.
- capacity issue *within* allowed set should be 503 with a different code.

Jordan Lee: but if capacity shortage triggers the *temptation* to go cross-region, that’s still not a policy violation; it’s just no capacity in allowed regions. message should say “pinned to EU; no capacity in EU right now; cross-region failover disabled”.

Naomi Feldman: yes. we promised customers we won’t silently run outside EU. so we should say that explicitly.

Mina Haddad: routing can differentiate:
- candidate_regions = allowed_regions intersect service_regions
- if empty -> config issue / 409
- if non-empty but all unavailable -> 503 (or 429?)

Sean Gallagher: please not 429. that’ll get misinterpreted by customers as rate limit. 503 w/ retry-after maybe.

Amara Diallo: gateway needs to map these outcomes to stable contract. routing can emit decision outcome enum and gateway formats.

Elena Popov: where do we put the actual “attempted cross-region fallback” signal? routing should log it but also return structured info so gateway can produce the right response.

Ravi Menon: routing currently just returns “no healthy backends” and gateway turns into generic 500/503. We’ll add structured error.

Brian Murphy: something like:
```
residency_outcome=BLOCKED_POLICY | NO_CAPACITY_ALLOWED_REGIONS | OK
selected_region=eu-central-1
attempted_regions=[...]
policy_id=...
```

Jordan Lee: careful: arrays in logs are ok, but error response should keep it simple. For the API response: allowed_regions + primary_region + attempted_region (single) maybe.

Marcus Lin: don’t leak internal region IDs if we can avoid. external region names only (EU/US/APAC or cloud region strings that docs mention).

Logan Wright: also: audit logs. if policy changes allow cross-region fallback, that’s a material config change. should be in audit trail.

Sean Gallagher: back to failure mode: what if policy service is down. do we fail open or closed.

Marcus Lin: fail open is a residency violation risk. for orgs with a residency policy, we must fail closed (block) if we can’t evaluate policy.

Naomi Feldman: +1 for enterprise. For non-enterprise/no policy set, we can proceed as normal.

Amara Diallo: implement: gateway fetches policy from control plane w/ short timeout; caches. If cache miss + policy fetch fails:
- if request has org/project flagged “residency_required” -> block w/ 503? or 409?

Brian Murphy: that’s not a policy violation; it’s enforcement system unavailable. I’d go 503 RESIDENCY_ENFORCEMENT_UNAVAILABLE.

Jordan Lee: agree. 409 implies deterministic config mismatch. Here it’s “we can’t confirm compliance”.

Mina Haddad: routing also needs the policy. If gateway attaches signed context, routing doesn’t need to call policy service on hot path.

Ravi Menon: that seems best. gateway does policy lookup + signs; routing validates signature and enforces constraints.

Elena Popov: +1. keeps routing fast. But watch out: we have non-gateway ingress paths (internal batch jobs, eval harness). They need to go through same gateway/policy evaluation or use shared lib.

Logan Wright: background processing: some requests enqueue moderation/analytics tasks. those queues must be region-local. If the queue service is global, that violates.

Sean Gallagher: for phase 1, can we gate: “no background tasks that ship prompt/completion outside region”? i want a list of out-of-scope subsystems.

Marcus Lin: ADR needs explicit allowed cross-region metadata: request_id, policy_id, account_id, status code, latency, token counts. No prompt text, no embeddings vectors.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: jumping in — from security standpoint, boundary must be “content never crosses region”. That means (a) routing selection constrained, (b) logs/traces/samples pipelines audited. I’m ok with phased hardening but only if we can clearly say what’s guaranteed at GA.

Naomi Feldman: noted. we’ll align wording: GA guarantee for hosted = request/response content + any derived content (embeddings) stays in allowed regions; operational metadata may be global.

Ravi Menon: ok action items:
- Jordan/Amara: propose signed residency context format + where stored (header vs metadata)
- Mina/Elena/Ravi: routing enforcement logic + error enums (policy block vs no capacity)
- Logan/Sean/Marcus: list telemetry pipelines that currently cross region + mitigation plan

Amara Diallo: I’ll add a note: streaming path needs same error payload. currently it can’t send JSON body once stream started. we should detect violation *before* starting stream.

Brian Murphy: +1: residency checks must happen before any token is emitted. no partial stream then error.

Sean Gallagher: also: what if region becomes unhealthy mid-stream. we cannot resume in another region. we should terminate stream w/ retry guidance, but keep it within region.

Ravi Menon: yep, no mid-stream cross-region. we’ll just close connection and client retries.

Jordan Lee: I’ll draft the boundary statement: gateway decides + signs, routing enforces, downstream must not export content. will post to ADR-021 thread.

Marcus Lin: please include “strip inbound x-rw-* headers” + signature validation requirement.

Ravi Menon: done. thanks all.

source.body:
Miles Thornton: We need to settle backup artifact format stability + migration strategy before we lock docs. Specifically: do we treat backups as a semver’d API? What are our compatibility guarantees?

Michael Nguyen: +1. ENG-9830 is blocked on this. Today the installer emits `formatVersion: 1` in the manifest but it’s basically “best effort.” If we’re shipping to regulated on-prem, “oops new installer can’t restore old backup” is not acceptable.

Ethan Park: Agree on not breaking restores, but we should be explicit about scope. We’re backing up control-plane state (db + config + some k8s objects) NOT “entire cluster.” That affects what “compatibility” means.

Connor O'Brien: From release side: we can enforce at build time that backup format changes are reviewed (ADR + changelog). But we need a clear policy: what’s MAJOR vs MINOR. Also, we have to support air-gapped bundle verification; migrations can’t require “reach out to Redwood servers.”

Miles Thornton: Let’s enumerate options:
1) Backup format is versioned (semver) and restore supports N previous majors
2) Backup format is “pinned” to platform version and we ship migration utilities
3) We never change backup format (lol)

Hanae Suzuki: Security angle: any migration step that decrypts/re-encrypts is a key-handling event. Need deterministic behavior + audit logs. Also: do we keep old ciphertext as-is and only rewrite manifest? depends on what changes.

Marcus Lin: Also threat model: backups are an attacker target. Format changes often come with metadata changes. We should avoid “silent coercion” on restore. If we detect unknown fields/version, fail closed with actionable message.

Benji Okafor: Practical: restore workflow needs to decide early if it can proceed. I want something like:
- read manifest
- verify checksums
- verify signature (air-gapped)
- check `formatVersion` compatibility
- only then decrypt / import

Michael Nguyen: +1. Can we do semver on backup *format* (manifest schema + archive layout) and separately track *data migrations* (db schema)? They’re related but not the same.

Ethan Park: Yep. DB migrations already exist in control-plane. Restore is trickier because we may restore to a different platform version.

Neha Kapoor: Reliability POV: we should define a contract that makes SRE life easier:
- N-1 restore works (newest installer can restore previous minor/patch backups)
- Major bumps require explicit operator action + preflight warning
- Provide a “dry run / validate” mode that tells you exactly what will happen

Allison Grant: Observability note: we need structured events when format mismatch happens (category: compatibility) so dashboards can show how often customers are hitting it.

Miles Thornton: Ok, semver proposal:
- `backupFormatVersion` = MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH
- MAJOR: breaking layout/schema change (requires migration tool)
- MINOR: backwards compatible additions (new fields optional)
- PATCH: bugfix / clarifications (no behavior change)

Marcus Lin: I’m mostly ok with semver, but define “backwards compatible” rigorously. Example: adding new metadata is fine if restore ignores unknown keys. But do we *want* ignoring unknown keys? That’s the classic footgun.

Michael Nguyen: On restore we can do: allow unknown keys in manifest but require known `backupFormatVersion` range. Unknown keys get logged (debug). If MAJOR mismatched, error. If MINOR higher than supported, error unless we can safely ignore.

Ethan Park: That turns into: restore supports `MAJOR == X` and `MINOR <= maxMinorSupported`. That’s fine.

Connor O'Brien: But you’re describing “new backup might not restore on older installer,” which is ok. We care more about “new installer restoring old backup.”

Neha Kapoor: Exactly. Customer expectation: you can upgrade tooling and still restore the last few backups.

Miles Thornton: So compatibility direction: backward compat for restore (new tool restores old backups). Forward compat (old tool restores new backup) not required.

Hanae Suzuki: With encryption: if we change encryption envelope format, that’s a MAJOR. But we should isolate encryption metadata in manifest and keep ciphertext opaque. Ideally: encryption is “provider + parameters” and doesn’t change often.

Marcus Lin: +1. Also: manifest should include KMS key reference (arn/uri) but never include plaintext material. And we should not back up actual secret values if we can avoid it; references only (aligns w/ ADR-027).

Michael Nguyen: I’ll paste what I think the manifest should look like long term (rough):

```yaml
backupFormatVersion: 1.1.0
createdAt: 2025-02-06T19:05:12Z
platformVersion: 2025.02.1
components:
  controlPlaneDb:
    engine: postgres
    logicalDump: true
    artifact: artifacts/control-plane-db.sql.zst.enc
  k8s:
    includedNamespaces: ["redwood-system", "redwood-control-plane"]
    artifact: artifacts/k8s-resources.tar.zst.enc
crypto:
  envelope:
    provider: aws-kms
    keyRef: arn:aws:kms:us-east-1:...:key/...
  dataKeyAlg: AES-256-GCM
integrity:
  manifestSha256: ...
  artifacts:
    - path: artifacts/control-plane-db.sql.zst.enc
      sha256: ...
    - path: artifacts/k8s-resources.tar.zst.enc
      sha256: ...
compat:
  minRestoreToolVersion: 0.9.0
  maxTestedRestoreToolVersion: 1.3.2
notes: "generated by redwood-private-installer"
```

Ethan Park: That’s good. `platformVersion` is important for data migrations. But I’m not sure about `maxTestedRestoreToolVersion` (feels weird). Maybe better: `createdByToolVersion` only, and our docs say what is supported.

Connor O'Brien: Agree. “max tested” will rot fast and look like a promise.

Miles Thornton: Maybe:
- `createdBy.tool` + `createdBy.version`
- `required.restoreTool.minVersion` (rare)
- and keep the rest as docs/compat matrix.

Allison Grant: For telemetry: include `backupId` UUID so we can correlate backup + restore drill + metrics. But for air-gapped, it’s still useful for logs.

Benji Okafor: Another detail: archive layout. Do we want deterministic layout?
`manifest.yaml` at root + `artifacts/` directory with fixed names. No timestamps in filenames.

Connor O'Brien: Please yes. Deterministic layout helps signing + reproducible bundles. Also in air-gapped import/export we want predictable paths.

Marcus Lin: Deterministic is good, but don’t bake secrets into path names (customer names, tenant ids). Keep it generic.

Michael Nguyen: Re: migration strategy: If we bump MAJOR, do we ship `backup migrate` that rewrites old to new?

Miles Thornton: Ideally yes, but only if necessary. Another model is: restore tool understands multiple majors (e.g., supports 1.x and 2.x). But that increases code path complexity.

Neha Kapoor: SRE preference: fewer migration steps during an incident. In DR you’re already stressed; “run migrate, then restore” adds risk.

Ethan Park: +1. If we can, keep restore tool able to read at least 1 previous major. But we can reserve the right not to.

Connor O'Brien: Release reality: maintaining multiple majors indefinitely is expensive. But supporting at least “current major and previous major” seems doable.

Marcus Lin: Security preference: fewer legacy parsers forever. Each parser is an attack surface.

Hanae Suzuki: If we do multi-major, enforce strict parsing + size limits (zip bombs etc). Also signature verification BEFORE extraction.

Benji Okafor: +1 to “verify before extract.” Current PR-512 does checksum verification but we should ensure we’re not untarring untrusted content prior to verifying.

Michael Nguyen: So we’re converging on:
- Semver `backupFormatVersion`
- Restore supports: current MAJOR and optionally previous MAJOR (policy)
- New restore tool must restore old backups within same MAJOR (all MINOR/PATCH)
- MAJOR bumps are rare + ADR required

Miles Thornton: Yes. Also add: MINOR changes must be additive + optional. PATCH changes must not alter semantics.

Ethan Park: Another tricky bit: DB dumps. If we do logical dumps, those are not always forward/back compat across postgres versions. But that’s not “backup format,” that’s “component artifact.” We need to document what postgres versions are supported.

Neha Kapoor: Put it in the RPO/RTO + supported versions matrix. But on restore, tool should preflight check the target environment matches requirements (db engine/version).

Allison Grant: And emit a metric/event when preflight fails due to version mismatch so we can see it.

Connor O'Brien: Action items:
- Miles/Michael: update ADR-027 addendum or new ADR for semver policy + multi-major support.
- Connor: add release gate: backup format changes require bump + changelog snippet.
- Benji: ensure installer checks `backupFormatVersion` range and errors with pointer to docs.

Marcus Lin: Add to ADR: “unknown major => fail closed.” And explicitly state we won’t ever auto-downgrade or “best effort” restore.

Hanae Suzuki: Also include statement about key rotation and what happens if KMS unavailable during restore (fail, don’t fallback to plaintext).

Michael Nguyen: I’ll comment on ENG-9830 with this proposal and link it to PR-512 notes.

Miles Thornton: Cool. I’ll draft the ADR text tonight and tag folks for review.

```yaml
    engine: postgres
    logicalDump: true
    artifact: artifacts/control-plane-db.sql.zst.enc
  k8s:
    includedNamespaces: ["redwood-system", "redwood-control-plane"]
    artifact: artifacts/k8s-resources.tar.zst.enc
crypto:
  envelope:
    provider: aws-kms
    keyRef: arn:aws:kms:us-east-1:...:key/...
  dataKeyAlg: AES-256-GCM
integrity:
  manifestSha256: ...
  artifacts:
    - path: artifacts/control-plane-db.sql.zst.enc
      sha256: ...
    - path: artifacts/k8s-resources.tar.zst.enc
      sha256: ...
compat:
  minRestoreToolVersion: 0.9.0
  maxTestedRestoreToolVersion: 1.3.2
notes: "generated by redwood-private-installer"
```

Ethan Park: That’s good. `platformVersion` is important for data migrations. But I’m not sure about `maxTestedRestoreToolVersion` (feels weird). Maybe better: `createdByToolVersion` only, and our docs say what is supported.

Connor O'Brien: Agree. “max tested” will rot fast and look like a promise.

Miles Thornton: Maybe:
- `createdBy.tool` + `createdBy.version`
- `required.restoreTool.minVersion` (rare)
- and keep the rest as docs/compat matrix.

Allison Grant: For telemetry: include `backupId` UUID so we can correlate backup + restore drill + metrics. But for air-gapped, it’s still useful for logs.

Benji Okafor: Another detail: archive layout. Do we want deterministic layout?
`manifest.yaml` at root + `artifacts/` directory with fixed names. No timestamps in filenames.

Connor O'Brien: Please yes. Deterministic layout helps signing + reproducible bundles. Also in air-gapped import/export we want predictable paths.

Marcus Lin: Deterministic is good, but don’t bake secrets into path names (customer names, tenant ids). Keep it generic.

Michael Nguyen: Re: migration strategy: If we bump MAJOR, do we ship `backup migrate` that rewrites old to new?

Miles Thornton: Ideally yes, but only if necessary. Another model is: restore tool understands multiple majors (e.g., supports 1.x and 2.x). But that increases code path complexity.

Neha Kapoor: SRE preference: fewer migration steps during an incident. In DR you’re already stressed; “run migrate, then restore” adds risk.

Ethan Park: +1. If we can, keep restore tool able to read at least 1 previous major. But we can reserve the right not to.

Connor O'Brien: Release reality: maintaining multiple majors indefinitely is expensive. But supporting at least “current major and previous major” seems doable.

Marcus Lin: Security preference: fewer legacy parsers forever. Each parser is an attack surface.

Hanae Suzuki: If we do multi-major, enforce strict parsing + size limits (zip bombs etc). Also signature verification BEFORE extraction.

Benji Okafor: +1 to “verify before extract.” Current PR-512 does checksum verification but we should ensure we’re not untarring untrusted content prior to verifying.

Michael Nguyen: So we’re converging on:
- Semver `backupFormatVersion`
- Restore supports: current MAJOR and optionally previous MAJOR (policy)
- New restore tool must restore old backups within same MAJOR (all MINOR/PATCH)
- MAJOR bumps are rare + ADR required

Miles Thornton: Yes. Also add: MINOR changes must be additive + optional. PATCH changes must not alter semantics.

Ethan Park: Another tricky bit: DB dumps. If we do logical dumps, those are not always forward/back compat across postgres versions. But that’s not “backup format,” that’s “component artifact.” We need to document what postgres versions are supported.

Neha Kapoor: Put it in the RPO/RTO + supported versions matrix. But on restore, tool should preflight check the target environment matches requirements (db engine/version).

Allison Grant: And emit a metric/event when preflight fails due to version mismatch so we can see it.

Connor O'Brien: Action items:
- Miles/Michael: update ADR-027 addendum or new ADR for semver policy + multi-major support.
- Connor: add release gate: backup format changes require bump + changelog snippet.
- Benji: ensure installer checks `backupFormatVersion` range and errors with pointer to docs.

Marcus Lin: Add to ADR: “unknown major => fail closed.” And explicitly state we won’t ever auto-downgrade or “best effort” restore.

Hanae Suzuki: Also include statement about key rotation and what happens if KMS unavailable during restore (fail, don’t fallback to plaintext).

Michael Nguyen: I’ll comment on ENG-9830 with this proposal and link it to PR-512 notes.

Miles Thornton: Cool. I’ll draft the ADR text tonight and tag folks for review.

source.text:
Priya Natarajan: quick RFC follow-up — thinking about making rollout metadata intentionally minimal on the wire and doing client-delta probing from the console. TL;DR: small pings for compatibility, server-side enrichment for ops. tradeoffs?
Ben Cho: +1 to minimalism, but worried about post-rollback debugging. we should keep at least: model, variant, pin_reason, expires_at/soft_ttl. otherwise ops blind.
Mateo Alvarez: +1. also how do we distinguish deprecation vs soft pin? suggest a compact enum like retire_hint: 'none'|'warn'|'soft_retire'|'hard_retire' so clients can surface warnings.
Priya Natarajan: one option. my proposal: wire payload stays tiny, something like:
```json
{"model":"redwood/llama-70b","variant":"q4o","pin_mode":"soft","expires_at":"2026-04-01T12:00:00Z"}
```
control plane enriches with deprecation hints, computed priority, origin, etc., only when a client opts in or for console UI.
Lina (eng-bot): deploy-bot: rollout-schema-v2 pushed to staging, schema validation tests: 45/50 passed, failing cases are optional fields parsing.
Ben Cho: practical concern — older SDKs will ignore expires_at. we need server-side grace/fallback window (eg: 72h default) + tenant override. otherwise pins could silently linger.
Mateo Alvarez: agreed. also when overlapping pins occur we need deterministic resolver rules (priority > created_ts > origin). should we encode 'priority' on-wire or keep it server-computed?
Priya Natarajan: prefer server-computed priority to keep wire small. but we can include a small optional debug view: origin and computed_priority when clients opt-in to richer payloads. that keeps default clients lean.
Ben Cho: sounds good. propose two small optional fields: "origin" (string) and "computed_priority" (int). optional, so normal clients ignore them. keeps debugging feasible.
Mateo Alvarez: telemetry-wise we should attach a tiny "delta_probe" sample: {last_seen_version, last_probe_ts, sdk_version}. very small, helps migration metrics.
Priya Natarajan: deprecation semantics need to be explicit in ops playbook: warn -> console alert only; soft_retire -> attempt autoreplace with compatible variant after N failures; hard_retire -> block new pins and trigger pager. include tenant/SLA exemptions for autoreplace.
Ben Cho: add constraint: autoreplace must respect tenant pin overrides and SLA tier — premium tenants opt-out of auto-migrations.
Mateo Alvarez: question for private installs: if control plane enriches metadata, should we sign that enrichment so on-host components can trust it?
Priya Natarajan: yes. for Private we should sign control-plane-issued enrichment payloads. hosted infra can rely on internal auth, but signature optional field for private installs.
Ben Cho: action items: (1) draft minimal wire schema doc + example payloads, (2) define server enrichment fields + signing rules, (3) implement grace window/fallback behavior, (4) add delta_probe telemetry proto, (5) update SDKs to parse optional debug fields.
Priya Natarajan: I'll draft the PR for the wire schema + example payloads and ping @Mateo Alvarez and @Ben Cho for review. thanks all.

source.text:
Talia Benson: Hey folks — need to schedule a design review for the delivery semantics ADR (audit export). This is the one covering at-least-once, idempotency, checkpointing, replay/backfill. Can we get 45m this week? Target attendees: @Ethan Park @Marcus Lin @Kevin Osei @Sean Gallagher @Rafael Mendes @Logan Wright @Jordan Lee. Link: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ARCH/adr-audit-export-delivery-semantics-and-idempotency (draft)

Ethan Park: +1. We should lock this before we finalize the control plane config API behavior. I can do Thu 11-12 PT or Fri 2-3 PT.

Marcus Lin: I’m in Thu 11 PT. Main question from Sec side: are we *explicitly* saying at-least-once and pushing dedup downstream? We need crisp language for customers + SOC2 evidence.

Kevin Osei: Same. Also need to be super clear on retention vs export. “Guaranteed delivery” gets interpreted as “exactly-once” and “no gaps”, which we can’t promise in outages w/ throttling.

Sean Gallagher: From SRE POV: exactly-once is basically a trap here unless we’re willing to build transactional sinks + state per destination at scale. Prefer at-least-once with bounded replay + tooling. Also need safety valves when downstream is hard-down (auto-pause, max lag).

Rafael Mendes: Agree. If we do exactly-once semantics at the exporter level, we’ll be fighting duplicates *and* missing events forever. At-least-once + stable `event_id` + clear guidance that SIEM should dedup is the sane path.

Logan Wright: Observability angle: at-least-once is easier to reason about in metrics. We can expose lag, retry counts, DLQ rates. Exactly-once claims will generate tickets when customers see duplicates in Splunk.

Jordan Lee: Question: how do we define idempotency key? `event_id` is per event (globally unique) — but exporters will batch. Do we ever need a `delivery_id` per destination attempt?

Benji Okafor: Draft ADR says: primary dedup key is `event_id` (uuid v7-ish) emitted at audit event creation time. Exporter retries can send duplicates; downstream should dedup on `event_id`. For S3, we’re writing files (partitioned) so dedup is more “consumer dedup” unless they ingest into something.

Marcus Lin: I’m ok with `event_id` but we must ensure it’s stable + non-PII and exists for *all* events. Also: how do we handle events emitted pre-v2 schema? Are we going to backfill IDs or just say beta is v2 only?

Ethan Park: Beta will require audit schema v2 (ENG-5127). We can do a compatibility mapping but exporter should only export events that have `event_id`. Otherwise we’ll create non-deduppable events.

Kevin Osei: That’s fine but we need to be explicit in beta docs: “only v2 events exported; older events not backfilled.” Also define backfill window clearly.

Sean Gallagher: Another angle: we should avoid “exactly once” anywhere in docs/UI. It’s not even true for Splunk HEC unless we implement an ack + checkpoint at event granularity and handle every edge case.

Jordan Lee: What if we do: exporter persists checkpoint as “max event_time seen” and “last exported event_id”? That still has ordering assumptions.

Benji Okafor: Current plan is checkpoint by monotonically increasing cursor from the audit store (think `event_seq` / `insert_id`), not `event_time`. Time is not safe for cursoring. ADR mentions “cursor is storage-assigned, strictly increasing per tenant/workspace”.

Rafael Mendes: Yes please. Using timestamps for cursoring will cause gaps during clock skew / delayed ingestion.

Logan Wright: For Splunk: HEC returns 200 even if events are partially accepted? (depends). We should clarify what we consider “delivered”. If we treat HTTP 200 as success for the batch, duplicates will be possible on retry but acceptable.

Marcus Lin: That’s the customer confusion point. We need the language: “at-least-once to destination endpoint; customer should deduplicate using `event_id`.” Plus: “export can be paused during prolonged downstream outage; audit log in Redwood remains source of truth.”

Kevin Osei: +1. Also need explicit mention of DLQ and operator-assisted replay: “failed batches may be queued for replay; replay can result in duplicates.”

Connor O'Brien: On rollout mechanics: we also need a decision on what happens if a config changes while exporter is mid-stream. Do we reset checkpoint? pause? This impacts “duplicates vs missing”.

Ethan Park: Draft behavior: config changes do NOT reset checkpoint by default. If filters/destination change, we continue from last cursor. If customer wants backfill, they set `backfill_window` (bounded). That might re-send older events = duplicates. UI must warn.

Sean Gallagher: Please add guardrails: max backfill window in beta (e.g., 7d) + rate limit replays. Otherwise someone will set 90d and nuke the exporter.

Talia Benson: Ok sounds like the meeting needs to cover:
1) Exactly-once vs at-least-once wording (customer-facing)
2) Cursor/checkpoint model (seq vs time)
3) Replay/backfill semantics + bounds
4) Destination-specific ack semantics (Splunk/Datadog/S3)
5) Safety valves (auto-pause / max lag)

Talia Benson: I’ll schedule Thu 11:00–11:45 PT. If you can’t make it, drop comments in ADR.

Google Calendar: Event created: “ADR Review: Audit Export Delivery Semantics + Idempotency” Thu Feb 6, 11:00–11:45am PT. Guests: Talia Benson, Ethan Park, Marcus Lin, Kevin Osei, Sean Gallagher, Rafael Mendes, Logan Wright, Jordan Lee, Benji Okafor, Connor O'Brien. Location: https://zoom.us/j/987654321

Marcus Lin: Thanks. I’ll add comments tonight on “what we can/can’t claim” for compliance.

Kevin Osei: I’ll also propose FAQ language: “at-least-once, may contain duplicates; use `event_id` for dedup.”

Sean Gallagher: I’ll review the failure modes section and ensure we’re not implicitly promising no duplicates.

Logan Wright: I’ll come with metrics/alerts we need to validate the semantics (lag, retry, DLQ depth).

Ethan Park: Great. Also Jordan/Benji can we make sure ADR calls out idempotency expectations per destination? Splunk/Datadog can dedup in pipeline; S3 is just object store.

Jordan Lee: yep, will add a note: S3 delivery is “at-least-once object writes” (possible duplicate objects) and consumer should handle. We should also use deterministic object keying where possible to reduce duplicates.

Benji Okafor: careful: deterministic keys can lead to overwrite = data loss if partial writes. Prefer unique keys + manifest? We can debate in the review.

Rafael Mendes: +1, overwrites are scary. Unique objects + checkpoint in our DB is safer; duplicates are OK.

Talia Benson: Perfect. See you Thu.
